**Tadeusz Olszewski** (born 1921 in Warsaw) graduated in architecture from the Warsaw Polytechnics (now Warsaw University of Technology) after returning to communist Poland. He made his poetic debut in 1944 with the collection *Echa Leśne* ("Forest Echoes"), co-authored with his brother Janusz. From 1956 to 1960, he worked as a journalist for *Ilustrowany Magazyn Turystyczny Światowid*. Between 1984 and 1986, he served as deputy editor-in-chief of *Tygodnik Kulturalny*, and from 1987 to 1991, he headed the poetry department of the monthly *Okolice*. In 1987, Olszewski publicly came out as gay and published an essay on homoerotic themes in modern Polish prose. He edited Poland’s first gay male monthly, *Filo*, under the pen name **Tadeusz Olsz** (1991–1992). His 1992 poetry collection *Niepokój* ("Unrest") marked a significant literary contribution, praised by figures like Leopold Staff. From 1996 to 2004, he was associated with the publishing house **Prószyński i S-ka**. His reviews and critical sketches appeared in *Twórczość* and *Odra*, and he served on the jury of the **Kazimiera Iłłakowiczówna Poetry Competition**. Olszewski lived in **Warsaw**, where he was also active in the **Warsaw Homosexual Movement (WRH)**.

**Promoting agribusiness, rural areas and history**  
Landwirtschaftliche Rentenbank, Germany's development agency for agribusiness and rural areas, provides special promotional loans focused on agriculture, renewable energy, and rural development. In addition to financing agribusiness, it supports regional authorities, banks, and EU-based institutions operating in rural areas. Its statutory promotional mandate includes funding for sustainable agriculture, AgTech and FoodTech startups, and projects like organic farming, emissions reduction, and animal welfare. Rentenbank extends loans through other banks on a competitively neutral basis, offering favorable terms. Beyond lending, it awards grants for agricultural innovations, applied research, and institutions vital to rural development. The Rehwinkel Foundation, affiliated with Rentenbank, supports scientific research, industry-academia collaboration, and scholarships for Master's students in agriculture-related fields. Civic engagement and public-sector investments target broadband expansion, rural tourism, and community wind farms, aligning with its mission to foster sustainable rural growth.

For small or backyard growers, collecting and destroying all leaf debris on the ground is an absolute necessity due to the potency of this disease because the fungus overwinters in this leftover leaf litter. This is its main form of survival. By removing and destroying these leaves, a grower can significantly decrease the amount of primary inoculum available in the spring. It will greatly decrease the apparent infection rate. There has also been a study done on the addition of a straw mulch bedding to the ground after all the leaves have been picked up. The addition of this mulch further reduced the spring infection rate. Leaf litter removal is not very practical for large commercial growers due labor needs and number of trees but if at all possible, a majority of the old leaves should try to be collected.

**16S rRNA Sequencing**  

The 16S ribosomal RNA (rRNA) gene is the most common housekeeping genetic marker for classifying and identifying bacterial species due to its universal presence in bacteria and archaea, combined with conserved and hypervariable regions. Approximately 1,500 nucleotides long, the 16S rRNA sequence contains highly conserved regions essential for ribosomal function and hypervariable regions unique to different taxa, enabling precise taxonomic discrimination. While 16S rRNA sequencing provides genus-level identification in over 90% of cases, species-level identification varies (50–90%) due to genetic similarity among closely related species. Databases like the Ribosomal Database Project (RDP) and the novel compact 16S rRNA database store curated rRNA sequences for bacteria, archaea, and eukarya, facilitating accurate microbiome analysis. This method surpasses culture-based techniques, particularly for slow-growing or uncultivable bacteria.

**COVID-19 (2019–present)**  
From the outset of the pandemic, experts speculated that COVID-19 might be "Disease X," a hypothetical pathogen predicted by the WHO. Shi Zhengli, director of the Center for Emerging Infectious Diseases at the Wuhan Institute of Virology, had researched bat coronaviruses and their potential for interspecies transmission. In early February 2020, Marion Koopmans, director of the WHO Collaborating Centre for Emerging Diseases at Erasmus MC and a member of the WHO's COVID-19 team, co-authored a *Cell* paper suggesting COVID-19 met the Disease X criteria. Peter Daszak, president of EcoHealth Alliance and a WHO team member, wrote in *The New York Times* that COVID-19 was Disease X, emphasizing its animal origins and pandemic potential. Both Koopmans and Daszak were involved in early discussions about the virus's origins and global impact.

In the iconic scene, Gwyneth Paltrow, as Margot Tenenbaum, steps off a green line bus to the sound of Nico's rendition. Browne later admitted on *Solo Acoustic* that he had licensed the song but forgotten about it until hearing his own guitar intro in the theater, initially not recognizing his playing. The grey fantail is territorial and it is a seasonal breeder. They raise several broods per season, usually each of three or four cream eggs, spotted grey and brown. The incubation period is around two weeks, with incubation and feeding duties shared by both parents. During the breeding season, the mass of testes of male grey fantails grows larger than in the non-breeding season. The growth of testes sizes could be subject to sperm competition, similar to other species exhibiting morphological traits. Male birds also have pronounced cloacal protuberances. This change of morphology is associated with polygynandry and the increase in copulation chance. Despite most grey fantails forming season-long monogamous pairs, a small number of male birds seeking extra-pair copulation have been recorded. To seek extra-pair copulation, the male bird invades neighbouring territories and waits at the nest where the female birds were not incubating. The intruding male might coerce a resident female into accepting extra-pair male copulation. Moreover, the aggression of the male bird could act as a deterrent to the female bird, leading to delayed ovulation, allowing the male to re-mate. This increases the likelihood of the male becoming the sole fertiliser of that female's eggs.

The route changed from previous years. The race started at the Shanghai Oriental Sports Center and took place almost entirely on wide, straight highways, with corners predominantly being expansive ninety-degree bends. The course initially followed the Middle Ring Road, the Huaxia Elevated Road and the G1501 Shanghai Ring Expressway, before entering the 10-kilometre-long (6.2 mi) tunnel under the Yangtze river to reach Changxing Island, shortly followed by a 8-kilometre-long (5.0 mi) bridge to Chongming Island, from where the route followed the course of previous years, along slightly smaller roads to the finish.

There are grave mounds and stone ships from the Bronze Age in Lau. From the Iron Age, there are seven grave fields, house foundations, collapsed stone walls and groove stones. Two of the stone ships and a round grave are situated at Bandeläins täppu on the edge of Lausbackar. They are dated from about 900 BC. 100 m (330 ft) south of the stone ships is a standing stone.

In April 1967, Steve Winwood left the Spencer Davis Group and formed Traffic with Jim Capaldi (drums), Dave Mason (guitar/songwriter), and Chris Wood (saxophone/flute), whom he met jamming at Birmingham’s Elbow Room. Seeking creative freedom, the band retreated to a cottage near Aston Tirrold, Berkshire, to write and rehearse, blending rock, folk, jazz, and R&B in isolation. The secluded setting fostered collaboration, attracting visits from musicians like Eric Clapton and Pete Townshend.

As military operations in Vietnam escalated, the demand for air refueling of attacking aircraft increased. Crews from the squadron became actively involved in Operation Young Tiger starting in 1965, refueling combat aircraft in Southeast Asia (after 1966, it was joined by its sister at Fairchild, the 43d Air Refueling Squadron). Shortly thereafter, the mission of refueling B-52s participating in Operation Arc Light was added. The 92d furnished tankers and crews to support combat in Southeast Asia until 1975.

In 2007, under General Superior Sister Dawn Tomaszewski, the Sisters of Providence founded **Providence Cristo Rey High School** in Indianapolis, Indiana, as a Catholic college-preparatory school for economically disadvantaged students. It opened that year and graduated its first class in 2011. The school belongs to the **Cristo Rey Network**, modeled after Chicago’s **Cristo Rey Jesuit High School**, the network’s original school. Students work at **300+ corporations** to offset tuition costs. School buses provide **curb-to-school service** with **56 pickup spots**, accommodating medical or disability needs. The school maintains a **photograph collection** showcasing student experiences.

Smith College has 285 professors in 41 academic departments and programs, for a faculty-student ratio of 1:9. It was the first women's college in the United States to grant its own undergraduate degrees in engineering. The Picker Engineering Program offers a single ABET accredited Bachelor of Science in engineering science, combining the fundamentals of multiple engineering disciplines.

On the Melle Hills is the castle of Diedrichsburg. In the north of the hill range lies the 2 km2 large and enclosed Diedrichsburg Wildlife Park, which was founded in 1963. It has a large population of semi-wild wild boars. It is particularly popular with tourists in spring when there are numerous newcomers to the park. On the Friedenshöhe, a subpeak of the Stuckenberg near Buer is the 28.6-metre-high Friedenshöhe observation tower, which was built in 1988 and which has been expanded into the Klimaturm. On the Ottoshöhe, a hill on the Eickener Egge near the centre of Melle, there is a 28.5 metre- high observation tower.

During the 2012 offseason, the Vancouver Canucks signed defenseman Jason Garrison to a six-year, $27.6 million contract, bringing the White Rock, B.C., native home after his tenure with the Florida Panthers. The Canucks lost longtime defenseman Sami Salo, who signed a two-year, $7.5 million deal with the Tampa Bay Lightning after a decade in Vancouver. Garrison was expected to replace Salo’s role on the blueline and quickly formed a reliable partnership with Dan Hamhuis, logging heavy minutes. When injuries sidelined right-shot defenseman Kevin Bieksa, Garrison—a left-handed shooter—excelled despite playing out of position, solidifying his importance to the Canucks' defense.

Jeffrey Epstein was arrested on **July 6, 2019**, in New York on federal sex trafficking charges involving minors as young as 14 from 2002 to 2005. Held at the **Metropolitan Correctional Center** in Manhattan, he pleaded **not guilty**. In **Florida (2008)**, he avoided federal prosecution via a plea deal, pleading guilty to **one state felony charge** of soliciting prostitution, serving **13 months** with work release, paying restitution, and registering as a **sex offender**. The deal shielded him from a potential **life sentence**.

Tariq Fancy, former BlackRock CIO of sustainable investing, wrote an op-ed stating that "the financial services industry is duping the American public with pro-environment, sustainable investing practices." Stating there is no evidence ESG investing has any social impact via a CNBC Exclusive In the USA Today article 'Financial world greenwashing the public with deadly distraction in sustainable investing practices' he claims "Wall Street is greenwashing the financial world, making sustainable investing merely PR, which is a distraction from the problem of climate change." In November 2013, then Scotland assistant manager Mark McGhee confirmed interest in Caulker, as Caulker's grandmother, Jessie, comes from Dollar, Clackmannanshire. The **active site** of **METAP2** features a characteristic **structural motif** shared by many metalloenzymes, including two **cobalt** or **manganese** ions coordinated by **bridging carboxylate ligands** (Glu204 and Asp251) and a **bridging water/hydroxide molecule**. In **human MetAP2** (PDB: **1B6A**), one metal ion is bound by **His178**, **His232**, and **Asp251**, while the other is coordinated by **Glu204** (bidentate), **Asp251**, and **Glu288**. The **bridging carboxylates** (Glu204 and Asp251) stabilize the catalytic transition state, enabling cleavage of the **N-terminal methionine** from nascent polypeptides. This architecture optimizes METAP2's role in **post-translational protein modification**.

LA-944 is an Air Force Junior Reserve Officer Training Corps located at Fontainebleau High School, Mandeville, Louisiana. It was created in July 1994 with an agreement between Fontainebleau High School, the St. Tammany Parish School Board, and the United States Air Force. LA-944 is a cadet-run corps where the members of the Senior Staff make all of the major decisions with little help from the Instructors. LA-944 received the Distinguished Unit Award (DUA) during the 1999–2000 year and the DUA with Merit in the years 2007–2008 and 2010–2011.

Both Sawamura's own family and that of his wife were active in the film industry. Sawamura's younger brother and sister were the actors Daisuke Katō and Sadako Sawamura. He married Tomoko Makino, the daughter of Shozo Makino, a film director and the head of Makino Productions. Area15 is an entertainment and retail complex located near the Las Vegas Strip, adjacent to Interstate 15 at the Desert Inn Road overpass. The site was previously occupied by a Fletcher Jones Mercedes dealership and nearby acreage once held the Scandia Family Fun Center. In 2005, New York-based real estate firm Fisher Brothers purchased the land for $50 million, originally planning a mixed-use development with Station Casinos. However, the 2008 economic downturn derailed these plans. By the mid-2010s, Fisher Brothers, partnering with creative agency Beneville Studios, began planning Area15 on the former dealership site. Winston Fisher and Michael Beneville, recognizing the need for "radical differentiation" to compete with the Strip, envisioned an immersive, technology-driven experience. The result was Area15, a futuristic complex blending interactive art, themed events, and nightlife, anchored by Meow Wolf’s immersive installations. The venue opened in 2020 after delays, becoming a hub for experiential entertainment.

During his years at Los Alamos, Ulam was a visiting professor at Harvard from 1951 to 1952, MIT from 1956 to 1957, the University of California, San Diego, in 1963, and the University of Colorado at Boulder from 1961 to 1962 and 1965 to 1967. In 1967, the last of these positions became permanent, when Ulam was appointed Professor and Chairman of the Department of Mathematics at the University of Colorado. He kept a residence in Santa Fe, which made it convenient to spend summers at Los Alamos as a consultant. He was an elected member of the American Academy of Arts and Sciences, the United States National Academy of Sciences, and the American Philosophical Society.

The Santa Fe Station hotel-casino faced significant labor disputes with the Culinary Workers Union after employees voted to accept union representation in October 1993. The company opposed the decision, but its final appeal was rejected in 1996. Negotiations began in January 1998, with the union accusing Santa Fe of bad-faith bargaining and intentional delays, while the company cited the complexity of unresolved issues. Tensions escalated, and in September 1998, approximately 59 union members were arrested during a sit-down strike. The union claimed Santa Fe was stalling contract talks, while the company argued the sheer volume of issues made swift negotiations impossible. These conflicts highlighted broader struggles over workers' rights and fair labor practices at the property.

Whanganui DHB served a population of 64,599 at the 2018 New Zealand census, an increase of 4,479 people (7.5%) since the 2013 census, and an increase of 2,388 people (3.8%) since the 2006 census. There were 25,281 households. There were 31,731 males and 32,871 females, giving a sex ratio of 0.97 males per female. The median age was 42.1 years (compared with 37.4 years nationally), with 13,068 people (20.2%) aged under 15 years, 11,142 (17.2%) aged 15 to 29, 27,807 (43.0%) aged 30 to 64, and 12,579 (19.5%) aged 65 or older.

Producer Alan Douglas had helped Duke Ellington with errands when they were both working in Paris in the early 1960s. HMS *Watchman* (D26) was ordered in 1916 as part of the "W" class destroyer program. Built by John Brown & Company on the Clyde, she was laid down on 17 January 1917, launched on 2 November 1917, and completed on 26 January 1918, commissioning the same day. Initially assigned pennant number D26, she joined the Eleventh Destroyer Flotilla in February 1918.

Rose Kekedo began her career in 1967 as the first woman to represent Papua New Guinea at the United Nations in New York City. The first church in Jerusalem shared all their money and possessions (Acts of the Apostles 2 and 4). Inspired by the Early Christians, many Christians have since tried to follow their example of community of goods and common ownership. Common ownership is practiced by some Christian groups such as the Hutterites (for about 500 years), the Bruderhof (for some 100 years) and others. In those cases, property is generally owned by a charity set up for the purpose of maintaining the members of the religious groups.

Caladeni × triangularis was first formally described 1827 by Richard Sanders Rogers from a specimen collected between Wagin and Narrogin. The description was published in Transactions and Proceedings of the Royal Society of South Australia. The epithet (triangularis) refers to the shape of the labellum of this orchid.

The *Jujutsu Kaisen* anime television series, directed by **Sunghoo Park**, initially planned to adapt the *Jujutsu Kaisen 0* prequel manga (originally titled *Tokyo Metropolitan Curse Technical School*) in its first episodes but instead introduced **Yuji Itadori** to establish the main story. The original LP was released by United Artists Jazz in 1963 in mono and stereo versions. United Artists was bought by EMI in 1979, and subsidiary Blue Note Records reissued the album on CD in 1987. This contained more recordings from the same session: four previously unreleased works written for the session, plus two alternative takes. The order presented in this edition was that in which the tracks were recorded. The sound quality of the original recording was improved for the 2002 Blue Note CD release by engineer Ron McMaster, using the original tapes and 24-bit remastering, adding clarity to the drums in particular. For this release, the first seven tracks were arranged in their original order, with the other four pieces and four alternative takes placed afterward, increasing the number of tracks to 15.

Jairam Valjee Chouhan was a prominent Kutchi railway contractor, known for his work with the Bengal Nagpur Railway, where he completed several bridges and doubling of railway lines. From 1921 to 1929, he camped near Paraghat to construct a railway bridge over the Arpa River and double the rail lines of the Bilaspur-Raigarh section. His main office was in Raigarh, where a colony, Jairam Valjee Colony, bears his name. He often collaborated with fellow Mistri contractors like Rai Saheb Mulji Jagmal Sawaria of Bilaspur and Rai Saheb Harilal Shamji of Raigarh.

Dodji is situated in a semi-arid savanna zone, with few permanent settlements until the decades before the Second World War. The town was founded by young Wolof members of the Mouride brotherhood. Many previously uninhabited areas in eastern Senegal were settled and communities established as part of the movement's drive to find land and livelihood for Senegal's population. Dodji's isolation is evinced by the deportation of strike leaders from Dakar to the town after a general strike called by the Dakar branch of the Union Nationale des Travailleurs du Sénégal (UNTS) in May 1963. The area around Dodji, historically very sparsely populated, has also been used by the French military as a live fire training ground.

On August 19, 2022, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau nominated O'Bonsawin to the Supreme Court of Canada to replace retiring Justice Michael Moldaver. On August 24, O'Bonsawin appeared for a special meeting with the Standing Committee on Justice and Human Rights to answer questions from parliamentarians from both the House of Commons and Senate. On August 26, 2022, the Office of the Prime Minister announced that her appointment was formally confirmed and she would join the Supreme Court on September 1, 2022, the same day that Moldaver was retiring. O'Bonsawin is the first Indigenous person to sit on Canada's highest court.

Ofentse Mwase was born in Rustenburg, North West, South Africa. His filmmaking interest began in 2005. In 2009, he shot a Short film iGolide, and receive nomination for Best Cinematographer of 2010.

Carnovsky was born in St. Louis, Missouri on September 5, 1897 to Ike and Jennie Carnovsky, both Russian Jewish immigrants. His father, a grocer, took him to performances of the Yiddish theater. She and her boyfriend, Rodney McCoy, had been sitting in her car outside of the alley after attending a school dance at Western Hills High School when they were attacked by an unknown assailant who pistol whipped Roy, leaving him unconscious. Walker's body was later recovered three days later from a drainage ditch, half an hour south of Fort Worth. Tunnell was born in Bryn Mawr, Pennsylvania, though sources conflict as to his year of birth. His tombstone as well as the Social Security Death Index and Tunnell's Department of Veterans Affairs BIRLS Death File reflect a birth date of March 29, 1924. Official NFL records and multiple sources in the early 1960s reported his date of birth as March 29, 1922. Tunnell listed his birth date as March 29, 1923, on his 1942 draft card and on his 1950 application for World War II benefits. Other sources record his birth year as 1925. **Positive displacement compressors** compress air by trapping a fixed volume in a chamber and reducing its volume using pistons, screws, or vanes. When maximum pressure is reached, a valve opens, discharging the air into an outlet system. Common types include piston, rotary-screw, and scroll compressors.

On **26 May**, British Foreign Secretary **William Hague** called for an urgent **UN Security Council** session following the **Houla massacre**, urging the Syrian regime to grant "full and immediate" access to **UN monitors** and cease military operations, as demanded by **Special Envoy Kofi Annan**. Syria's speaker denounced UN resolutions as biased, while Russia, a key ally of **Damascus**, had previously blocked anti-Assad measures but condemned the massacre. Regime opponents reportedly lost faith in the Security Council due to its inaction.

One of the earliest claims of pre-Columbian transoceanic contact involves the Valdivia culture of coastal Ecuador (3000–1500 BCE), whose pottery was noted by archaeologist Emilio Estrada and colleagues to resemble Middle Jōmon period (2000–3000 BCE) pottery from Japan. They suggested transpacific contact might explain the similarities, particularly in rim designs and incised decorations. However, chronological inconsistencies and lack of broader evidence led most archaeologists to dismiss this theory, though the resemblances remain a topic of debate.

In September 2016, computer forensics firm Elcomsoft discovered that iOS 10 weakened the encryption of local iTunes backups compared to iOS 9, making them vulnerable to brute-force attacks. Elcomsoft found that iOS 10 used a less secure password verification mechanism, skipping security checks and allowing passwords to be tried approximately 2,500 times faster than in iOS 9—up to 6 million attempts per second. Apple acknowledged the issue (CVE-2016-4685), stating that an attacker with access to an encrypted backup could potentially determine the password. The flaw was fixed in a subsequent update by removing the weak hash algorithm.

**The Antebellum** is an 8,212 sqft estate and gardens in Grogan's Point, inspired by The Houmas plantation. Developed by the Westbrook Building Company in 1988 as one of six historically inspired estates, it later inspired three additional plantation estates in East Shore of The Woodlands.

While residing in Birmingham, Eddowes began a relationship with former soldier Thomas Conway, who had served in the 18th Royal Irish Regiment and who received a small regimental pension. The couple had two children: Catherine Ann (b. 1863); and Thomas Lawrence (b. 1867), and initially supported themselves by selling chapbooks—frequently featuring gallows ballads—penned by Conway and occasionally via labouring work when his health permitted. No evidence exists to support the two having actually married following their initial acquaintance in 1861 or 1862, although shortly after the two began a relationship, Eddowes began referring to herself as "Kate Conway". She later had Conway's initials ("TC") crudely tattooed in blue ink on her left forearm.

During the Mamluk period, Damascus maintained its own architectural traditions, heavily influenced by the earlier Ayyubid style. As the "second city" of the sultanate, it was the most patronized urban center outside Cairo, though many Mamluk-era buildings have since been damaged or partially preserved. The city still holds the second-highest concentration of Mamluk monuments after Cairo. Early Mamluk structures in Damascus continued the local tradition of flamboyant stonework and muqarnas portals, features that later spread to Cairo in the 14th century.

In September 2016, it was discovered that the encryption of local iOS backups made with iTunes is weaker for iOS 10 devices than for devices running iOS 9. Russian software firm ElcomSoft discovered that the iOS 10 backup process skips several security checks, making it "approximately 2,500 times" faster to try passwords, enabling 6 million password tries per second compared to the 2,400 password tries per second for the same process ElcomSoft has used on iOS 9. The firm stated that the impact is "severe". Apple acknowledged the problem, said it planned to issue a security update, but also stated that iCloud backups were not affected. The iOS 10.1 update subsequently fixed the issue.

Production facilities were moved to Spirit Lake, Iowa, with operations beginning on August 5, 2011. In March 2013, Indian unveiled its new 111 cu in (1,820 cc) "Thunder Stroke" engine, and by August 2013, began selling redesigned motorcycles based on this powerplant.

In October 2014, the British Government agreed to send 12 members of the 2nd Battalion, The Yorkshire Regiment (2 YORKS) to Irbil to train Peshmerga forces on using UK-supplied heavy machine guns. The initial deployment of 12 troops eventually rose to 50 before being bolstered by an additional batch numbering in the "low hundreds." By 2016, an additional 30 troops were deployed to train Iraqi forces, bringing the total number of British troops in Iraq to 300. David Lodge married Mary Jacob in 1959 when both were 24 and studying English. He later reflected with amazement on their adherence to Catholic birth control methods. Their first child, Stephen, was born in 1960, followed by a daughter, Julia, in 1962. Their third child, Christopher, born in 1964, had Down's syndrome. The couple raised their three children in Birmingham, where Lodge balanced family life with his early literary career.

Bipolar disorder (BD) is a mood disorder usually characterized by extreme mood swings and oscillating periods of intense emotion, such as mania and depression. The cycling between manic and depressive episodes is the main hallmark of bipolar disorder. In BD, there seems to be a disconnect between the Frontoparietal Network (FPN) and Defualt Mode Network (DMN), in which the FPN is unable "to suppress task irrelevant DMN activity during cognitive performance" which leads to BD's cognitive impairments. It also appears that in BD, there is a disruption in the recruitment of the salience network which contributes to cognitive dysregulation. Bipolar disorder is also thought to be characterized by abnormal functional connectivity between the FPN and motivational networks, with the DMN playing a mediation role. All these aberrant connectivity and dysregulation leads to the BD pathology witnessed in patients with this mood disorder.

The *Lumber Workers Industrial Union of Canada* began publishing *Metsätyöläinen* ("Lumberworker") in 1930, edited by Sanna Kannasto. Printed by the Vapaus press in Sudbury, Ontario, it was published there until at least 1932.

The history of Kintetsu Railway traces back to the founding of Nara Tramway Co., Ltd. on September 16, 1910, which was renamed Osaka Electric Tramway Co., Ltd. (Daiki) a month later. On April 30, 1914, Daiki completed the Ikoma Tunnel and launched the Osaka-Nara line (now the Nara Line). In the 1920s, it expanded with the Kashihara, Osaka, and Shigi lines, followed by the Kyoto Line, a joint venture with Keihan Electric Railway. In 1927, Daiki established Sangu Electric Railway Co., Ltd. (Sankyu), which consolidated Ise Electric Railway Co., Ltd. (Iseden) on September 15, 1936. These developments laid the foundation for Kintetsu's extensive network.

The Court of Minstrels originally held jurisdiction over all counties in England. By the fifth year of Charles I's reign, its authority was limited to Cheshire, Lancashire, and parts of Wales. Under Charles I, minstrels who failed to attend faced a fine of 40 shillings, which increased to 100 shillings by 1630. Legend holds that King Arthur himself once attended the court, echoing Geoffrey of Monmouth's tales of his legendary gatherings at Caerleon.

Although often used by bodybuilders during their "cutting" cycles, the drug has been more recently known to the mainstream, particularly through publicized stories of use by celebrities such as Victoria Beckham, Britney Spears, and Lindsay Lohan,  for its off-label use as a weight-loss drug similar to usage of other sympathomimetic amines such as ephedrine, despite the lack of sufficient clinical testing either supporting or negating such use. In 2021, a Mexican fitness influencer Odalis Santos Mena died after suffering a cardiac arrest while being anesthetized for a procedure of miraDry, a treatment that uses thermal energy to eliminate underarm sweat glands. The coroner reported that Mena’s death was attributed to a combination of Clenbuterol and anesthesia.

Cooper Hummel attended Lake Oswego High School in Lake Oswego, Oregon. He played for Lake Oswego Little League, which reached the 2007 Little League World Series, finishing third in the U.S. He later played three seasons at the University of Portland.

On Luther's recommendation, Speratus became court chaplain of Albert, Duke of Prussia, a post he held from 1524 to 1529. In 1530, he became the Protestant bishop of Pomesania, a post he held until his death in 1551. He was active there in defending the tenets of faith advanced by Luther, and in attacking such rival sects as the followers of Caspar Schwenckfeldt and Andreas Osiander. He died in Marienwerder on 12 August 1551.

At the 1999 New South Wales election, Oldfield was elected to the New South Wales Legislative Council, where One Nation achieved 6.34% of the vote and narrowly missing out winning a second seat (contested by Brian Burston). This was the only seat in the New South Wales Parliament that the party secured. It was their first seat ever held in New South Wales.

**Other recognized leagues**  

Several early defunct baseball leagues are officially considered major leagues by Major League Baseball (MLB). In 1969, the Special Committee on Baseball Records recognized six major leagues dating back to 1876: the National League (founded in 1876), the American League (1901), the American Association (1882–1891), the Union Association (1884), the Players' League (1890), and the Federal League (1914–1915). The National Association (1871–1875), though baseball's first professional league, was excluded due to its "erratic schedule and procedures," though some researchers argue for its inclusion. Baseball-Reference.com includes its statistics alongside other major leagues. The Union Association's major-league status is disputed by researchers like Bill James, who cite its short duration, poor competitive balance, and low level of play. On December 16, 2020, MLB announced it was elevating seven Negro Leagues (operating between 1920 and 1948) to major-league status, rectifying their 1969 omission.

Ta'Niya Latson began her high school career at Westlake High School in Atlanta, Georgia, where as a freshman she played alongside standout teammate Raven Johnson. The name **Gedi** is believed to be a corruption of **Ghritaghadya**, meaning "abode of ghee (clarified butter)." According to legend, a merchant from Anhilwad used magic to steal ghee from the warehouse of **Malav**, a local ruler. When the theft was discovered, Malav refused the merchant's monetary compensation, instead using the funds to build a temple. This story, along with the inscription, highlights the region's historical and economic significance. Like **Tel Megiddo (Armageddon)**, Gedi's name reflects its layered history, though its conflicts remain less documented than those of the Jezreel Valley.

He sought to change Venezuela from a "backward and savage country", to a more prosperous, admiring the United States and especially France. Guzmán reportedly stole money from the treasury, abused his power, and, after a disagreement with a bishop, expelled any clergy who disagreed with him and seized property belonging to the Catholic Church. In *Rise of the Robots*, Martin Ford argues that while 20th-century technological advances primarily displaced lower-skilled laborers, the 21st century increasingly threatens skilled workers' jobs. Professions in manufacturing, textiles, and footwear have seen work outsourced to the developing world, exacerbating job losses in advanced economies. Ford contends that emerging technologies, unlike past innovations, will not generate sufficient new employment, as new industries—such as YouTube and Instagram—are characterized by tiny workforces and outsized revenues. He predicts these sectors will "rarely, if ever, be highly labor-intensive." While expanding education (e.g., full-day kindergarten) may help, Ford asserts it is insufficient. Instead, he advocates for a dramatic policy response: a guaranteed annual minimum income, proposing $10,000 per year as a starting point to mitigate the erosion of meaningful work.

Competition occurs when members of the same species, or several different species, compete for shared resources in a given habitat. According to the competitive exclusion principle, when environmental resources are limited, species cannot occupy nor be supported by identical niches. Eventually, one species will out-compete the other, which will push the disadvantaged species to extinction.

Some programmes are in place to preserve or maintain species which are challenged in their natural environment by raising them in a protected environment. One such example in the United Kingdom is the freshwater pearl mussel (Margaritifera margaritifera) which can live for over 100 years, but have seen a loss of their habitat through changes in water quality and being fished for their pearls. The species was given protected status in the Wildlife and Countryside Act 1981, and hunting, selling or buying the pearls has been illegal since 1998, hasn't stopped illegal activity in removing them from rivers. Another problem in the breeding cycle of the freshwater pearl mussel is that when the young are released by the female, they attach to the gills of passing salmon and trout (without causing any harm to the host fish) and as these fish are now rarer in some rivers, this too has had an effect on the survival of the species.

There is a vaccine for yellow fever which was developed in the 1930s, the yellow 17D vaccine, and it is still in use today. The initial yellow fever vaccination provides lifelong protection for most people and provides immunity within 30 days of the vaccine. Reactions to the yellow fever vaccine have included mild headache and fever, and muscle aches. There are rare cases of individuals presenting with symptoms that mirror the disease itself. The risk of complications from the vaccine are greater for individuals over 60 years of age. In addition, the vaccine is not usually administered to babies under nine months of age, pregnant women, people with allergies to egg protein, and individuals living with AIDS/HIV. The World Health Organization (WHO) reports that 105 million people have been vaccinated for yellow fever in West Africa from 2000 to 2015.

The African common toad is a large sturdy toad with a warty skin. Males grow to a snout-to-vent length of 62 to 91 mm (2.4 to 3.6 in) and females reach 70 to 130 mm (2.8 to 5.1 in). The paratoid glands are large and either parallel or kidney-shaped and the male has a single vocal sac under the chin. The dorsal surface is dark olive-brown with dark patches on the back, often arranged fairly symmetrically, and in younger animals, there is a paler band along the spine. There are smaller dark blotches on the upper lip and the eyelids, and the warts on the flanks are often separated by dark markings. The throats of males are black and the underparts of both sexes are white to beige. The call is a rattling sound made up of two pulses and lasting for about 0.9 second. When threatened, they would bounce to confuse (or escape) predators and would also move through water. They also have webbed hind feet to propel through water.

In 2005, Tawakkol Karman co-founded the human rights group **Women Journalists Without Chains (WJWC)** with seven other female journalists, originally naming it *Female Reporters Without Borders* before changing it to secure a government license. The organization promoted freedom of expression, democratic rights, and media advocacy, particularly for women and marginalized groups. Karman faced threats, harassment, and intimidation from Yemeni authorities, including phone calls and letters, after refusing to accept the Ministry of Information’s rejection of WJWC’s applications to establish a newspaper and radio station. The group also advocated for SMS news services, which the government tightly controlled despite their exclusion from the Press Law of 1990. A popular WJWC SMS news service was shut down by ministerial order. In 2007, WJWC released a report documenting press freedom violations since 2005, and in 2009, Karman criticized the Ministry of Information for persecuting journalists through trials. Between 2007 and 2010, she regularly led protests in Sana’a’s Tahrir Square, demanding democratic reforms and an end to government repression.

Accelerated soil burial tests are used to record the degradation process of the plastic in the ground by replicating the conditions of a landfill, a typical disposal site for plastics. These tests are used after the service life of the material has been depleted, and the next step for the material is disposal. Typically, samples are buried in biologically active soil for six months and are exposed to air to ensure that there is sufficient oxygen so that the aerobic mechanism of degradation can occur. The experimental conditions must reflect natural conditions closely, so the moisture and temperature of the soil are carefully controlled. The type of soil used must also be recorded, as it can affect the degradation process.

In 1609, the trial of the murderer stalled due to the absence of a prosecutor, prompting a protest to preserve the right of Spynie and his siblings to pursue justice later. After coming of age, Spynie agreed to waive prosecution when Edzell swore "by his great oath" that the killing was accidental and promised to pay 8,000 merks and transfer the lands of Garlobank, Perthshire, to Spynie and his sister. Edzell obtained a remission for the murder under the great seal on 7 March 1617.

**Aftercare**  
The scientific evidence guiding post-myringotomy care is limited, with no conclusive studies proving adverse effects from water exposure after tube insertion. However, some epidemiologic studies before 1992 suggested a statistical benefit to using ear protection (e.g., swim ear plugs) in reducing infections like swimmer’s ear, particularly in children with tubes. While strong evidence is lacking, ear protection is often recommended as a precaution, especially in dirty water (e.g., untreated pools, natural bodies with high bacteria). For chlorinated pools, ear plugs are optional but advised for frequent swimmers or those with tubes. The primary goal is to prevent infections caused by contaminated water. Bathing or shampooing typically does not require protection.

St Teilo's Church, Llandeilo Tal-y-bont, was built in the 12th or 13th century on marshy land near Pontarddulais, Glamorgan. It fell out of use by 1930, and demolition began in 1984 for its reconstruction at St Fagans National History Museum.

The saying "nice guys finish last" is a condensation by journalists of a quotation by Durocher—he did not originally say this himself, though it has often been attributed to him, and he did appropriate it as his own. The original quotation was "The nice guys are all over there, in seventh place" (July 6, 1946) about the 1946 New York Giants—seventh place was next to last place in the National League. This was shortly afterwards rendered as "'Nice Guys' Wind Up in Last Place, Scoffs Lippy", thence its present form.

The development of the M777 began in 1987 as the Ultralight Field Howitzer (UFH), initially designed by Vickers Shipbuilding and Engineering's Armaments Division in Barrow-in-Furness, England. Approximately 70% of the M777's parts are now US-built, including the M776 gun barrel, manufactured at the Watervliet Arsenal in New York. Final assembly occurs in Hattiesburg, Mississippi, blending British design with U.S. manufacturing.

The increasing use of ICTs in the workplace has presented organizations with new ethical challenges, as ICT adoption boosts productivity and innovation but also blurs the line between personal and professional lives. While ICT facilitates flexibility and efficiency, its pervasive use can lead to cyber loafing—non-work-related activities like personal internet use during work hours. Christine Maggiore died on December 27, 2008, at the age of 52 in her California home. She had been treated for pneumonia in the six months prior to her death, as reported by the Los Angeles County coroner's office. A doctor familiar with her family noted that antiretroviral therapy could have prevented her death, while fellow AIDS denialists, like Celia Farber, speculated that a radical "detox cleansing" regimen may have weakened her immune system, leading to pneumonia. Her death certificate lists disseminated herpes viral infection and bilateral bronchial pneumonia as the causes of death, with oral candidiasis as a contributing factor. It also states that no autopsy was performed. Despite her claims of vibrant health without HIV medication, her death was linked to AIDS-related conditions.

Another mass execution was discovered near the eastern city of Deir ez-Zor. The unidentified corpses of 13 men had been discovered shot to death execution-style. This incident raised awareness that the violence in Syria was heading towards an inexorable vicious cycle of tit-for-tat attacks between the different parties involved. According to the opposition, the 13 people who were shot at point blank range and later found in a field were employees at the electricity company in Deir Ezzor, who went on strike in protest of the massacres committed by the Syrian regime. They all had their hands tied behind their backs and were shot in the head according to a UN report. The head of a UN observer mission in Syria was “deeply disturbed” by the killings in Deir Ezzor, calling it an “appalling and inexcusable act.” Several days later, the dead were identified to be military and the opposition than claimed they were army defectors killed by government forces. However, on 5 June, the jihadist group the Al-Nusra Front to Protect the Levant claimed responsibility for the killings, stating that they had captured and interrogated the soldiers in Deir al-Zor and "justly" punished them with death, after they confessed to crimes.

**Immunohistochemical assessment**  
The estrogen receptor test (ERT) immunohistochemical (IHC) assessment is used to detect estrogen receptor (ER) expression in breast carcinoma, guiding treatment decisions. Hormone receptor-positive (HR+) breast cancers, including ER-positive (ER+) and progesterone receptor-positive (PR+) tumors, are more likely to respond to endocrine therapies like tamoxifen or aromatase inhibitors. Monitoring ER activity is essential because it predicts therapeutic efficacy and prognosis. In 2017, six breast carcinoma cases were analyzed via ERT IHC, classifying ER activity based on staining intensity and distribution. Samples were fixed in paraformaldehyde, glutaraldehyde, and sucrose, then embedded in paraffin before staining. The IHC staining used a DS-5Mc color camera system with Nikon software, employing specific antibodies to detect ER proteins. This process helps identify patients who may benefit from hormone-targeted treatments.

**Conservation**  
Several programmes in the United Kingdom aim to preserve vulnerable aquatic species, such as the endangered freshwater pearl mussel (*Margaritifera margaritifera*), which can live over 100 years. Habitat degradation, water pollution, and illegal pearl hunting have severely reduced its populations. Protected since 1998 under the Wildlife and Countryside Act, hunting, selling, or buying Scottish pearls has been illegal since the same year, yet poaching persists. A key challenge in its breeding cycle is its reliance on young salmon and trout as host fish for larvae, with declining fish stocks further threatening recovery. Conservation efforts focus on safeguarding habitats and combating wildlife crime to ensure the survival of this ecologically vital species.

The magazine *Studime Filologjike* was originally named *Buletin i Institutit të Shkencavet* (Bulletin of the Institute of Sciences) and was published by the Albanian Institute of History, established in 1947. Post-World War II, the institute played a key role in developing Albania's academic infrastructure. The original magazine featured sections on history, linguistics, and literature. After its first issue, it was renamed *Buletin i Universitetit Shtetëror të Tiranës* (Bulletin of the State University of Tirana) and was published by the State University from 1948 to 1957. In 1957, the magazine split into two publications: *Buletin për Shkencat Shoqërore* (Bulletin for Social Studies), focusing on history, economics, and law, and *Buletin për Shkencat Natyrore* (Bulletin for Natural Sciences). Article resumes were provided in French.

Fred Turner discusses the production and eventual demise of both the Catalog and Review in From Counterculture to Cyberculture: Stewart Brand, the Whole Earth Network, and the Rise of Digital Utopianism. Turner notes that in 1983, The Whole Earth Software Catalog was proposed by John Brockman as a magazine which "would do for computing what the original [Whole Earth Catalog] had done for the counterculture: identify and recommend the best tools as they emerged." Brand announced the first publication of the quarterly Whole Earth Software Review at the SoftCon trade show at the Louisiana Superdome in New Orleans in March 1984. While both were published as an extension of Whole Earth, the Catalog was a large glossy book sponsored by Doubleday and published in Sausalito California while the Review was a small periodical published in San Diego. The Catalog and Review were notable for being "devoid of any industry advertising" and for being "accessible and user friendly - written in an glib, conversational style that takes most of the bugs out of microprocessing."

Beyond main arms and armour, a infantryman's "military kit" generally includes combat boots, battledress or combat uniform, camping gear, heavy weather gear, survival gear, secondary weapons and ammunition, weapon service and repair kits, health and hygiene items, mess kit, rations, filled water canteen, and all other consumables each infantryman needs for the expected duration of time operating away from their unit's base, plus any special mission-specific equipment. One of the most valuable pieces of gear is the entrenching tool—basically a folding spade—which can be employed not only to dig important defences, but also in a variety of other daily tasks, and even sometimes as a weapon. Infantry typically have shared equipment on top of this, like tents or heavy weapons, where the carrying burden is spread across several infantrymen. In all, this can reach 25–45 kg (60–100 lb) for each soldier on the march. Such heavy infantry burdens have changed little over centuries of warfare; in the late Roman Republic, legionaries were nicknamed Marius' mules as their main activity seemed to be carrying the weight of their legion around on their backs.

On 2 December 2017, the local iwi Te Kawerau ā Maki placed an unofficial rāhui (traditional prohibition) over the kauri forest in the Waitākere Ranges to slow the spread of kauri dieback, a disease threatening the trees. The rāhui aimed to protect the forest ecosystem by restricting public access, though initial compliance was poor. Despite this, the Waitākere Ranges remained the only large forest near West Auckland still open to visitors. On 20 February 2018, Auckland Council announced the closure of all forested areas in the Waitākere Ranges, citing the rāhui's ineffectiveness and a doubling of infected trees to nearly 20%. As a preventive measure, high-risk tracks in the disease-free Hunua Ranges were also closed.

Bethel, Maine, became a pioneering site for organizational development in 1947 when psychologist Kurt Lewin, along with colleagues from the National Training Laboratories (NTL), conducted groundbreaking research on group dynamics and behavioral science. Joined by renowned scholars and practitioners, Lewin refined concepts like T-groups (training groups), which enhanced individual self-awareness and group effectiveness. NTL, later renamed the NTL Institute for Applied Behavioral Science, organized summer sessions in Bethel, leveraging its remote setting for intensive workshops. These initiatives not only advanced organizational development but also significantly influenced Bethel’s economy and open-minded community culture. In 2017, NTL relocated its headquarters to Alexandria, Virginia, as part of a broader federal consolidation, marking the end of an era for Bethel’s direct institutional ties to the field.

Newly hatched Bombay locust nymphs are green with black spots. After they have grown and shed their skin several times they become more variable in colour. Some are plain green, and others are either orange-brown or green with a black spot at the base of each wingpad. The immature adults are at first pale brown with a yellowish dorsal stripe and a dark-coloured prothorax with two lateral pale bands. After six to eight weeks the general colour becomes darker and changes to a rosy red colour, particularly noticeable on the hind wings. When the locusts become mature the following year, they turn dark brown.

Scientists have studied the properties of cirrus using several different methods. Lidar (laser-based radar) gives highly accurate information on the cloud's altitude, length, and width. Balloon-carried hygrometers measure the humidity of the cirrus cloud but are not accurate enough to measure the depth of the cloud. Radar units give information on the altitudes and thicknesses of cirrus clouds. Another data source is satellite measurements from the Stratospheric Aerosol and Gas Experiment program. These satellites measure where infrared radiation is absorbed in the atmosphere, and if it is absorbed at cirrus altitudes, then it is assumed that there are cirrus clouds in that location. NASA's Moderate-Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer gives information on the cirrus cloud cover by measuring reflected infrared radiation of various specific frequencies during the day. During the night, it determines cirrus cover by detecting the Earth's infrared emissions. The cloud reflects this radiation back to the ground, thus enabling satellites to see the "shadow" it casts into space. Visual observations from aircraft or the ground provide additional information about cirrus clouds. Particle Analysis by Laser Mass Spectrometry (PALMS) is used to identify the type of nucleation seeds that spawned the ice crystals in a cirrus cloud.

**Alternative medicine**  
Some evidence suggests acupuncture may modestly relieve allergic rhinitis symptoms, with studies showing reduced nasal symptoms, medication use, and IgE levels. Trials targeting acupoints like L120, ST2, ST36, or the sphenopalatine acupoint (SPA) are extensive but vary in quality. The American Academy of Otolaryngology supports acupuncture, while the American Academy of Allergy, Asthma & Immunology does not recommend complementary-alternative medicine due to insufficient evidence. Other alternative therapies lack robust scientific backing.

**Infant mortality**  
A 1995–2000 U.S. study found infants of Mexican-immigrant women had 10% lower mortality rates than non-Hispanic white women, supporting the "epidemiologic paradox." Refugee communities like Mexican immigrants face socioeconomic hardship but exhibit low infant mortality, while Palestine refugees in Gaza experience higher rates (15.8 per 1,000 in 2013) compared to Israel (3.5) and the Arab world. Neonatal mortality among Palestine refugees, driven by inadequate care, mirrors patterns in low-resource regions, with preventable causes like poor neonatal care leading deaths. Similar trends are observed in other conflict-affected areas.

In June 1994, near Seefeld in Austrian Tirol, at a 1600 metres high mountain trail to the Reither Spitze, in the vicinity of the Reither Joch-Alm, Bernd Lammerer discovered a pterosaur skeleton. The remains have been secured as five stone plates, removed on several occasions. In 2003, Peter Wellnhofer identified the fossil as a specimen of Eudimorphodon, a cf. E. ranzii. As it was 10 to 25% shorter than the latter's holotype, Wellnhofer considered it a juvenile. The same year Fabio Marco Dalla Vecchia doubted the comparability to E. ranzii and suggested that it represent a separate Eudimorphodon species. In 2009, Dalla Vecchia concluded that the specimen was neither a juvenile nor closely related to Eudimorphodon.

The kinase ATM (ataxia-telangiectasia mutated) phosphorylates KAP1 at S824, disrupting viral latency by derepressing herpesvirus genomes. Chloroquine activates ATM without DNA damage, enabling KAP1 phosphorylation and rendering HCMV chromatin permissive for transcription, augmented by TNF. This may induce lytic viral gene expression, potentially purging latent virus when combined with antiretroviral therapy (ART). Such approaches aim to eliminate persistent infections or enhance immune-mediated tumor clearance in malignancies like Burkitt lymphoma.

The Spurling test is used to evaluate cervical radiculopathy in individuals with abnormal electromyogram studies. It is relatively sensitive in diagnosing nerve root compression but less so for patients without classic radicular signs. A 2011 study evaluated 257 patients with clinical cervical radiculopathy, correlating Spurling's test findings with CT scans. The test showed 95% sensitivity and 94% specificity for nerve root pathology. However, its overall sensitivity remains mild-to-moderate, necessitating combined use with other tests for accurate diagnosis.

Pope Pius VII (reigned 1800–1823) faced Napoleon I as the central challenge of his pontificate, seeking to preserve the Church’s independence amid the emperor’s demands for control over ecclesiastical appointments and policies. Napoleon insisted the Papal States adhere to his Continental System and demanded Pius VII submit to French authority, while the pope resisted, seeking the restoration of papal territories and the release of the "Black Cardinals"—thirteen cardinals exiled and stripped of property for refusing to attend Napoleon’s 1810 wedding. Pius VII’s conciliatory yet firm diplomacy aimed to reclaim the Church’s autonomy, even as Napoleon’s dominance temporarily overshadowed the papacy.

**Research and analysis**  
After its discovery in 2010 near the Yukagir coastline, Yuka was stored in Siberian permafrost before being transported to Moscow for study. The mummified remains, preserved in a special glass cage at freezing temperatures, were first examined by researchers including Albert Protopopov and a team from the Sakha Academy of Sciences (Yakutia). Yuka, regarded as the best-preserved mammoth in paleontological history, was later displayed in Moscow, Japan, and Taiwan. By the time scientists arrived, much of the low bluff where Yuka was found had eroded. Since at least 2014, Yuka has been exhibited in Moscow, offering unprecedented insights due to its intact soft tissues, fur, and brain.

The grey fantail (Rhipidura albiscapa) is a seasonal breeder, typically raising several broods per season, each consisting of 3–4 cream-colored eggs spotted with grey and brown. Both parents share nest-building duties, constructing a compact, cup-shaped nest from moss, bark, and spiderweb, often lined with feathers and fur. Incubation lasts around two weeks, with both adults also sharing feeding responsibilities for the chicks. During the breeding season, males develop large testes and pronounced cloacal protuberances, a morphological adaptation linked to sperm competition, suggesting frequent extra-pair copulations. Despite being monomorphic and monochromatic, males exhibit aggressive behavior toward their mates during nest-building, possibly to guard against cuckoldry, though they do not engage in prolonged mate guarding. Nest predation is high, with 83% of clutches lost before fledging. The species' reproductive strategy combines high paternal care with traits indicative of a promiscuous mating system.

Valsartan blocks the actions of angiotensin II, which include constricting blood vessels and activating aldosterone, to reduce blood pressure. The drug binds to angiotensin type I receptors (AT1), working as an antagonist. This mechanism of action is different than that of the ACE inhibitor drugs, which block the conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II. As valsartan acts at the receptor, it can provide more complete angiotensin II antagonism since angiotensin II is generated by other enzymes as well as ACE. Also, valsartan does not affect the metabolism of bradykinin like ACE inhibitors do.

**PEP and love triangle**  
Malachy Fisher was diagnosed with HIV after numerous unprotected sexual encounters with Mercedes McQueen. After dumping Malachy, Mercedes had sex with his brother, Kris Fisher, leading to a love triangle. When Malachy revealed his HIV status, Kris sought immediate medical attention at a clinic, where he received post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP)—including entry inhibitors like maraviroc—to reduce his risk of infection. This storyline marked a significant moment in British soap operas, as it introduced PEP treatment to a mainstream audience, dispelling myths about HIV transmission and prevention. Hollyoaks used the plot to highlight modern medical advancements while exploring the emotional fallout between the brothers and Mercedes.

**Economy of Bosnia and Herzegovina**  

As part of Yugoslavia, Bosnia and Herzegovina was among its poorer republics, with a centrally planned economy and inefficient industries. The 1992–1995 Bosnian War devastated the country, killing around 100,000 people and displacing over two million. The conflict caused an estimated $50 billion in material damage, collapsing GDP by 80% and triggering hyperinflation, food shortages, and a surge in the informal economy. Post-war challenges included high unemployment, widespread poverty, and the struggle to transition to a market economy. By 2017, Bosnia’s debt stood at over $5 billion, with unemployment near 40% (18–22% including the gray economy). Despite reconstruction aid, recovery remains slow, with GDP still below pre-war levels. Structural reforms since 2015 aim to revitalize the private sector and attract investment.

**The Change (1991 and 2018)**  
In *The Change: Women, Ageing, and the Menopause* (1991), feminist scholar Germaine Greer critiques societal myths around aging women, preferring the term "climacteric" over "menopause." The *New York Times* reviewer Natalie Angier hailed it as a "brilliant, gutsy, exhilarating, exasperating fury of a book." Greer challenges fears perpetuated by industries, writing in *The Age* (2002) that scaring women into compliance is "big business and hugely profitable." Unlike conventional menopause literature, she explores historical, cultural, and medical perspectives on aging women, rejecting hormone replacement therapy as exploitative. The book was updated and reissued in 2018, reaffirming its influence in feminist discourse.

The essay *The Construction of Identity in Art: Alberto Rey's Journey* highlights Rey's exploration of identity in his work. For over a decade, Rey's art focused on reclaiming his Cuban heritage, during which he grappled with concerns of cultural displacement and memory. To analyze what he valued, he painted cherished objects from his childhood in Havana and Miami. The *Appropriated Memories Series (1996–1997)* aims to reconstruct fragmented personal and collective histories, using images from family albums and Cuban archives to represent both personal nostalgia and broader political narratives. Since painting these beloved memories, Rey's work has sought to mend the disconnect between his Cuban roots and his life in the U.S. The series reflects his ongoing engagement with themes of identity, memory, and cultural legacy.

As early as 18 June 1973, the Social Democrats handed in a popular Initiative, in which a demand was made for a loan of 200 million francs, payable over 10 years, to fund the extension of the public transport system. The tram network that had been badly neglected, was to profit from this in particular. The city council dragged its feet over the issue, considering it unnecessary as they were following their own modernisation concept. However, on 13 March 1977 the initiative was narrowly approved with 61,599 votes to 58,588 (51.25%) in favour. Consequently, the Zürich tram network was extensively modernised and optimised. In 1978 the tunnel section below the A1L road that had been approved seven years ago was almost finished. Of the 200 million francs that had been approved, 123 million were earmarked for a new tramline leading from Milchbrück through the tunnel to Schwamendingen (and from there on to Hirzenbach and the Stettbach railway station). The plan was approved in a municipal referendum on 24 September 1978 by 69,170 to 44,627 votes (60.78% yes). The 2.5 km long Milchbuck–Schwamendingen tunnel started operating on 1 February 1986. Since then, two lines stop at the three underground stations there: Tierspital, Waldgarten und Schörlistrasse.

Spurling's test is somewhat specific when used for individuals with an abnormal electromyogram study and is a relatively sensitive physical examination maneuver in diagnosing cervical spondylosis or acute cervical radiculopathy. It is not a very sensitive test when used for individuals without classic radicular signs suggestive of cervical radiculopathy. In 2011, one study evaluated 257 patients with clinical cervical radiculopathy and correlated CT scan findings with clinical exam findings using the Spurling's test. The Spurling's test was 95% sensitive and 94% specific for diagnosing nerve root pathology.

Hydras are small freshwater cnidarians, typically about 0.5–2 inches long, with a tubular body divided into a head and foot. The head consists of a mouth surrounded by tentacles, which capture prey like small invertebrates. The foot, or basal disc, secretes a sticky substance to attach to substrates like rocks or vegetation. Hydras inhabit quiet freshwater environments, from ponds to streams, where they anchor themselves while feeding and regenerating. The basal disc enables stability and locomotion.

PhBL is a constructivist form of learning, in which learners are seen as active knowledge builders and information is seen as being constructed as a result of problem-solving. Information and skills are constructed out of ‘little pieces’ into a whole relevant to the situation at the time. When phenomenon based learning occurs in a collaborative setting (the learners work in teams, for example), it supports the socio-constructivist and sociocultural learning theories, in which information is not seen only as an internal element of an individual; instead, information is seen as being formed in a social context. Central issues in the sociocultural learning theories include cultural artifacts (e.g. systems of symbols such as language, mathematical calculation rules and different kinds of thinking tools) – not every learner needs to reinvent the wheel, they can use the information and tools transmitted by cultures.

The lazuline sabrewing is found in the mountains of north-central and western Venezuela, the Sierra de Perijá on the Venezuela-Colombia border, the Sierra Nevada de Santa Marta and Eastern Andes of Colombia, and into Ecuador as far south as Napo Province. It inhabits semi-deciduous montane forest, the edges of plantations, the lower reaches of páramo, and gardens. In elevation it generally ranges between 900 and 3,000 m (3,000 and 9,800 ft). In Venezuela it is most common between 1,200 and 2,300 m (3,900 and 7,500 ft) and has been recorded as low as 450 m (1,500 ft) in Colombia.

What is now White Plains was part of Cherokee lands ceded to the United States with the signing of the Third Treaty of Tellico in 1805. The Walton Road, a stagecoach road completed in 1801 connecting Knoxville and Nashville, brought the earliest permanent legal settlers to the Upper Cumberland region. Around 1804, an early pioneer named Daniel Alexander established an inn along the Walton Road at what is now White Plains. In 1808, Alexander sold the inn and land to William Quarles, a lawyer and Revolutionary War veteran from Bedford County, Virginia. Quarles moved his family, belongings, and thirty slaves to the new land in December 1809. According to family tradition, Quarles named the land "White Plains," as the waves of prairie grass appeared white in the winter sunlight as Quarles looked out over the land from the edge of the Cumberland Plateau.

Thaler was elected to the New York Supreme Court in November 1971 but never assumed office after being indicted on December 21, 1971, for criminally receiving $800,000 in stolen Treasury bills. The **2016 United Kingdom European Union membership referendum** saw the **Scottish National Party (SNP)** as the only party to oppose the parliamentary bill authorising the vote a year prior, having supported continued membership in the **1975 EEC referendum**. The **Remain campaign** was led by **Britain Stronger in Europe**, backed by Labour, the Liberal Democrats, and the SNP, while **Prime Minister David Cameron** and **Chancellor George Osborne** supported it despite the Conservative Party's official neutrality. The **UK Independence Party (UKIP)** was the only main party in Great Britain to campaign for **Leave**. An infantryman's military kit extends beyond main arms and armor, including combat boots, battledress, camping gear, survival equipment, secondary weapons, ammunition, rations, water, and mission-specific items. One of the most valuable pieces of gear is the entrenching tool—a folding spade—used for digging defenses, daily tasks, and even as a weapon. Infantry often share heavier equipment like tents or crew-served weapons, distributing the load among several soldiers. Marching loads typically range from 25–45 kg (60–100 lbs), with some exceeding 45 kg (100 lbs). Such heavy burdens have persisted for centuries; in the late Roman Republic, legionaries were nicknamed "Marius' mules" due to their role as long-service professionals who carried most of their equipment themselves. This nickname reflected their primary activity—bearing the weight of their legion on their backs—after reforms by Gaius Marius eliminated baggage trains and forced soldiers to self-sustain. The term underscores the enduring challenge of balancing combat effectiveness with mobility, a concern still relevant to modern infantry.

Oklahoma City University's faculty consists of highly qualified educators, with over 85% holding terminal degrees in their disciplines. All classes are taught in person by faculty, with an 11:1 student-faculty ratio. Average class sizes are 17 for freshmen and 13 for upperclassmen.

*"Sing Faster: The Stagehands' Ring Cycle"* is a documentary film chronicling the backstage preparations for the San Francisco Opera's production of Wagner's *Der Ring des Nibelungen*. Filmed in the weeks leading up to opening night, it captures the cast and orchestra in rehearsals while focusing on the stagehands—members of the International Alliance of Theatrical Stage Employees (IATSE)—who rehearse complex tasks like moving massive set pieces, including a 4,000-pound dragon, requiring up to 20 crew members. Backstage, they manage props, rigging, and quick scene changes, ensuring seamless performances. The film highlights their skilled, often unseen labor.

The magazine started as Buletini i Institutit të Studimeve (Bulletin of the Institute of Studies), published by the Albanian Institute of History established in 1946, representing the first scientific institute in post World War II Albania. It had separate sections for linguistics, literary studies, and history. After the first issue with this name, it promptly changed to Buletin i Institutit të Shkencave (Bulletin of the Institute of Sciences), published by the Albanian Institute of Science (1948-1957) which had replaced the Institute of Studies. The magazine preserved it previous structure. In 1952, it split in two separate publications: Buletini për Shkencat Natyrore (Bulletin for the Natural Sciences), and Bulletini për Shkencat Shoqërore (Bulletin for Social Studies). The later one focused on linguistics, history, archaeology, ethnography, etc. Articles started being accompanied by a resume in French.

**Investigating fires** involves determining a fire's origin and cause, often complicated by the destruction of key evidence by the fire itself. Many fires are accidental, caused by human activities like unattended campfires or poor maintenance (e.g., car fires from electrical faults). In areas storing organic waste, spontaneous combustion is possible. Fire investigators examine burn patterns, debris, and witness accounts to reconstruct events, sometimes collaborating with forensic experts to identify arson or other causes.

The verification regimes in arms control treaties contain many tools to enable them to hold parties accountable for their actions and violations of their treaty agreements. The START Treaty verification provisions were the most complicated and demanding of any agreement at the time by providing twelve different types of inspection. Data exchanges and declarations between parties became required and included exact quantities, technical characteristics, locations, movements, and the status of all offensive nuclear threats. The national technical means of verification (NTM) provision protected satellites and other information-gathering systems controlled by the verifying side, as they helped to verify adherence of international treaties. The international technical means of verification provision protected the multilateral technical systems specified in other treaties. Co-operative measures were established to facilitate verification by the NTM and included displaying items in plain sight and not hiding them from detection. The new on-site inspections (OSI) and Perimeter and Portal Continuous Monitoring (PPCM) provisions helped to maintain the treaty's integrity by providing a regulatory system manned by a representative from the verifying side at all times. In addition, access to telemetry from ballistic missile flight tests are now required, including exchanges of tapes and a ban on encryption and encapsulation from both parties.

**Tannuella** is an early mollusk from the **Early Cambrian** (~530 million years ago) and a potential cephalopod ancestor. While **Plectronoceras** is the oldest confirmed cephalopod fossil, Tannuella's chambered shell suggests a link to cephalopods, possibly diverging from **monoplacophorans**. Hypotheses propose Tannuella as a precursor to cephalopods, marking a transition from bottom-dwelling to buoyant, open-water forms. [Sources: Brock & Paterson (2004); Vinther (2015)]

**History**  
ISG15 was first identified in 1979 by Peter Lengyel and colleagues as a 14.5 kDa protein. In 1984, Ernest Knight Jr's group purified it as a 15 kDa interferon (IFN)-induced protein, later cloned in 1986. Initially termed the "15 kDa protein," it was renamed interferon-stimulated-gene-15 (ISG15) in 1987 after James E. Darnell's team linked its transcription to IFN-β. That same year, ISG15 was found to cross-react with anti-ubiquitin antibodies, revealing its structural similarity to ubiquitin. The ubiquitin-like conjugation of ISG15 to cellular proteins, termed "ISGylation," was later characterized. In subsequent decades, studies, including work by Lenschow et al. (2007), highlighted ISG15's critical antiviral role against pathogens like influenza and herpesviruses. Additionally, ISG15 was discovered to be secreted—despite lacking a signal peptide—by immune cells like neutrophils and monocytes, and detected in human serum, suggesting extracellular immunomodulatory functions, though its secretion mechanism remains unclear.

James Risen first uncovered information about the National Security Agency's warrantless surveillance program, code-named "Stellar Wind," in the early 2000s. Alongside co-writer Eric Lichtblau, Risen investigated the program, which began shortly after the 9/11 attacks, but their reporting faced resistance. *The New York Times* initially spiked the story in 2004 at the request of the White House, with Executive Editor Bill Keller and Washington Bureau Chief Philip Taubman agreeing to delay publication—even meeting with President George W. Bush to discuss the matter. After repeated rejections, Risen included the revelations in his 2006 book, *State of War: The Secret History of the CIA and the Bush Administration*, angering *Times* editors. Under pressure from the impending book release, *The New York Times* finally published much of Risen and Lichtblau's report in December 2005, exposing the legal disputes within the Bush administration over the program. The story later earned Risen and Lichtblau a Pulitzer Prize.

DNA damage response (DDR) is the overarching mechanism which mediates the cell's detection and response to DNA damage. This includes the process of detecting DSB within the cell, and the subsequent triggering and regulation of DSB repair pathways. Upstream detections of DNA damage via DDR will lead to the activation of downstream responses such as senescence, cell apoptosis, halting transcription and activating DNA repair mechanisms. Proteins such as the proteins ATM, ATR and DNA-dependent protein kinase (DNA-PK) are vital for the process of detection of DSB in DDR, and these proteins are recruited to the DSB site in the DNA. In particular, ATM has been identified as the protein kinase in charge of the global meditation of cellular responses to DSB, which includes various DSB repair pathways. Following the recruitment of the aforementioned proteins to DNA damage sites, they will in turn trigger cellular responses and repair pathways to mitigate and repair the damage caused. In short, these vital upstream proteins and downstream repair pathways altogether forms the DDR, which plays a vital role in DSB repair pathways regulation.

Everglades University enrolls 3,072 students annually, with 98% attending full-time. Minorities make up 57% of the student body, primarily Black and Hispanic. The university employs 290 faculty, maintaining an 11:1 student-to-faculty ratio. (43 words)

Rentenbank’s special promotional loans are geared towards businesses in the agricultural, horticultural, viticultural and silvicultural sectors, including manufacturers of agricultural input materials as well as agriculture-related trade and service companies. The bank also finances projects in the food industry and the associated upstream and downstream industries. Further, Rentenbank promotes civic engagement and public-sector investments in rural areas. The bank’s statutory promotional mandate explicitly includes promoting renewable energy, renewable agricultural resources, organic farming, agriculture-related environmental and consumer protection, and animal welfare. The loans are extended via local banks on a competitively neutral basis. In addition, Rentenbank awards grants for innovations and applied research projects, as well as for projects and institutions that are of particular importance to agribusiness and rural areas. Rentenbank’s Rehwinkel Foundation supports scientific research, promotes events that foster collaboration between agricultural science and industry, and awards scholarships to students on relevant Master’s programmes. Rentenbank focuses its efforts on promoting innovation, in particular start-ups that are developing new, tech-based business ideas that can play a vital role in driving innovation in agriculture.

Cyrus Harris (born August 22, 1817, in Pontotoc, Mississippi) moved to Mill Creek in 1855 and became the first governor of the Chickasaw Nation, serving five terms. He made his home near Mill Creek, Indian Territory, and was buried in a rural cemetery in Murray County, Oklahoma.

Mugo was trained as a gynaecologist and epidemiologist. She initially began her career at rural hospitals, and, later, the Pumwani Maternity Hospital.
