Developing as a tropical depression on October 18 near Palau Island, Hester gradually intensified as it moved westward towards the Philippines. Across the Philippines, Hester was responsible for six deaths and ₱5 million in damage. After passing over Mindanao and the Visayas as a tropical storm between October 20 and 21, the storm intensified into a typhoon before striking Palawan. Once over the South China Sea, Hester further strengthened and ultimately attained peak winds of 165 km/h (105 mph). On October 23, the storm made landfall near Huế, South Vietnam. Once onshore, Hester rapidly weakened and dissipated on October 24 over Laos.

In China, the basic pension insurance provides 40% of the insured's monthly wage under central government guidelines. Disability benefits vary by severity, categorized into 10 degrees. Temporary disability benefits are calculated as a percentage of the individual's wage (e.g., 25% disability yields 2/3 of the average weekly wage × 25%) and last until maximum medical improvement is reached. For permanent disabilities, a first-degree disability entitles the insured to 90% of their previous wage plus a 27-month lump sum, while a sixth-degree disability grants 60% of the wage (if no suitable job is offered) plus a 16-month lump sum. A tenth-degree disability provides a seven-month lump sum. Minimum benefit amounts are determined by provincial governments based on local living standards.

Aside from his membership in the Muiderkring (which was not as formerly supposed, an official club), at the start of the 1630s he was also in touch with René Descartes, with Rembrandt, and the painter Jan Lievens. He became friends with John Donne, and translated his poems into Dutch. He was unable to write poetry for months because of his anguish over his wife's death, but eventually he composed, inspired by Petrarch, the sonnet Op de dood van Sterre (On the death of Sterre), which was well received. He added the poem to his Dagh-werck, which he left unfinished: the day he has described has not ended yet, but his Sterre is already dead. After sending the unfinished work to different friends for approval, he eventually published it in 1658 as part of his Koren-bloemen.

The "Vanguard and neighborhood program controversy" at River Oaks Elementary School began when HISD board trustee Ron Franklin introduced a request on behalf of the River Oaks neighborhood to reinstate a neighborhood program in late 1995. Parents from River Oaks pressured the school to restore local enrollment, which had been discontinued years earlier when most families opted for private schools. Some Vanguard parents, many of whom were middle-class professionals from areas like West University Place, opposed the proposal, fearing that admitting affluent neighborhood children would displace students from across HISD and reduce the school’s diversity. They also worried that the River Oaks program would diminish their control over the school. At the time, the school had around 500 students, with racial demographics reflecting broader national trends (47% white, 27% Hispanic, 15% Black). Black, Hispanic, and white Vanguard parents protested the change, arguing it would undermine integration. Franklin accused them of a "them versus us" mentality, noting that most other Vanguard schools included neighborhood components.

In April 1964, Roddenberry presented the Star Trek draft to Desilu Productions, a leading independent television production company. He met with Herbert F. Solow, Desilu's director of production. In introducing federal discrimination protections for LGBTI people, the Gillard Government promised that religious bodies would be exempt, unless they were aged care providers receiving Commonwealth funding. The main exemptions are in sections 37 and 38 of the Sex Discrimination Act 1984, which include the ability for religious educational institutions to discriminate against LGBT students and teachers "in good faith in order to avoid injury to the religious susceptibilities of adherents of that religion". In 2017, Perth teacher Craig Campbell was dismissed from a Baptist school after he revealed his sexuality on social media. The Greens promised to repeal religious exemptions to LGBT anti-discrimination protections before the 2016 election, while in January 2018 the Labor Party announced it had no plans to act upon the issue. In the wake of the same-sex marriage debate, the Turnbull Government commissioned a review of religious freedoms chaired by Phillip Ruddock, after conservative Coalition politicians called for increased religious freedoms to discriminate against LGBT people. The need to protect religious freedom was emphasised by Scott Morrison after he replaced Malcolm Turnbull as Prime Minister.

The Egyptian revolution of 2011 began on January 25 with mass protests and clashes between demonstrators and police, who used tear gas and water cannons. By January 28, police withdrew from the streets, and the military was deployed to restore order. Prison guards were later accused of torturing detainees, including a fatal case involving water hoses. On February 2, pro-Mubarak loyalists, including horse and camel riders, stormed Tahrir Square, attacking protesters with whips and swords in the "Battle of the Camel." The clashes left at least three dead and 1,500 injured. A fact-finding judicial committee, led by judges, later confirmed security forces' lethal violence, estimating 846 deaths during the uprising.

The **1492 conclave**, held in August 1492, saw Cardinal Rodrigo Borgia elected pope, taking the name **Alexander VI**. The deciding vote reportedly came from **Cardinal Orsini**.

**Bipolar disorder (BD)** is a heterogeneous mental illness characterized by alternating episodes of mania (or hypomania in BD-II) and depression, often accompanied by cognitive impairments such as executive dysfunction and attentional deficits. The main hallmark of BD is the dysregulation of mood states with opposing affective valence. Neuroimaging studies reveal disrupted functional connectivity in key brain networks, including the **default mode network (DMN)**, **frontoparietal network (FPN)**, and **salience network (SN)**. The FPN, critical for cognitive control, shows altered connectivity, contributing to impaired executive function. Cognitive dysregulation in BD is further linked to disrupted SN recruitment. BD is thought to involve a functional imbalance between hyperactive ventral/limbic regions (emotion generation) and hypoactive dorsal/cognitive regions (regulation). The DMN, implicated in self-referential processing, exhibits aberrant connectivity, particularly in euthymic patients. These disruptions lead to affective instability, cognitive deficits, and impaired integration of emotional and cognitive information.

**Boca de Potrerillos, astronomical observatory 7,000 years ago**  
Archaeologist Denise Carpinteyro Espinosa of Mexico's National Institute of Anthropology and History (INAH) reports that the petroglyphs at Boca de Potrerillos, some related to astronomy and others to daily life, reveal advanced knowledge of the skies. Hunter-gatherer groups inhabited this site, crossed by a river, for over 7,000 years, indicating its enduring importance. Studies suggest the engravings reflect timekeeping for hunting and other activities linked to celestial observations. The site's ecological conditions, once cooler and richer in flora and fauna, supported prolonged human occupation, with petroglyphs offering insights into ancient astronomical practices and survival strategies.

In 1935, LWIUC was disbanded. This followed a shift in policy in the Communist International. The communists now abandoned the idea of forming militant trade unions, and began to work within the established mainstream unions. The workers previously organized by LWIUC joined the Lumber and Sawmill Workers' Union (a union affiliated to the American Federation of Labor).

Afshar studied guitar at the Boston Conservatory of Music. She won the 2000 Orville H. Gibson Award for Best Female Classical Guitarist, as well as the 10th, 11th and 12th annual "Premier Guitarist" awards by the Memphis Chapter of the National Academy of Recording Arts & Sciences, Inc. Afshar was awarded the 2008 Distinguished Teaching Award, the 2000 Eminent Faculty Award, and the 1996 Distinguished Research Award at The University of Memphis. Her other awards include the Tennessee Arts Commission Individual Artist Fellowship Award in Music, the National Endowment for the Arts Recording Award, a Top Prize in the Guitar Foundation of America Competition, Grand Prize in the Aspen Music Festival Guitar Competition, and First Prizes in both the Music Teachers' National Association and the American String Teachers' Association Guitar Competitions.

The Constitution of Tuvalu provides that succession to the Tuvaluan throne is determined by the Parliament of Tuvalu, but until such laws are enacted, it defers to the line of succession under British law. The succession follows absolute primogeniture, governed by the UK's Succession to the Crown Act 2013 and the Act of Settlement 1701, requiring the monarch to be a Protestant "in communion" with the Church of England. The current heir apparent is Prince William, the eldest son of King Charles III. While William holds the title of Prince of Wales in the UK, he has no official title specific to Tuvalu. The shared monarchy requires unanimous realm consent for succession changes.

The 1897 season marked the pinnacle of the professional football era in Greensburg. That year's squad had 27 players, which included a number of new ones. Among those players was George Barclay, of Millville, who played a halfback at Bucknell University and Lafayette University. Barclay brought several of Lafayette top players with him to Greensburg to enhance the team. Greensburg went on to post a 10-1 record. The team's only defeat came at a 12–6 loss in the ninth game to Latrobe. However, the outcome of that game was reversed by a 6–0 score in a season-ending rematch at Latrobe. Greensburg, along with Washington and Jefferson College, had the best football records in Pennsylvania for the 1897 season. Barclay, however, was the only Greensburg player among the eleven named by The Pittsburg Times to their “All-Western Pennsylvania” team.

**Four Peel Gin**, originally named **Watershed Gin**, is an **American-style gin** produced by Watershed Distillery since **2010**. Classified as a **modern gin**, it features a **unique blend of botanicals** anchored by these peels. Bottled at **88 proof**, it is celebrated for its crisp, refreshing profile, ideal for cocktails or pairing with tonic.

**Royal Air Force**  
John Ponsonby was commissioned into the Royal Air Force as a pilot in April 1983, after nine years of service as an infantry officer in the British Army. On 10 April 1983, he was appointed to a permanent commission and promoted to flight lieutenant, with seniority from 10 March 1980. He advanced to squadron leader on 1 July 1988 and to wing commander on 1 January 1994. Ponsonby was appointed an Officer of the Order of the British Empire (OBE) in the 1998 New Year Honours List and served as an aide-de-camp (ADC) to Queen Elizabeth II from 2001 until relinquishing the appointment upon promotion to air commodore (exact date unspecified). He was awarded the Queen's Commendation for Valuable Service in the 2002 Operational Honours List for "gallant and distinguished services in Northern Ireland" from 1 October 2001 to 31 March 2002. Promoted to air vice marshal on 4 January 2005, he concluded his career in this rank.

*Four Saints in Three Acts* premiered at the Wadsworth Atheneum in Hartford, Connecticut, in February 1934 before opening on Broadway at the 44th Street Theatre on February 20, 1934. Defying operatic conventions, the work featured Gertrude Stein’s avant-garde libretto, which prioritized linguistic play over narrative clarity, and Virgil Thomson’s score, blending hymns and folk influences. Directed by John Houseman—then a theater newcomer—with Alexander Smallens as musical director, the production’s abstract, plotless style and all-African American cast made it a landmark of the trans-Atlantic avant-garde. Its Broadway run lasted 48 performances, cementing its status as an experimental milestone.

On 23 October, a two-seat F/A-18D crashed into the mountain Lopper near the Pilatus. The pilot Stefan "Stiwi" Jäger of the Fliegerstaffel 11 and the Aviation medical examiner Volker Lang of the Aeromedical Center (AMC) as passenger were killed. The machine was in the course of a patrol mission on the way to Lake Lucerne an simulated Air policing training. The aviation medical examiner flew, according to the Luftwaffe, to get an impression of the requirements in the cockpit of a fighter jet on such an air policing mission. . As a result of the Aviation accidents and incidents investigation of the Military justice, a "misconception of the situation" has been determined by the pilot as the reason for the controlled flight into terrain. In the investigation report, the investigator of the military jury recommended, on the one hand, that passenger flights should be carried out on combat aircraft only in good weather, and on the other hand the examination of the training of the reversing maneuver, especially in bad weather situations and in low level flight. . This could have consequences for the security management of the Swiss Air Force (as of June 2014).

Born in Berlin, Clotilde was the daughter of Major Hans Edler von der Planitz (1863–1932) from Berlin and Margarete von Muschwitz (1868–1955). She was a member of the German lower nobility. On 25 January 1919, she married Alexander Sakharoff, a Russian dancer, teacher and choreographer.

Football in Turkey stems back to the late 19th century, when Englishmen brought the game with them while living in Salonica (then part of the Ottoman Empire). The first league competition was the Istanbul Football League, which took place in the 1904–05 season. The league went through several variations until the creation of the Millî Lig (Süper Lig) in 1959. Between the creation of the Istanbul League and Millî Lig, several other regional leagues took place: Adana (1924), Ankara (1922), Eskişehir (1924), İzmir (1924), Bursa (1924), and Trabzon (1922), to name a few. The first competition to bring forth a national champion was the former Turkish Football Championship, which began in 1924 and continued until 1951. The championship format was based on a knockout competition, contested between the winners of each of the country's top regional leagues. The National Division (Turkish: Millî Küme) was the first national league competition in Turkey. Started in 1937, the National Division consisted of the strongest clubs from the Ankara, Istanbul, and İzmir leagues. The championship lasted until 1950.

The Pakistan Air Force (PAF) has been using their F-16A fleet to attack militant positions and support the Pakistan Army's operations in North-West Pakistan against the Taliban insurgency. Since May 2009, PAF F-16s have used 500 lb and 2,000 lb unguided and laser-guided bombs to attack caves, tunnels, training camps, ammunition dumps and hide-outs used by the militants. Over 100 combat missions have been flown in South Waziristan and over 300 in the Swat Valley as of 29 July 2009. Prior to the operations in the Swat Valley, approximately 10 of the PAF's F-16s were fitted with high-resolution infra-red sensors for reconnaissance purposes, supplied by the United States, to provide the Pakistani military with detailed imagery of the area.

*Potamopyrgus oppidanus* is endemic to Wellington, New Zealand, and has only been found on Te Ahumairangi Hill in the town belt. St. Thomas Law is best known for its emphasis on relationships, practical training and social justice. Absorption of adapalene through the skin is low. A study with six acne patients treated once daily for five days with two grams of adapalene cream applied to 1,000 cm2 (160 sq in) of skin found no quantifiable amounts, or less than 0.35 ng/mL of the drug, in the patients' blood plasma. Controlled trials of chronic users of adapalene have found drug levels in the patients' plasma to be 0.25 ng/mL.

After the Belgian defeat, around 225,000 Belgian soldiers (around 30 percent of the total force mobilised in 1940) who had been made prisoners of war in 1940 were sent to prisoner of war camps in Germany. The majority of those in captivity (145,000) were Flemish, and 80,000 were Walloons. Most had been reservists, rather than professional soldiers, before the outbreak of war and their detention created a large labour shortage in civilian occupations.

Te Tai Tokerau's boundaries are similar to those of the pre-Mixed Member Proportional (MMP) Northern Maori electorate. Te Tai Tokerau was created ahead of the first MMP election in 1996. In the 2002 boundary redistribution, the size of the electorate shrank to make room for an increase in the number of Māori electorates from six to seven. El Salvador's estimated hydroelectric potential is 2,258 MW, with 1,409 MW located on the Río Lempa, of which only 21% is utilized. The Comisión Ejecutiva Hidroeléctrica del Río Lempa (CEL), a state-owned entity, manages four hydroelectric plants on the Lempa (totaling 472 MW), generating 41% of the country's hydroelectric output. These plants contribute 388 MW, accounting for roughly 10.7% of El Salvador's total electricity production (based on 1997 demand of 3,636 GWh). Hydroelectricity is seasonal, dependent on the May-October rainy season.

The year **1897** marked the pinnacle of Greensburg Athletic Association's professional football era, fielding a **15-man squad** that included **Millville native Ed Abbaticchio**, a standout halfback who played at Bucknell and Lafayette. Coach **George Barclay** bolstered the team by recruiting top players from the **University of Pennsylvania**, helping Greensburg to a **7-2 record**. Their ninth-game loss came against **Latrobe (12-6)**, but Greensburg avenged the defeat in the season finale, **shutting out Latrobe 6-0**. In the UK, where it is known to farmers as black-grass, it is a major weed of cereal crops as it produces a large amount of seed which is shed before the crop is cut. It has developed resistance to a range of herbicides used to control it. Herbicide resistance testing is often needed to understand what herbicides will be effective at treating it. It can occur at very high densities, competing with the crop and seriously reducing the yield of crops such as wheat and barley if not controlled.

During World War II the government dramatically affected the city's population and structures. To better organize the home front and concentrate industrial and material resources, the government evacuated 26,000 people and numerous industries from the European theatre of war. Alma-Ata hosted over 30 industrial facilities removed from the European section of the USSR, eight evacuated hospitals, 15 institutes, universities and technical schools; and around 20 cultural institutions. Motion picture production companies from Leningrad, Kyiv, and Moscow were also moved to Alma-Ata at this time. This brought in so many ethnic Russians that the Kazakhs became a minority in the region.

At the 2018 announcement of the updated shortlist of blueprint priority diseases, the media speculated that a future Disease X could be created intentionally as a biological weapon. In 2018, WHO R&D Blueprint Special Advisor Group member Røttingen was questioned about the potential of Disease X to come from the ability of gene-editing technology to produce synthetic viruses (e.g., the 2017 synthesis of Orthopoxvirus in Canada was cited), which could be released through an accident or even an act of terror. Røttingen said it was unlikely that a future Disease X would originate from a synthetic virus or a bio-weapon. However, he noted the seriousness of such an event, saying, "Synthetic biology allows for the creation of deadly new viruses. It is also the case that where you have a new disease there is no resistance in the population and that means it can spread fast".

**Rejuvenation efforts**  

Eastern Kentucky native Dr. Patrick Angel spent much of his career at the Office of Surface Mining Reclamation and Enforcement (OSMRE), instructing mining companies on land reclamation after strip-mining and mountaintop removal. Initially, his method involved compacting soil and planting grass, which he considered the only reliable plant to stabilize the ground. However, in 2002, scientists like Angel realized Appalachian forests were not regenerating, leaving over 1.5 million acres—larger than Delaware—barren except for grass. Recognizing this ecological disaster, Angel spearheaded efforts to plant more than 187 million trees across 275,000 acres of former mining land. Dr. Kathy Newfont, an Appalachian history professor at the University of Kentucky, called these reforestation efforts "one of the most hopeful things I'd heard about the region in decades."

Human listeners combine information from two ears to localize and separate sound sources originating in different locations in a process called binaural hearing. The powerful signal processing methods found in the neural systems and brains of humans and other animals are flexible, environmentally adaptable, and take place rapidly and seemingly without effort. Emulating the mechanisms of binaural hearing can improve recognition accuracy and signal separation in DSP algorithms, especially in noisy environments. Furthermore, by understanding and exploiting biological mechanisms of sound localization, virtual sound scenes may be rendered with more perceptually relevant methods, allowing listeners to accurately perceive the locations of auditory events. One way to obtain the perceptual-based sound localization is from the sparse approximations of the anthropometric features. Perceptual-based sound localization may be used to enhance and supplement robotic navigation and environment recognition capability. In addition, it is also used to create virtual auditory spaces which is widely implemented in hearing aids.

After the war, Geulen became involved with the Jewish community in Belgium and maintained contact with the children with whom she had come into contact. The Cavaliers began play in the 1970–71 season as an expansion team. They set losing records in each of their first five seasons before winning their first division title in 1976. The 1976 team was led by Austin Carr, Bobby "Bingo" Smith, Jim Chones, Dick Snyder, Nate Thurmond and head coach Bill Fitch. It was remembered for the "Miracle at Richfield," in which the Cavaliers defeated the Washington Bullets, 4–3, in the Eastern Conference Semifinals. They won Game 7, 87–85, on a shot by Snyder with four seconds to go. The Cavaliers moved on to the Eastern Conference Finals for the first time, but were without Chones after he broke his toe before the series opener. As a result, the Cavaliers went on to lose, 4–2, to the Boston Celtics. They made playoff appearances in the following two seasons before a six-year playoff drought.

Damien Chazelle was initially supposed to direct and produce, but was sidetracked by his film Babylon and dropped out. He was replaced by John Requa and Glenn Ficarra. Fagbenle was added to the cast in recurring capacity. In December 2021, Theo Stockman was added to the cast in recurring role, with Anthony Edwards noted as being cast in February 2022.

**HDL1** is an autosomal dominant Huntington's disease-like syndrome caused by an eight-octapeptide repeat insertion in the **PRNP** gene. Only one family has been reported. It is a familial prion disease mimicking inherited neurodegenerative disorders.

**In Recent News**  
Tariq Fancy, the former chief investment officer for sustainable investing at BlackRock, gained attention for his 2021 *USA Today* op-ed, where he accused the financial services industry of "duping the American public" with misleading sustainable investing practices, calling it a "deadly distraction." He claimed Wall Street was greenwashing the economic system, with ESG-labeled funds often failing to deliver real environmental or social impact. *The Guardian* later quoted him stating ESG investing lacks genuine social impact. Fancy expanded his critique in a three-part essay, *The Secret Diary of a 'Sustainable Investor'*, on Medium, detailing his disillusionment with BlackRock's ESG practices. In June 2022, he added a fourth chapter, further condemning the industry’s "convenient fantasies" amid climate urgency.

Dolunay Soysert was born on 25 March 1973 in Adana, Turkey, to a Circassian family. At age three or four, her family moved to Ankara due to her father's job. She completed primary and middle school there before finishing high school at Istanbul's Private Moda College.

**Breeding**  
By the mid-1980s, northern white rhinos were wiped out in Uganda and Sudan, with only 13 remaining in Garamba National Park, Zaire. In 1986, the IUCN’s Conservation Breeding Specialist Group (CBSG) met at Dvůr Králové Zoo to discuss conservation strategies, including importing an older male, Dinka, from London. In 1996, Saut, a calf from the original 1975 group, was returned from San Diego Zoo to boost breeding efforts. However, attempts to breed northern whites with southern rhinos failed, as did artificial insemination. Conservationists later harvested oocytes from females like Nabire for in vitro fertilization, with embryos planned for transfer to southern white surrogates—a groundbreaking but risky effort to save the subspecies.

Eisenhower unveiled the New Look, his first national security policy, on October 30, 1953. It reflected his concern for balancing the Cold War military commitments of the United States with the risk of overwhelming the nation's financial resources. The new policy emphasized reliance on strategic nuclear weapons, rather than conventional military power, to deter both conventional and nuclear military threats. The U.S. military developed a strategy of nuclear deterrence based upon the triad of land-based intercontinental ballistic missiles (ICBMs), strategic bombers, and submarine-launched ballistic missiles (SLBMs). Throughout his presidency, Eisenhower insisted on having plans to retaliate, fight, and win a nuclear war against the Soviets, although he hoped he would never feel forced to use such weapons.

In October of 2022, Hinchcliffe joined the crew of F1's "Weekend Warmup" for its 'grand-prix week' editions for the United States Grand Prix and the Mexico City Grand Prix.

There are two main ways in which minerals hydrate. One is conversion of an oxide to a double hydroxide, as with the hydration of calcium oxide—CaO—to calcium hydroxide—Ca(OH)2, the other is with the incorporation of water molecules directly into the crystalline structure of a new mineral. The later process is exhibited in the hydration of feldspars to clay minerals, garnet to chlorite, or kyanite to muscovite.

White Wolf, originally formed as **Wolf** in 1975 in Edmonton, Alberta, briefly changed their name to **Whitefox** before settling on **White Wolf**. Chromolithographs were widely used in the 19th and early 20th centuries for advertisements, book illustrations (including children's and gift books), and scientific works, offering a cheaper alternative to hand-colored engravings. However, they are now rare due to the labor-intensive, costly production process—requiring multiple heavy stones—and the deterioration of early prints. By the late 19th century, photomechanical processes and offset lithography replaced chromolithography as more efficient methods. Though chromolithographs were more affordable than original paintings, later color printing methods further reduced costs. Preservation efforts focus on maintaining surviving examples of this once-revolutionary but now obsolete technique.

The Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia (FARC), Colombia's oldest leftist guerrilla group, financed its insurgency through drug trafficking, kidnapping, and extortion. Before the 2002 coup attempt against Hugo Chávez, discontent brewed within the Venezuelan military, culminating in high-ranking officers publicly opposing his government. Chávez's administration allegedly forced the military to assist FARC, including providing safe haven, arms, and training in guerrilla warfare to pro-Chávez militias. The U.S. Treasury accused Chávez's government of funding FARC's Caracas office, while the International Institute for Strategic Studies (IISS) claimed FARC trained Venezuelan civilians in urban combat during the coup attempt. Venezuelan diplomats dismissed these allegations, though leaked documents revealed FARC's requests for logistical support. The IISS noted Chávez later distanced himself from FARC due to international pressure.

The Village of Seneca Falls residents voted to dissolve the village on March 16, 2010, with the dissolution taking effect at the end of 2011. The village began exploring dissolution in 2008, commissioning two studies that examined cost savings, service consolidation, and governance impacts. Supporters projected annual savings of $3 million, with lower property taxes for residents. The final vote passed 652–586, making Seneca Falls the largest New York village to dissolve and the first to do so under the state's modern dissolution laws. The town absorbed village services, including police and utilities.

Eisenhower appointed five justices of the Supreme Court of the United States. In 1953, Eisenhower nominated Governor Earl Warren to succeed Chief Justice Fred M. Vinson. Many conservative Republicans opposed Warren's nomination, but they were unable to block the appointment, and Warren's nomination was approved by the Senate in January 1954. Warren presided over a court that generated numerous liberal rulings on various topics, beginning in 1954 with the desegregation case of Brown v. Board of Education. Eisenhower approved of the Brown decision. Robert H. Jackson's death in late 1954 generated another vacancy on the Supreme Court, and Eisenhower successfully nominated federal appellate judge John Marshall Harlan II to succeed Jackson. Harlan joined the conservative bloc on the bench, often supporting the position of Associate Justice Felix Frankfurter.

The negotiations that led to START I began in earnest in early 1985, though tensions had escalated in November 1983 when the Soviet Union severed communication with the U.S. amid fears triggered by NATO's Able Archer 83 exercise. In January 1985, U.S. Secretary of State George Shultz and Soviet Foreign Minister Andrey Gromyko agreed to a three-part plan—the Nuclear and Space Talks (NST)—addressing strategic arms (START), intermediate-range missiles (INF), and missile defense (SDI). This framework gained momentum at the November 1985 Geneva Summit. The INF Treaty, signed in December 1987, emerged from these talks. START I was finalized on 31 July 1991, signed by U.S. President George H. W. Bush and Soviet leader Mikhail Gorbachev.

In 1871, Gordon obtained the position of chief engineer for water supply in Victoria, Australia, where he continued the works of engineer Joseph Brady on the Coliban Water Supply for Bendigo. He was commissioned to report on public waterworks in 1871, but the appointment was subsequently cancelled. In October 1871 the Duffy government began fresh negotiations and Gordon moved to Melbourne, arriving on 5 May 1872 to take up the position as chief engineer of the Board of Lands and Works. One of his major achievements in this period was the construction of Australia's first arch concrete dam in 1873, at Stoney Creek, for the Geelong water supply. He transferred to the Water Supply Department on 2 February 1875, where he was chief engineer until the infamous Black Wednesday, (9 January 1878), when dozens of public servants were sacked in a government financial crisis. Despite the matter of his dismissal being raised in the Victorian Parliament, and Gordon having written to the Victorian Governor and the Queen, in an attempt to be reinstated and gain compensation. He did not get his post back.

Hibbertia bicarpellata is a shrub that typically grows to a height of 1.5 m (4 ft 11 in), its branches with ridges from below the leaf bases, and its branches and leaves covered with rosette-like bundles of hairs. The leaves are elliptic, 20–45 mm (0.79–1.77 in) long and 8–15 mm (0.31–0.59 in) wide on a petiole 0.5–2 mm (0.020–0.079 in) long. The flowers are arranged singly in leaf axils on a stiff peduncle 4.4–7.5 mm (0.17–0.30 in) long, with linear to lance-shaped bracts 3.5–5 mm (0.14–0.20 in) long. The five sepals are joined at the base, the three outer sepal lobes 7–10 mm (0.28–0.39 in) long and the inner lobes 5.6–6.6 mm (0.22–0.26 in) long. The five petals are egg-shaped with the narrower end towards the base, yellow, 6.8–9.6 mm (0.27–0.38 in) long and notched at the tip. There are twenty to twenty-six stamens arranged around the two carpels, each carpel with four ovules. Flowering occurs from February to September.

The active site of MetAP2 has a structural motif characteristic of many metalloenzymes—including the dioxygen carrier protein, hemerythrin; the dinuclear non-heme iron protein, ribonucleotide reductase; leucine aminopeptidase; urease; arginase; several phosphatases and phosphoesterases—that includes two bridging carboxylate ligands and a bridging water or hydroxide ligand. Specifically in human MetAP2 (PDB: 1BOA), one of the catalytic metal ions is bound to His331, Glu364, Glu459, Asp263, and a bridging water or hydroxide, while the other metal ion is bound to Asp251 (bidentate), App262 (bidentate), Glu459, and the same bridging water or hydroxide. Here, the two bridging carboxylates are Asp262 and Glu459.

The music video for Frank Ocean's "Thinkin Bout You," directed by High5Collective and released on **September 15, 2011**, features Ocean in doctor's garb alongside actress Aja Warren. High5Collective, known for producing unofficial, high-quality videos on spec, claimed this as the song's official visual, later confirmed by media reports noting Ocean's appearance and his promotion of the clip on Tumblr. The video juxtaposes cultural imagery of medical trauma and youthful rebellion, with Ocean playing a caregiver to a bloodied, ailing girl (Warren), whose dreams and struggles are central to the narrative. Beyond her storyline, the video explores themes of tragedy and lawlessness, subverting traditional gender roles by depicting Ocean in a nurturing rather than dominant role. The visuals, though not a literal interpretation of the lyrics, serve as a poignant accompaniment to the song's emotional depth.

The Australian/New Zealand leg of the **All Hope Is Gone World Tour** marked the first time Slipknot brought their full production to Australia, with percussionist Shawn Crahan calling it "the real set, the real deal." The band traveled with over **11 tons of equipment**, including hydraulic drum risers, pyrotechnics, and elaborate stage setups. Prior to the first show, vocalist **Corey Taylor** (Slipknot's lead singer) sprained his thumb while skydiving, though the injury did not prevent him from performing. This leg showcased Slipknot's most ambitious live production in the region to date.

The first step in preparing a hot air balloon for flight after unpacking it from its carrying bag is laying it out on the ground and connecting it to the basket and burner. A gasoline-powered fan is commonly used to blow cold air into the envelope, partially inflating the balloon to establish its basic shape. This cold inflation ensures the envelope is stable before the burner flame is aimed into the mouth to heat the air inside. During this process, a crew member stationed opposite the mouth holds a crown line tied to the balloon's apex, preventing excessive sway and keeping the throat open to avoid fabric interference with the flames. Once the balloon is upright, the pilot and passengers climb into the basket. When ready for launch, the pilot directs more heat into the envelope, making the balloon buoyant enough to lift off. The crown line operator plays a critical role in maintaining stability during inflation, especially in breezy conditions.

The Riverside Area Council (#045) was founded in 1919 as the Riverside Council. In 1927, the Hemet-San Jacinto Valley Council (#028) merged into the Riverside Area Council (#045). In 1944, the council changed its name to Riverside County and Redlands, then in 1945 it went back to Riverside County. The Grayback Council (#024) was founded in 1945 as the Redlands Area Council. It changed its name in 1952. The Arrowhead Area (#048) council was formed in 1922 as the San Bernardino Valley council. Prior to adopting the name Arrowhead Area in 1933, the council was known as the San Bernardino District from 1923 to 1933.

Govardhan of Gour had two sons: Garuda, his heir, and another son named Radhaballabh, who later embraced Sunni Islam in 1662 and adopted the name Muhammad Hazer. Radhaballabh married a woman from an upper-class Muslim family, and their son, Ghulam Rabib, became the progenitor of the zamindars of Selbaras. Ghulam Rabib's descendant, Ghulam Jilani Choudhury, married Ashrafunnesa Choudhurani in the 1930s, and their son, Ahmad Taufiq Choudhury, joined the Ahmadiyya movement, rising to become the regional leader of Khuddam-ul Ahmadiyya in Sylhet. After migrating to Mymensingh, Ahmad Taufiq became the Ameer of Ahmadiyya Muslim Jamaat Bangladesh post-independence. Muhammad Darvesh, another descendant, had a son named Muhammad Rafi Allabaksh Shirur. The lineage continued through successive generations, with Muhammad Hazer succeeded by his son, Ghulam Rabib, cementing the family's prominence in the region.

VSHORADS or Very Short Range Air Defence System is a man portable air defence system (MANPAD) meant for neutralizing low altitude aerial threats at short ranges. The missile uses solid fuel based dual-thrust rocket motor and is developed by Research Centre Imarat . On 27 September 2022, DRDO conducted two successful launches from Integrated Test Range, Chandipur. To increase mid-air maneuverability, the missile is equiped with miniaturized Reaction Control System (RCS).

Superdelegates, or automatic delegates, are unpledged Democratic National Convention delegates who can support any presidential candidate. They include party leaders, elected officials, and former presidents, comprising 15–20% of total delegates (e.g., 747 of 5,083 in 2016).

In February 2000, The New York Times selected Forest City Ratner Companies as the developer for its 40-story Eighth Avenue tower. Negotiations with the city and state began the following month, with the Times initially offering $75 million for the site and seeking a 66% reduction in real estate taxes. However, the state demanded $125 million, while the city insisted on full tax payments. Critics noted the Times' hypocrisy for opposing corporate tax relief while seeking such benefits. A nonbinding agreement was signed in June 2000, with the Times agreeing to pay $100 million. The company was to occupy half the tower (floors 2–28), while Forest City leased the remaining space. Nearby, other media headquarters like the Hearst Tower and the Conde Nast Building were also under development.

In May 2014, a group of Iranian fans were arrested for creating a tribute video to Pharrell Williams' song "Happy," showing unveiled women and men dancing in Tehran. Police chief Hossein Sajedinia condemned the "vulgar clip" for "hurt[ing] public chastity." Williams responded on Twitter, calling the arrests "beyond sad." President Hassan Rouhani appeared to criticize the arrests, tweeting that "#Happiness is our people's right." Most detainees, including Reihane Taravati, were later released on bail, but in September 2014, seven individuals—three women and four men—were convicted of producing a "vulgar" video and "illicit relations," receiving sentences of six months to one year in prison and 91 lashes, suspended for three years. Amnesty International condemned the verdicts as an attack on freedom of expression. (137 words)

DCPA is released directly into the environment during its use as a herbicide. DCPA exists in both the vapor and particulate phases when exposed to the air. In the vapor phase, DCPA should react slowly with hydroxyl radicals with an estimated half-life of 36 days. Particulate-phase DCPA may be physically removed from air by wet and dry deposition. With a high Koc of 3900, DCPA is presumably immobile in soil, and thus may strongly attach to inorganic material in soil and other environments.

The treatment of performers in Gulag theater varied by camp and period, with some granted privileges like reduced labor. The earliest Solovetsky theater group, organized in 1923 by prisoners including Serhii Armanov and H. I. Nikitin, rehearsed after 12-hour workdays and performed in the Assumption Cathedral. Though still forced laborers, actors like those in Kurbas’ later troupe were occasionally spared harsher duties. In contrast, musicians in Kazimierz Zarod’s camp faced brutal conditions; after passing the gates, they played for guards before returning to grueling labor. Performers’ status depended on camp administrators and shifting Soviet policies.

Kader has been recognized for her distinguished contribution to global peace. She has sought to increase awareness of pressing global issues such as human trafficking and much of her work has been committed towards building peace in the world. In pursuit of these goals, Kader has hosted numerous events, panel discussions, peace conferences, vigils, prayer services and peace marches including International Peace Conferences at the Arab League Headquarters in Cairo, Egypt, Annual UN International Day of Peace celebrations throughout the world and an International Peace Walk in Sharm el-Sheikh, Egypt (December 19, 2005) among others. Through this work, Kader has strived to contribute towards peace-building initiatives by promoting mutual respect, camaraderie and religious tolerance and by condemning violence and hatred.

The **Bañados del Izozog** is a vast wetland in Bolivia's Santa Cruz Department, primarily fed by the **Parapetí River**, which originates in the Andes. Smaller tributaries flow in from the **southwest**. Due to high year-round temperatures, much of the water **evaporates**, but during wet seasons, the marshes overflow into the **San Miguel River**, which connects to the **Madeira River** and eventually the Amazon. The **Quimome River**, a tributary of the Parapetí, flows into the **Izozog marshes**. A significant portion of surface flow and infiltration drains **eastward**, entering the **Paraguay River** catchment in the Río de la Plata Basin.

The Congo Pedicle's acquisition by Katanga secured its control over the mineral-rich southern portion of the Central African Copperbelt, while the Luapula River's course determined the division of the main copper orebody between the Congo and Northern Rhodesia. The loss of the Congolese Copperbelt to Northern Rhodesia stemmed from the British South Africa Company's (BSAC) failure to secure the Garanganza kingdom, where British missionaries like Dan Crawford operated but avoided political involvement, in line with the Plymouth Brethren's policy of neutrality. Msiri's assassination by Omer Bodson of the Congo Free State (CFS) expedition, led by Canadian Captain William Grant Stairs, ended BSAC hopes of claiming the region. Stairs, viewed by some in Northern Rhodesia as a mercenary and traitor, ensured Katanga's dominance, leaving Northern Rhodesia's Luapula Province isolated and underdeveloped compared to Elisabethville's thriving mining hub.

Emlen Tunnell was born in Bryn Mawr, Pennsylvania, though his exact birth year remains unclear. His tombstone, the Social Security Death Index, and the Department of Veterans Affairs BIRLS Death File list March 29, 1924, while official NFL records and early 1960s sources reported March 29, 1922. Tunnell himself listed March 29, 1923, on his 1942 draft card and 1950 World War II benefits application. Other sources cite 1925 as his birth year. The **Battle of Actium** (31 BCE) was the culmination of a power struggle between Octavian and Mark Antony, former allies in the **Second Triumvirate**, renewed in **Tarentum** in 37 BCE. Octavian saw Antony—now allied with **Cleopatra VII**, **Caesarion’s mother**—as a threat to Rome. Antony, married to **Octavia Minor** (Octavian’s sister), abandoned her for Cleopatra, moving to **Alexandria**, where he recognized Caesarion as Caesar’s heir. Octavian and the Senate condemned Antony’s actions as treasonous, portraying him as a puppet of Egypt, which fueled the conflict leading to Actium.

**Etymology and early history**  
Alan is a masculine given name of Celtic origin, derived from the Gaelic *ailin* (little rock). It was introduced to England by Breton followers of William the Conqueror in the 11th century, later spreading to Ireland. In Ireland and Scotland, it is linked to Gaelic forms meaning "fair" or "handsome." The name gained popularity through figures like Alan Fergeant, Count of Brittany.

Rodez offers vibrant cultural events year-round, with summer being particularly lively. The city boasts three notable museums—Soulages, Fenaille, and Denys-Puech—attracting art lovers and tourists. The *Vend's de fête* association organizes musical performances at café and restaurant terraces, while *Rodez Plage* transforms the public garden into a festive hub. The *Night of Museums*, featuring free access and performances, highlights the city's artistic appeal, drawing locals and visitors alike.

On July 20, 2006, the newly formed Indian Motorcycle Company, largely owned by London-based private equity firm Stellican Limited, announced its new home in Kings Mountain, North Carolina. There, it restarted production of the Indian Chief, manufacturing limited numbers of these motorcycles with a focus on exclusivity rather than performance. The "Kings Mountain" models were continuation models based on the series developed in 1999 by the defunct Gilroy operation, which ceased in 2003. The 2009 Indian Chief incorporated a redesigned 105-cubic-inch Powerplus V-twin powertrain with electronic closed-loop sequential port fuel injection, supported by a new charging system for increased capacity.

The miners' Hovel is the workmen’s mess room and the place where miners could dry their wet pit clothes at the end of the day. A fire was kept lit in the hovel and wet clothes left hanging in there overnight were usually dry again by morning. The men could also brew a hot drink in the hovel, eat their 'snap'(lunch usually in a tin or cloth covered bowl) or fry bacon and egg on a shovel over the fire. In some pits with a good boss, a barrel of beer may be provided to take underground.

By 1943 a new All-American Girls Softball League was formed, playing a hybrid form of softball and baseball which never really became baseball until overhand pitching began in 1948. Women's softball was enormously popular in the early 1940s, and often drew good crowds. In June 1943, Time magazine estimated there were 40,000 women's softball teams in the U.S., including popular touring clubs such as Barney Ross' Adorables and Slapsie Maxie's Curvaceous Cuties. The new league was conceived by chewing gum magnate and Chicago Cubs owner Philip K. Wrigley, who decided in 1942 to start a women's professional softball league, concerned that the 1943 major-league season might be canceled because of World War II. The circuit would eventually shift gears and become the All-American Girls Professional Baseball League, and was dissolved at the end of the 1954 season. About 500 women attended the initial call. Of these, 280 were invited to the final try-outs at Wrigley Field in Chicago, where 60 of them were chosen to become the first women to ever play professional baseball. The 60 players were placed on the rosters of four fifteen-player teams: the Rockford Peaches, the South Bend Blue Sox, the Racine Belles and the Kenosha Comets.

After the war, Andrée Geulen became deeply involved with the Jewish community, working at the Belgian Ministry for Repatriation and the Aid for Israelite Victims of the War (AIVG), later the Jewish Social Service. She helped reunite hidden children with surviving families, maintained lifelong bonds with many, and advocated for their rights. The AIVG centralized postwar Jewish aid, providing shelter, legal assistance, and reparations. Geulen also fought against racism and antisemitism. In 1948, she married Charles Herscovici, a Jewish Holocaust survivor whose family perished at Auschwitz, while he and his brother had escaped to Switzerland.

The **New Look** was Dwight D. Eisenhower's first national security policy, unveiled in 1953. Reflecting concerns about balancing Cold War military commitments with fiscal responsibility, the policy shifted focus from conventional forces to **strategic nuclear weapons**, emphasizing deterrence through the **nuclear triad** (bombers, intercontinental ballistic missiles, and submarine-launched missiles). The New Look aimed to counter Soviet threats asymmetrically by threatening **massive retaliation**, while reducing defense costs. Eisenhower's strategy prioritized winning a nuclear war if necessary, as outlined in NSC 5810/1, asserting the U.S. would retaliate decisively and prevail. The policy relied on **strategic bombing** and nuclear superiority to deter both conventional and nuclear aggression from the Soviet bloc.

Dresselhaus studied physics at University of California, Berkeley, receiving his bachelor's degree in 1951 and his doctorate in 1955. The primary goal of mallet finger treatment is to restore finger function by allowing the tendon or bone to heal. Correa lawrenceana var. genoensis is a shrub that typically grows to a height of 2 m (6 ft 7 in) and has egg-shaped leaves 24–65 mm (0.94–2.56 in) long, 7–34 mm (0.28–1.34 in) wide and more or less glabrous on the lower surface. The flowers are usually borne singly, sometimes in groups of up to seven, in leaf axils on stalks 7–25 mm (0.28–0.98 in) long with thread-like bracteoles. The calyx is urn-shaped, 9–12 mm (0.35–0.47 in) long and glabrous, and the corolla is narrow cylindrical, 17–25 mm (0.67–0.98 in) long and yellowish green. Flowering mostly occurs in spring.

On 22 November 1961 at 00:00:00 GMT, SAMOS 4, the first Program 101B satellite, was a US Air Force first-generation photo surveillance satellite, lifted off. Extensive efforts were made to ensure mission success, such as X-ray testing for bad transistors and super-clean propellant tanks. Unfortunately, the launch was another failure, albeit less dramatic than SAMOS 3. The Atlas suffered a guidance malfunction at T+245 seconds into launch that resulted in loss of pitch control, as well as improper booster and sustainer cutoff signals. Booster jettison happened a few seconds early, while the sustainer engine burned to LOX depletion. The booster pitched up about 160° at SECO, leaving the Agena oriented in the wrong direction for orbital insertion, so when its engine fired, it drove SAMOS 4 into the Pacific Ocean instead of orbit.

The wetland of the Bañados del Izozog in the Bolivian department of Santa Cruz receives most of its water from the Parapetí River, but also has other smaller tributaries from a predominantly western direction. Much of the water in the Bañados del Izozog eventually evaporates due to the high year-round temperatures. However, in wet years during the rainy season, the marshes overflow and drain into both the Quimome River which flows into Concepción Lake, and via the Itonomas River into the Amazon River, and a large proportion via surface flow and infiltration takes a southeasterly direction and enters the catchment area of the Paraguay River in the Río de la Plata Basin.

Preston on Stour is a historic village located approximately 3–4 miles south of Stratford-upon-Avon, on the western bank of the River Stour, near the road linking Stratford to Shipston-on-Stour. Little development since the mid-19th century has preserved its character.

The **Lazuline sabrewing** (*Campylopterus falcatus*) is found in Colombia, Ecuador, and Venezuela. Its habitat preference includes forested slopes and feeders in these highland ecosystems.

Before the establishment of his kingdom, he was Governor of Deccan and a commander on behalf of Tughlaq's. On 3 August 1347, the elderly Nazir Uddin Ismail Shah (Ismail Mukh) who had revolted against the Delhi Sultanate, voluntarily stepped down in favour of Bahman Shah, a native of Delhi. His revolt was successful, and he established an independent state on the Deccan within the Delhi Sultanate's southern provinces with its headquarters at Hasanabad (Gulbarga) and all his coins were minted at Hasanabad. The majority of the Bahmanid army was led by convert Muslims of Indian origin. With the support of the influential Chishti Sufi Shaikhs, he was crowned "Alauddin Bahman Shah Sultan – Founder of the Bahmani Dynasty". They bestowed upon him a robe allegedly worn by the Prophet. The extension of the Sufi's notion of spiritual sovereignty lent legitimacy to the planting of the Sultanate's political authority, where the land, people, and produce of the Deccan were merited state protection, no longer available for plunder with impunity. These Sufis legitimized the transplantation of Indo-Muslim rulership from one region in South Asia to another, converting the land of the Bahmanids into being recognized as Dar ul-Islam, while it was previously considered Dar ul-Harb.

The Owatonna Firemen's Hall serves as the headquarters for **Steele County SKYWARN**, a volunteer severe weather spotting organization associated with **Steele County Emergency Management**. In 2004 Risen came upon information about the National Security Agency's surveillance of international communications originating or terminating in the United States, code-named "Stellar Wind". He and Eric Lichtblau, who had obtained similar information, co-wrote a story about "Stellar Wind" just before the 2004 United States presidential election. Risen said the New York Times spiked the story at the request of the White House. The two reporters rewrote and resubmitted the story a number of times after the election but were rejected each time. Risen decided to publish the information in a book along with information about the CIA's Operation Merlin. He warned the paper about the book and suggested it publish the information itself. Bill Keller, Executive editor of the New York Times, and Philip Taubman, Washington Bureau chief, were furious with Risen. Keller and Taubman negotiated with the Bush administration and, after a delay, the paper published much of Risen and Lichtblau's report about "Stellar Wind" in December 2005. Risen's book, titled State of War: The Secret History of the CIA and the Bush Administration, was published in January 2006.

Following the Russian Revolution and subsequent formation of the Soviet Union, the UOLE was initially granted funding by the local Soviet administration. However, the society came under scrutiny from Soviet authorities; one source noted that the society was considered a potential stronghold of counter-revolutionary activity in Yekaterinburg. In the 1920s, several prominent members were arrested, property was seized from the UOLE, and the society was forcibly closed in 1929.

The cornerstone-laying ceremony for the United States Capitol took place on **September 18, 1793**, when **President George Washington** and his entourage crossed the **Potomac River at 10:00 a.m.** They were joined by the **Alexandria Volunteer Artillery** and Masonic lodges from **Virginia, Maryland, Georgetown, and Federal City**. Upon arrival, the procession marched to the Capitol site, where Washington was received by **Joseph Clark**, the Grand Master of Maryland. A **silver plaque**, crafted by **Georgetown silversmith Caleb Bentley**, was handed to Washington, who placed it in the foundation trench before lowering the cornerstone. The plaque honored Washington’s "military valor and prudence" and marked the dedication in the "first year of his second term" and the Masonic year **5,793**. The ceremony included Masonic rites, artillery volleys, and a speech by Clark.

The **Thompson Ice House** is a historic ice harvesting museum located on **Route 129** in **South Bristol, Maine**, near its intersection with **McFarlands Cove Road**. Adjacent to the site is **Thompson's Ice Pond**, a small, shallow **1-acre** reservoir used for ice harvesting. The ice house itself is a **reconstructed** wooden structure with exterior walls made from **salvaged original materials**, insulated with **sawdust** to preserve ice. Large **double doors** on the east and west sides facilitate the movement of ice blocks, while a **channel** on the east side allows floated ice to enter the building for stacking. Originally built in the 19th century, the reconstructed ice house continues its legacy of community ice harvesting, now preserved as a museum and event space.

Together with Julián Palacios, the Padrós brothers (Carlos and Juan), the Giralt brothers (José, Armando, Mario) and Antonio Neyra, Cárdenas was a member of Madrid FC's first-ever complete season, in 1902–03. A solid midfielder, he was nonetheless one of the several players who left Madrid FC at the end of 1903 to form their own club, Español de Madrid, among whom were Antonio Neyra and the Giralt brothers. Cárdenas played an important role in helping Español de Madrid win the 1903–04 Campeonato Regional Centro, hence qualifying to the 1904 Copa del Rey, which ended in controversy as they were unable to participate in the final, meaning Athletic Club were declared the champions.

This rural settlement consists of a scattering of crofts and other houses and a small harbour with a pier. The harbour is no longer used for commercial purposes, although boats operate in the area for small amounts of fishing and trips for tourists to see white-beaked dolphins, minke whales and harbor porpoises. The pier, about 150 yards (140 m) long, has a small bight on the southwestern side, and a rocky beach continues towards the nearby settlement of Ham. The hamlet also contains a Baptist church, Skarfskerry Point, a double-headed point 30 feet (9 m) in height, marks the eastern entrance point of Brough Bay. The Loch of Mey lies just to the southeast, which also features in a series of children's stories, The Loch of Mey Monster, by a local author from 2007 to 2009.

B.A. Van Sise graduated from Fordham University with degrees in Visual Arts and Modern Languages. A Nikon travel photography ambassador, he has contributed photo essays to the *Village Voice*, *New York Times*, and *BuzzFeed*, and his work is held in the Smithsonian's National Portrait Gallery.

The casemate came under fire in January 1945, when the Hatten area was the scene of fighting between American and German forces during the German Operation Nordwind offensive. German forces included the 25th Panzergrenadier Division and the 21st Panzer Division. American forces, principally the US 14th Armored Division, retreated south to the casemate line and dug in among the casemates. Fighting around Hatten began on 8 January and continued until 21 January, when the Americans pulled back to Haguenau. Casualties among the 14th Armored included 104 dead, 112 missing and 899 wounded. and 83 casualties among the inhabitants of Hatten. The Esch casemate was heavily damaged in the fighting. The area was recaptured by the Tabors Marocains of the French First Army on 18 March 1945.

In 2007 Coe was appointed as vice President of the International Association of Athletics Federations (IAAF) and was reappointed in 2011. When Lamine Diack president of the IAAF announced that he was standing down in 2013 seemed likely to announce Coe as his successor as there had never been an election for the President position. Coe, in November 2014 announced that he would stand for election for this position in 2015. In December 2014, Coe unveiled his manifesto, ‘Growing Athletics in a New Age.' On 19 August 2015, in Beijing, he was elected president of IAAF against Sergey Bubka, by 115 votes to 92 votes.

There is little scientific evidence to guide the care of the ear after tubes have been inserted. A single, randomized trial found statistical benefit to using ear protective devices when swimming although the size of the benefit was quite small. In the absence of strong evidence, general opinion has been against the use of ear protection devices. However, protection such as cotton covered with petroleum jelly, ear plugs, or ear putty is recommended for swimming in dirty water (lakes, rivers, oceans, or non-chlorinated pools) to prevent ear infections. For bathing, shampooing, or surface-water swimming in chlorinated pools, no ear protection is recommended.
Shortly after his professional career, Wayne Lee coached the Hamilton Hurricanes in the CJFL, leading them to an undefeated 1990 season. He later taught gym at Vankleek Hill Collegiate Institute.

The concept of **Hindu Rashtra** (Hindu nation) emerged amid rising Hindu consciousness during the colonial period, as Hindus—forming about 80% of India's population by the 20th century—played a central role in the nationalist movement. Modern Hindu thinkers sought to unify Hindu society and assert cultural dominance. In 1925, **Dr. Keshav Baliram Hedgewar** founded the **Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh (RSS)** in Maharashtra, initially to counter Hindu-Muslim tensions. The RSS grew into a vast right-wing Hindu nationalist organization, with over 585,000 members and 57,000 branches by 2020, shaping India's socio-political landscape.

The idea behind Il Divo came when Simon Cowell, inspired after listening to the Three Tenors, decided to form a multinational quartet of talented young singers to recreate the quality of Luciano Pavarotti, José Carreras, and Placido Domingo. Cowell searched worldwide for young singers who were willing to embark on the project from January 21, 2001, to December 16, 2003, when Miller was signed. Prior to Il Divo, each member enjoyed moderate success individually.

The Newcastle Arena opened in 1995 with the Newcastle Comets as its resident basketball team, relocated from Sunderland where they were known as the Sunderland Scorpions. In 1996, Sir John Hall bought the Comets as part of his Sporting Club project, renaming them the Newcastle Eagles for the 1996–97 season to align with the group's bird-themed teams.

In 2007, at age 16, Casey Cizikas was involved in a tragic incident during a youth rugby game in Mississauga, Ontario, where he tackled 15-year-old Manny Castillo, lifting and driving him headfirst into the ground. Castillo, a Lorne Park High School student who had suffered a concussion two weeks prior, died two days later from his head injury. A doctor testified that Castillo’s prior concussion likely contributed to the severity of his injury. Cizikas, who described the tackle as a "rugby move," was convicted of manslaughter but not publicly named during the trial due to being a minor. The Castillo family questioned the charges, while Peel Regional Police defended the conviction, stating the act would have been criminal even off the field. Cizikas received probation and community service.

**History**  
Eagle Point Park was conceived in 1907 when Charles Mulford Robinson, a prominent city planner and advocate of the City Beautiful Movement, selected the clifftop site overlooking the Mississippi River during his visit to Dubuque. His 1907 *Report on the Improvement of the City of Dubuque, Iowa* inspired Judge Oliver Shiras, who chaired a citizen committee formed in April 1908. By June 1908, with support from the Dubuque Women's Club, the city acquired 100 acres from A.L. Rhomberg, expanding to 164 acres through later donations. The park opened in May 1909. During the Great Depression, the Works Progress Administration (WPA) funded major renovations, including structures designed by architect Alfred Caldwell using locally quarried limestone, such as shelters, bridges, and the iconic Eagle Point Lodge. A streetcar turnaround was built near the entrance, and public transit once served the park. In 1936, President Franklin D. Roosevelt praised the WPA’s work as a "worthwhile boondoggle" during a campaign visit. The former bus shelter now serves as a picnic area.

Alfred Tennyson would later recall the shocked reaction in Britain when word was received of Byron's death. The Greeks mourned Lord Byron deeply, and he became a hero. The national poet of Greece, Dionysios Solomos, wrote a poem about the unexpected loss, named To the Death of Lord Byron. Βύρων, the Greek form of "Byron", continues in popularity as a masculine name in Greece, and a suburb of Athens is called Vyronas in his honour.

The only source of information about Catharina Margaretha Linck is a court document dated 13 October 1721. It summarizes testimony from her trial alongside her wife and reviews possible punishments for the monarch’s consideration. Scholars characterize Linck as a lesbian, placing her in a context where women adopted male attire for various reasons, including to engage sexually with other women, whether or not the other woman was aware.

The Australians returned to first-class action on 11 July in a two-day match at Grace Road, Leicester that was drawn. The AIF opened with 551 for five declared (Pellew 187, Willis 156* and Collins 121) which was their total at the end of the first day. Leicestershire with 224 and 28 for no wicket held on through the second day. The match was another example of the folly of trying to play first-class cricket over two days only. However, the next one was also a two-day affair and Derbyshire defeated the AIF by 36 runs at the County Ground, Derby. It was a low-scoring game with Derbyshire scoring 181 and 112 while the Australians could only muster 125 and 132 in reply. Derbyshire paceman James Horsley did the damage, taking six for 55 and six for 62.

Basharat Mirbabayeva was born on 5 December 1916 in Verny (now Almaty, Kazakhstan). By the mid-1930s, she worked as a railway radio operator while dedicating her free time to training at an aeroclub under instructor Abdusamat Taimetov, later a famed Soviet pilot. In August 1935, she gained recognition as the first Uzbek woman parachutist after her second jump, documented in *Pravda Vostoka*.

Rowell was introduced to the wilderness at a very young age and completed his first roped climb in Yosemite Valley when he was 16. For the rest of his life, he climbed mountains and explored landscapes. He began taking pictures on excursions into the wild so he could share his experiences with friends and family. After graduating from Berkeley High School in 1958, he stayed in Berkeley to study at the University of California but dropped out to pursue climbing.

The first cutbacks to the Peekskill Lighting and Railroad Company's trolley system occurred in the early 1920s, with a significant service reduction implemented in 1925 when the Oregon line was limited to Saturday-only service (Context 7). The Oregon line ceased operations entirely on an unspecified date, and the remaining track around Peekskill was closed in 1926, ending trolley service. That same year, the company created a new subsidiary, the Peekskill Motor Bus Company, which operated five routes (Context 4) using the old Post Office at Oak Street and College Avenue as a hub (Context 1). The bus routes were later taken over by the Westchester County Department of Transportation, though the exact year is unspecified (Context 5). The transition reflected broader trends of declining trolley use amid rising automobile popularity (Context 9).

**Since 1983**  
Pope John Paul II reformed canonization through the apostolic constitution *Divinus Perfectionis Magister* (25 January 1983), implemented by norms issued on 7 February 1983 by the Congregation for the Causes of Saints. This simplified process, initiated by Pope Paul VI, reduced the adversarial role of the *Promoter of the Faith* (popularly called "Devil’s Advocate"), shifting its focus to oversight rather than opposition. The reforms aimed to streamline procedures while ensuring integrity. Pope Benedict XVI appointed Monsignor Carmelo Pellegrino as Promoter of the Faith in November 2012, succeeding others in this role. The changes sought to make canonization more efficient without compromising rigor.

Crunchyroll acquired international distribution rights, streaming it on October 30, 2020, while WeTV released it in Southeast Asia in 2021 as part of its expanded regional offerings.

Colbert appeared as a correspondent on The Daily Show between 1997 and 2005. During this time, the comedian developed the character that would later form the basis for his Colbert Report persona, one that its creator calls a "fool who has spent a lot of his life playing not the fool". Colbert frequently cites Stone Phillips — whom he describes as having "the greatest neck in journalism" — as a source of inspiration for the character, as well as Geraldo Rivera, "because he's got this great sense of mission... He just thinks he's gonna change the world with this report". As a correspondent, Colbert was regularly pitted against knowledgeable interview subjects, fellow correspondent Steve Carell, or host Jon Stewart in scripted exchanges which typically revealed the character's lack of knowledge of whatever subject he was discussing. Other Daily Show correspondents have since adopted a similar style; former correspondent Rob Corddry recalls that when he and Ed Helms first joined the show's cast in 2002, they "just imitated Stephen Colbert for a year or two".

Sipe's research and his book Sex, Priests and Power are depicted in Tom McCarthy's 2015 film Spotlight as crucial in the Boston Globe's Pulitzer Prize–winning 2002 investigation of predatory priests and the decades-long cover-up of their crimes by the Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Boston. The 1995 book is shown onscreen in its bright-red-covered hardback edition when the investigative team meet the first victim, Phil Saviano, the founder of the New England chapter of SNAP. As a favor to McCarthy, actor Richard Jenkins, who starred in McCarthy's 2007 film The Visitor, performed uncredited as Sipe's voice in three phone calls, each based on real-life conversations with Spotlight reporters, including a conference call that is a turning point in the investigation: Sipe made the metric calculation that 6% of priests are pedophiles, which the investigative team's subsequent research verifies.

The treatment of performers involved in Gulag theater varied widely over time and among the different camps. Solovetsky's earliest theater group was completely voluntary and self-organized, and the actors (all amateurs) were afforded no special privileges. Like other prisoners, they put in full workdays clearing forests, and they had to schedule rehearsals during the few hours they had free. The musicians who comprised the band in Kazimierz Zarod's camp were also treated like non-performers: after the prisoners had passed through the gates on their way to work in the forest, the musicians simply put down their instruments and joined the march at the end of the line.

GT 64 received generally unfavorable reviews from critics, who criticized the game's limited number of tracks. N64 Magazine noted that, while the game had been touted as having 12 tracks, it actually only has three, without considering the mirror variants and the fact that each track offers both a short and a long route. The magazine concluded that GT 64 is inferior to Gran Turismo or GTI Club, but still more enjoyable than Automobili Lamborghini. Nintendo Power highlighted the game's energetic music and sound effects. Next Generation gave the game negative review, calling it as neither arcade nor simulation game. In Japan, Famitsu gave it a score of 23 out of 40.

The Hindu, Buddhist, and Jaina traditions observe many holidays based on lunar cycles in the lunisolar Hindu calendar system. Festivals like Vaisakhi, Pongal, and those linked to Sankranti follow the solar cycle. Lunar-based dates vary significantly on the Gregorian calendar, sometimes by weeks, while solar-based festivals like Pongal (14 January) nearly always fall on the same Gregorian date yearly, varying by just one day in rare exceptions. The winter solstice marks Pongal, celebrating the Sun's return northward into Capricorn. [63][64]

When Epstein was placed in the Security Housing Unit (SHU), the jail informed the Justice Department that he would have a cellmate and that a guard would look into the cell every 30 minutes. These procedures were not followed on the night he died. On August 9, Epstein's cellmate was transferred, and no replacement was brought in. The evening of his death, Epstein met with his lawyers, who described him as "upbeat" before being escorted back to the SHU at 7:49 p.m. by guard Tova Noel. CCTV footage shows that the two guards failed to perform the required institutional count at 10:00 p.m. and recorded Noel briefly walking by Epstein's cell at 10:30 p.m., the last time the guards entered the tier where his cell was located. Through the night, in violation of the jail's normal procedure, Epstein was not checked every 30 minutes. The two guards assigned to check his cell overnight, Noel and Michael Thomas, fell asleep at their desk for about three hours and later falsified related records. Two cameras in front of Epstein's cell also malfunctioned that night. Another camera had footage that was "unusable".

In 1984, they founded GoodTimes Home Video Corporation (eventually renamed GoodTimes Entertainment) with 25 Public Domain video titles. They expanded into the production and distribution of low-priced fitness videos although its most recognized line of products were a series of low-budget traditionally animated films. Many of its home-video titles (such as Aladdin, Beauty and the Beast, Jungle Book, Hercules, Pocahontas, Cinderella, The Little Mermaid, Sinbad, and Thumbelina) were named similarly or identically to big-budget animated films from other studios (though their plots were sometimes very different), and released close to the theatrical or home-video releases of the other studios. Walt Disney Pictures sued GoodTimes because the packaging of the GoodTimes videotapes closely resembled that of Disney potentially confusing customers into buying a GoodTimes movie when they thought they were buying a Disney movie. As a result of that lawsuit, GoodTimes was required to print its name atop its VHS covers, but it was still allowed to produce animated films.

Many holidays in the Hindu, Buddhist and Jaina traditions are based on the lunar cycles in the lunisolar timekeeping with foundations in the Hindu calendar system. A few holidays, however, are based on the solar cycle, such as the Vaisakhi, Pongal and those associated with Sankranti. The dates of the lunar cycle based festivals vary significantly on the Gregorian calendar and at times by several weeks.The solar cycle based ancient Hindu festivals almost always fall on the same Gregorian date every year and if they vary in an exceptional year, it is by one day.

Prior to 2007, PA 830 ran further west into Washington and Pine Creek Townships. The route began at US 322 near Emerickville. It had an interchange with I-80 at Exit 86 and traveled a winding route heading northeast. At Red Barn Road, it made a right turn and passed the old entrance to the DuBois Regional Airport. It made a sharp right curve at Allens Mills Road where the present routing continues today. The route between US 322 and Red Barn Road is still state-maintained as SR 1830 and the section of Red Barn Road between SR 1830 and the modern-day PA 830 is SR 1013. Parts of the old PA 830 are still drivable between the PA 830 / SR 1830 intersection and Allens Mills Road, but it is not completely passable due to breaks in the pavement and the new parking lot for the airport.

The next step in a hot air balloon flight is unpacking the balloon from its carrying bag, laying it out on the ground, and connecting it to the basket and burner. A fan, often gasoline-powered, is used to blow cold (outside) air into the envelope. The cold air partially inflates the balloon to establish its basic shape before the burner flame is aimed into the mouth heating the air inside. A crew member stationed opposite the mouth, holds a rope (crown line) tied to the apex (crown) of the envelope. Some balloons, AX7 and larger, may have two (or more) crown lines. The "crown-man" role is twofold: one is to prevent the envelope from excessive sway, and two is to prevent the envelope from rising before it is sufficiently buoyant. Once the balloon is upright, pilot and passengers climb into the basket. When the pilot is ready for launch, more heat is directed into the envelope and the balloon lifts off.

The PlayStation 2 remake of *Nights into Dreams*, developed by Sega, was released exclusively in Japan on February 21, 2008. This enhanced version featured upgraded visuals, 16:9 widescreen support, an illustration gallery, and a movie viewer mode. It was sold at a budget price of 3,800 yen, with a premium *Nightopia Dream Pack* bundle (4,800 yen) including a picture book. According to central government guidelines, the basic pension insurance is set to 40% of the insured monthly wage. The disabled person is also eligible to receive temporary and permanent Disability benefits. Temporary benefits are in the amount of 100% of the individual's wage up to one year. There are 10 degrees of disability, with the percentage of the wage that the person receives ranging from 90%(for a first degree disability) to 60% (for a 6th degree disability). In addition to the pension, the person receives a benefit in the amount of 27 months worth of the person's previous wage (for a first degree disability) to 7 months of wages (for a 10th degree disability). The minimum amount of a benefit is determined by the minimum wage in the area.
