Several other early defunct baseball leagues are officially considered major leagues, and their statistics and records are included with those of the two current major leagues. In 1969, the Special Baseball Records Committee of Major League Baseball officially recognized six major leagues: the National League, American League, American Association, Union Association (1884), Players' League (1890), and Federal League (1914–1915). The status of the National Association as a major league has been a point of dispute among baseball researchers; while its statistics are not recognized by Major League Baseball, its statistics are included with those of other major leagues by some baseball reference websites, such as Retrosheet. Some researchers, including Nate Silver, dispute the major-league status of the Union Association by pointing out that franchises came and went and that the St. Louis club was deliberately "stacked"; the St. Louis club was owned by the league's president and it was the only club that was close to major-league caliber. In December 2020, Major League Baseball announced its recognition of seven leagues within Negro league baseball as major leagues, and in 2021, baseball reference website Baseball-Reference.com began to include statistics from those seven leagues into their major-league statistics.

The Community Development Program (CDP) was referred to the Finance and Public Administration References Committee for a Senate inquiry on 22 March 2017, with findings handed down by 14 September 2017. The report concluded that the CDP "cannot and should not continue in its current form," recommending a new program focused on community empowerment rather than a punitive, attendance-based approach. It criticized the CDP's centralized, top-down administration and excessive compliance burdens, advocating instead for local decision-making. The inquiry's 22 recommendations covered reforms such as dedicated Centrelink support, local jobs plans, and consultation with Indigenous communities to address joblessness and administrative inefficiencies.

Her short thriller *Alibi* won **Best Crime/Mystery Short** at the 2016 Manhattan Film Festival, while her NYFA PhD thesis *Morning After Midnight* earned the **Best Student Film** award at the Treasure Coast International Film Festival. Archaeologist Emilio Estrada and co-workers wrote that pottery which was associated with the Valdivia culture of coastal Ecuador and dated to 3000–1500 BCE exhibited similarities to pottery which was produced during the Jōmon period in Japan, arguing that contact between the two cultures might explain the similarities. Chronological and other problems have led most archaeologists to dismiss this idea as implausible. The suggestion has been made that the resemblances (which are not complete) are simply due to the limited number of designs possible when incising clay.

**DNA damage response**  

The DNA damage response (DDR) is a highly coordinated signaling network that detects, signals, and repairs DNA lesions, including double-strand breaks (DSBs). Detection of DSBs involves the MRN complex (Mre11-Rad50-Nbs1), which recruits proximal kinases such as **ATM, ATR, and DNA-PKcs** to damaged DNA sites. Among these, **ATM** is the primary kinase orchestrating global cellular responses to DSBs, activated via autophosphorylation and dimer dissociation. Once recruited, these kinases phosphorylate downstream targets like **H2AX, MDC1, and Chk2**, triggering cell-cycle checkpoints, apoptosis, or direct repair activation. The DDR ensures genomic stability by integrating DSB detection with repair pathways, such as homologous recombination (HR) and non-homologous end joining (NHEJ). Additionally, DDR regulates processes like oxidative stress responses and programmed DSBs in meiosis or V(D)J recombination. Dysregulation of DDR contributes to genomic instability, a hallmark of cancer.

Henry Abbey and John Schoeffel became partners in the theatre business in 1876, Abbey dealing with the artiste management and Schoeffel looking after the business side. They began by hiring the Academy of Music, Buffalo, NY (1852–1956) in 1876–7 with Lotta, and later at Abbey's Park Theatre from 1876 to 1882 (when it burned down). In Boston they rebuilt the Beethoven Hall as the Park Theatre in 1879. They took the lease of Booth's Theatre, New York City, from Dion Boucicault on 1 January 1880 until 1 May 1881. On 8 March 1880 they formed the company of Abbey and Schoeffel.

Sports Center Rožaje (Bosnian/Montenegrin: Sportski centar Rožaje) is a sport center located in Rožaje, Montenegro, where OK Ibar plays its home matches. The center is located on Bandžovo Brdo, next to the main city football stadium. Construction of the complex began in 2006. The director is Rešid Pepić. The 1999 reforms in Ireland restructured health services, particularly in the Eastern Region, where Dublin, Kildare, and Wicklow—home to nearly half the state's population—were served by the Eastern Health Board. The **Health (Eastern Regional Health Authority) Act 1999** dissolved this board, replacing it with the **Eastern Regional Health Authority** and three area health boards. Outside this region, seven health boards remained. Post-reform, Ireland had one regional health authority and ten health boards (three under the authority and seven elsewhere).

The New York Times Building, designed by Renzo Piano in collaboration with FXFOWLE Architects, was developed through a joint venture between The New York Times Company, Forest City Ratner Companies, and ING Real Estate. This 52-story tower (2006) marked Piano’s first New York City project. Gensler designed the interior, while AMEC Construction Management oversaw the core and shell. E-J Electric Installation Company handled the Times’ lower-section fit-out. Thornton-Tomasetti served as structural engineer, with RWDI as wind consultant. Sika supplied sealants, and Banker Steel provided structural steel. Flack + Kurtz designed the MEP systems. The site at 620 Eighth Avenue is owned by a Tishman Speyer-led partnership, which acquired it in a $175 million deal.

Valsartan is an angiotensin II receptor blocker (ARB) that selectively binds to and antagonizes the angiotensin type 1 (AT1) receptor, preventing angiotensin II from inducing vasoconstriction, aldosterone release, and other hypertensive effects. By blocking AT1 receptors, valsartan lowers blood pressure and reduces cardiovascular strain. Unlike ACE inhibitors, which block angiotensin I conversion to angiotensin II and increase bradykinin levels (causing side effects like cough), valsartan provides more complete angiotensin II antagonism by acting downstream, independent of its synthesis pathway, and does not affect bradykinin metabolism.

The 2012–13 NHL lockout began when the Collective Bargaining Agreement expired on September 15, 2022, prompting the league to impose a work stoppage. Prior to the expiry, NHL teams, including the Vancouver Canucks, reassigned players to minor and junior leagues. The Canucks sent several players to their AHL affiliate, the Chicago Wolves, including Zack Kassian, Eddie Lack, Chris Tanev, and Kevin Connauton, while first-round pick Brendan Gaunce was assigned to Belleville of the OHL. During the lockout, Canucks management expressed hope for a swift resolution in a message to fans, while also implementing cost-cutting measures, reducing staff pay by 20% and shortening the workweek by one day to avoid layoffs. The lockout marked the third consecutive labor dispute in the NHL.

The consequences of Zurich's rejected U-Bahn project unfolded gradually. On 13 March 1977, the Social Democrats handed in a popular initiative for a 200-million-franc loan, payable over 10 years, to extend the public transport system, specifically mentioning the tram network. The initiative was rejected, with only 50,114 votes (28.5%) in favor. By 1978, the tunnel section below the A1L road was nearly finished. Of the approved 200 million francs, 13.2 million were earmarked for a new tramline from Milchbuck through the tunnel to Schwamendingen. The plan was approved in a municipal referendum on 24 September 1978, with 63% support. The 1.5 km Milchbuck–Schwamendingen tunnel, opened on 1 February 1986, serves three underground stations—Milchbuck, Schwanendingerplatz, and Saatlen—where trams 7 and 9 operate. This partial realization of underground infrastructure reflected compromises after the U-Bahn's 1973 rejection.

Biotin-rich foods and supplements may help strengthen brittle fingernails. A few small studies support biotin supplement use to that effect. One study in 35 people with brittle fingernails found that 2.5 mg of biotin per day for six weeks to seven months improved symptoms in 63% of participants.

Wilson was born in Grapeland, Texas in 1921, learning to play guitar and harmonica at an early age. Acquiring his first steel guitar sometime between the age of 12 and 18, Wilson performed at various Houston clubs. He served in the United States Army and became a private first class. After serving in the Army, Wilson decided to pursue a musical career.

In his junior season at San Leandro High School, Cunningham averaged 17.8 points, 4.5 rebounds and 3.3 assists per game. As a senior, He averaged 20.4 points, 6.3 rebounds and 2.6 assists per game. He earned academic All-America honors his senior year.

Tropical Storm George originated on September 27 from a strong tropical wave northeast of the Lesser Antilles and southeast of Bermuda. A large system, it moved to the northwest and later west-northwest. George began strengthening on September 30 as it decreased its forward speed. The next day, while remaining nearly stationary south of Bermuda, a nearby ship reported that George had reached hurricane status. It continued moving very slowly, passing only 100 mi (160 km) south of Bermuda. The island experienced winds of 30 to 40 mph (48 to 64 km/h). Aside from rainbands, little impact was reported on Bermuda.

The Taiwan Provincial Government was established by the ROC in 1945, with Chen Yi as its first Chief Executive. His rule saw the 1947 February 28 Incident uprising. Chen Yi was recalled after the crackdown, and the position was reformed under Wei Tao-ming.

At the 2000 census, there were 5,676 people, 2,364 households and 1,302 families residing in the city. The population density was 947.0 per square mile (365.9/km2). There were 2,543 housing units at an average density of 424.3 per square mile (163.9/km2). The racial makeup of the city was 96.26% White, 0.33% Black or African American, 0.86% Native American, 0.25% Asian, 0.02% Pacific Islander, 1.39% from other races, and 0.88% from two or more races. 3.42% of the population were Hispanic or Latino of any race.

M. Cristina Marchetti began her academic career as an assistant professor at Syracuse University in 1988, becoming a full professor later. She left Syracuse in 2018 after 30 years to join UC Santa Barbara as a Distinguished Professor. From 2016 to 2021, she served as co lead-editor of *Physical Review X* (PRX), a journal run by the American Physical Society (APS), alongside another editor. Her leadership roles include chairing APS divisions and editing the *Annual Review of Condensed Matter Physics* since 2017.

In the United States and Canada, many non-Native sports teams have historically used Indigenous names, mascots, and imagery, claiming to "honor" Native peoples—a practice widely criticized as cultural appropriation. Cornel Pewewardy, a Comanche-Kiowa scholar, professor emeritus of Indigenous Nations Studies at Portland State University, and vice-chair of the Comanche Nation, argues that such mascots exemplify *dysconscious racism*—normalizing racist stereotypes and numbing audiences to their harm. He asserts that these caricatures perpetuate cultural violence, maintaining power imbalances by reducing Indigenous identities to commodified symbols. Invented media representations distort authentic Indigenous experiences, reinforcing colonial dynamics of control and dehumanization. The practice upholds systemic racism while trivializing Indigenous cultures, undermining efforts toward equality and decolonization.

Ruth Cameron, initially an actress, performed in theaters across North America and Europe before marrying jazz bassist Charlie Haden. She then managed his career while transitioning into music production. In 2005, she co-produced *Land of the Sun*, which won the Grammy for Best Latin Jazz Album.

The Colorado Rockies acquired Drew Pomeranz, Alex White, Joe Gardner, and Matt McBride from the Cleveland Indians on July 31, 2011, in exchange for ace Ubaldo Jiménez. Pomeranz made his MLB debut on September 11, 2011, pitching five shutout innings against the Cincinnati Reds for his first win. In four September starts, he went 1-1 with a 5.40 ERA.

Summoning's musical style has evolved significantly since their 1995 debut album *Lugburz*, which featured raw, fast-paced black metal with raspy guitars, live drums, and high-pitched screamed vocals, alongside a dark, gloomy atmosphere influenced by Austrian bands like Abigor. However, their sound transformed with *Minas Morgul* (1995), marking a turning point toward a more epic, atmospheric style characterized by layered synthesizers, programmed drums with heavy reverb, and slower tempos—a signature approach they maintained in subsequent decades. Their music blends black metal with medieval and fantasy-inspired soundscapes, creating a unique, cinematic quality. While early works used live drumming, Summoning later relied on programmed drums to enhance their atmospheric depth. Lyrically, the band draws heavily from J.R.R. Tolkien's works, particularly the darker realms of Middle-earth like Mordor, with most lyrics directly sourced or inspired by his writings. This Tolkien-centric theme, combined with their distinct fusion of black metal and orchestral elements, has cemented Summoning's reputation as pioneers of epic atmospheric black metal.

The voting pattern in NA-54 shows a division of votes between two parties/candidates, with other candidates playing a minor role in the elections. In 2002, the top two contenders received 72% of the polls votes, with the winner securing the seat following a close competition with the runner-up. The winner's share increased sharply in 2008 when the top two contenders received 88% of the polled votes. According to the results, the winner secured the seat with a margin of 23.4% votes, showing a clear dominance in the elections. The polls in 2013 also witnessed a close race between the top two candidates who secured 87% of the polled votes. Historical data shows that the second runner-up's share has decreased consistently over the years, indicating a clear two-party race in the constituency. Given the consistent voting pattern, the next polls may witness a similar trend. However, the decrease in the winner's share and the close race in 2013 suggest that an alliance between the runners-up may yield different results in upcoming elections.

In 1967, Kekedo represented PNG at a meeting of the United Nations in New York City, the first woman to do this. K. A. Mathiazhagan was among the five founding leaders ("Aimberum Thalaivargal") of the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam (DMK), formed in 1949 when C. N. Annadurai and others split from the Dravidar Kazhagam (DK). Alongside Annadurai, the group included Kudanthai K.K. Neelamegam, R. Nedunchezhiyan, and N. V. Natarajan. Mathiazhagan played a key role in the DMK's early agitations, including the 1950s *mummunai porattam* (three-pronged struggle). Elected as a DMK candidate from Gobichettipalayam, he served in the Tamil Nadu legislative assembly during the 1960s and 1970s, later representing the AIADMK in subsequent elections.

The relationship between abnormal feeding patterns and language patterns and language performance on the BSID-III at 18–22 months among extremely premature infants was evaluated. 1477 preterm infants born at <26 weeks gestation completed an 18-month neurodevelopmental follow-up assessment including the Receptive and Expressive Language Subscales of the BSID-III. Abnormal feeding behaviors were reported in 193 (13%) of these infants at 18–22 months. It was determined with the help of the BSID-III that at 18 months adjusted age, premature infants with a history of feeding difficulties are more likely to have a language delay.

The **Trujillo regime** began in February 1930 when Dominican President Horacio Vásquez was overthrown in a coup led by General Rafael Trujillo, who had previously been appointed commander of the National Police by Vásquez. Rafael Estrella Ureña became provisional president, while Trujillo and Ureña agreed to a power-sharing arrangement for the May 1930 election, running as president and vice-president, respectively, winning in a fraudulent vote. A supporter of the coup, Rafael Brache, was appointed Secretary of the Interior.

L. David Mech, a prominent wolf researcher, supports regulated hunting, fishing, and trapping as wildlife management tools, drawing criticism from animal protectionists. He advocates for state-led wolf management, arguing it balances ecological and human interests. His personal website lists wildlife conservation among his key interests. In *BioScience* (October 2017, Vol. 67, No. 10), he co-authored a study closing with a notable quote about wolves. His 2012 article in *Biological Conservation* (January issue) questioned the "sanctification" of wolves, warning against unscientific idealization. Environmentalists, including NGOs, have criticized his stance, with one attributing to him a quote accusing them of perpetuating a "billion-dollar enterprise" using "tainted science."

In 2007 under the leadership of General Superior Sister Ann Margaret O'Hara, the Sisters of Providence decided to found a high school in Indianapolis which focuses on low-income, minority students. Providence Cristo Rey HS opened in August 2007 and graduated its first class in 2010. It is part of the Cristo Rey Network of high schools, the original being Cristo Rey Jesuit High School in Chicago. Students are placed with more than 93 corporations to earn part of their tuition. For transport, school busses have more than 50 pickup spots and also convey students to their places of work. The school has a collection of videos presenting the experience of students.

In its original informal form, bandy was played in North America but gradually faded as its elements were absorbed into the emerging sport of ice hockey, which organized in the northeastern U.S. and Canada. Meanwhile, bandy developed as an organized sport in the Midwest, particularly Minnesota, where Scandinavian immigrants introduced it in the late 19th century. Organized bandy arrived in the U.S. by the 1890s, though it remained overshadowed by ice hockey’s growth.

The land that became Belmont was originally used for tobacco manufacturing by the Lorillard family, French Huguenots who moved their snuff operations from Lower Manhattan to the Bronx River in 1792. They expanded their estate, naming it "Belle Mont" (later Belmont), and acquired much of the central Bronx. A street in Belmont is named after them. After relocating to New Jersey in 1870, the city purchased part of their land in 1888 for Bronx Park, while the remaining area was developed into the Belmont neighborhood. The family also donated land for the Bronx Botanical Gardens and St. Barnabas Hospital.

Nordhoff High School's athletic teams, nicknamed the Rangers, were charter members of the Frontier League in 1957. They later joined the Citrus Coast League, established in 2024 under the CIF Southern Section, after departing the Tri-County Athletic Association.

**The Hovel** at Racecourse Colliery served as a cramped but vital shelter for miners, providing a place to rest, dry clothes, and prepare food. Wet pit clothes were hung near the fire, often left overnight to dry, while a white rag might mark a drying line. Miners brewed "Boogre Brew" or similar drinks and fried bacon on shovels over the hearth. 'Snap' referred to their packed lunch, carried in tins like the iconic "Dudley" water container. In better-run pits, a good boss might supply candles or tallow dips for underground work. The Hovel’s smoky, wood-scented air mingled with the lingering smell of sweat and alcohol.

During **World War II**, the **SS Clarksdale Victory** was a **Victory-class cargo ship** operated by the American President Lines, serving as a fast supply carrier. From **April 26 to May 8, 1945**, it supported Allied forces at the **Battle of Okinawa**. On **April 27**, an artillery shell landed **15 yards (45 feet)** from the ship, damaging one lifeboat with shell fragments. The same day, the **SS Canada Victory** was struck by a kamikaze plane, causing an explosion that sank it in **seven minutes**, killing **two Armed Guards** and wounding **twelve**. Three Victory ships, including Canada Victory, were lost to kamikaze attacks.

In July 2014, President Obama nominated Tefft as the United States Ambassador to Russia in Moscow, after receiving Russia's approval. The Senate confirmed Tefft in a voice vote on July 31, 2014. The confirmation followed several attempts as a number of ambassadorial appointments were being held up at the time. Strained relations with Russia over pro-separatist activity in eastern Ukraine, the country's annexation of Crimea, and the alleged shooting down of a commercial airliner, prompted senators to finally approve the nomination. He presented his credentials to President Vladimir Putin on November 19, 2014 and left the position on September 28, 2017.

*Washing Machine* received widespread acclaim from music critics, who praised its adventurous and challenging sound. Stephen Thomas Erlewine of *AllMusic* described it as Sonic Youth's "most fearless exploration since their SST records," highlighting its unpredictable paths and refusal to take "easy choices." He noted that the album "demands attention" and reaffirmed the band's creative vitality. Thurston Moore, in *CMJ New Music Monthly*, downplayed the extended length of the closing track "The Diamond Sea," joking that he'd prefer "every song [to] be 20 minutes long." Meanwhile, *Chicago Reader* critic Peter Margasak hailed *Washing Machine* as a "return to form," with "The Diamond Sea" standing out as the album's "centerpiece." The record was seen as a bold rebound after *Experimental Jet Set, Trash & No Star*, cementing Sonic Youth's reputation for innovation.

After the Civil War in New Orleans, Stewart took his father's surname of Pinchback and became active in the Republican Party. Dorothy Paget's mother was a member of the New York Whitney family, which was one of the most prominent thoroughbred horse racing and breeding families in America. Paget too owned a stable of thoroughbreds as well as the Ballymacoll Stud breeding farm in County Meath, Ireland. Her horses won a total of 1,532 races in both flat and hurdling. She was the British flat racing Champion Owner in 1943, the year her horse Straight Deal won The Derby. She was leading National Hunt owner in 1933–34, 1940–41 and 1951–52. She owned seven Cheltenham Gold Cup winners, Golden Miller five times, 1932–1936 inclusive, Roman Hackle in 1940 and Mont Tremblant in 1952. Her four Champion Hurdle winners were Insurance in 1932 and 1933, Solford in 1940 and Distel in 1946. Golden Miller also provided her with her solitary victory in the Grand National in 1934, still the only occasion any horse has won the two major prizes of British steeplechasing in the same season.

Cuba began sending medical aid to Haiti in 1998 after Hurricane Georges devastated the country, marking the start of a sustained health collaboration. By 2010, over 6,094 Cuban medical personnel had worked in Haiti, performing 207,000 surgeries—including 45,000 vision-restoring operations—and conducting 14.6 million consultations. Cuba also trained 550 Haitian doctors at the Latin American Medical School (ELAM) in Havana, with another 567 studying there as of 2010. This aid significantly improved Haiti's health outcomes: infant and maternal mortality dropped by over 50%, and life expectancy rose from 54 to 61 years between 1999 and 2007. Haitian President René Préval acknowledged Cuba's long-term commitment, stating, "You did not have to wait for an earthquake to help us."

In 1963 she published research on the sensitivity of gut microorganisms to antibiotics. The 1963 publication studied gut isolated microbes of the Death's Head Cockroach, Blaberus caniifer Burmeister, by isolating pure cultures from the cockroach gut and culturing them on trypticase soy agar. Moore later tested the cultures for antimicrobial susceptibility and antibiotic resistance by placing disks containing antibiotics at various concentrations on the plates (see Disk diffusion test). After incubation, Moore considered the bacteria sensitive to the antibiotic if there was a zone of inhibition present around the disk and, as resistant to the antibiotic if there was no zone of inhibition around the disk. Additionally, the study investigated whether antibiotics were bacteriostatic or bactericidal by removing agar from the zones of inhibition and culturing in nutrient broth. If these liquid cultures showed growth, the antibiotic was concluded to be bacteriostatic, and if there was no growth, the antibiotic was concluded to be bactericidal. Her work is considered some of the earliest on microbiomes.

**GoodTimes Entertainment** was founded in 1984 as **GoodTimes Home Video Corporation** by Joseph Cayre and his brothers, initially releasing **25 public-domain films** on VHS to cater to budget-conscious consumers. The company expanded into low-priced fitness videos, such as the *29 Minute Workout* series, and later gained recognition for its **low-budget animated films** produced by studios like Jetlag Productions and Golden Films. Titles like *Aladdin*, *The Little Mermaid*, and *Pinocchio*—often sharing names with Disney films—were among its most notable releases. In **1993**, **The Walt Disney Company sued GoodTimes**, alleging that its VHS packaging misled consumers, but the court ruled in GoodTimes' favor, finding the artwork similarities insufficient to prove confusion.

The verification tools under the **START I Treaty** were designed to ensure compliance and build confidence between the parties by holding them accountable for treaty obligations. The verification regime, the most complex of its time, provided **twelve types of inspections**, including baseline data inspections, suspect site inspections, and reentry vehicle inspections. Data exchanges and declarations became mandatory, covering **exact quantities, technical characteristics, locations, movements, and the status of all offensive nuclear threats**. The **national technical means of verification (NTM)** provision protected **satellites and other information-gathering systems**, which played a critical role in monitoring adherence. Similarly, the **international technical means of verification** provision safeguarded multilateral technical systems specified in other treaties. Cooperative measures, such as displaying items in plain sight, facilitated NTM verification. **On-site inspections (OSI) and Perimeter and Portal Continuous Monitoring (PPCM)** ensured treaty integrity by enabling continuous oversight and regulatory checks. Additionally, parties were required to **exchange telemetry tapes, interpretative data, and acceleration profiles** from ballistic missile tests, with encryption and encapsulation banned to ensure transparency. These measures collectively provided a robust framework for verifying reductions in nuclear arsenals and deterring illicit activities.

During the 1970s and 1980s, Pierre Paulin was invited to decorate prestigious spaces, including the Elysée's private apartments for Pompidou in 1971 and François Mitterrand's office in 1983. In 1979, he launched his own consultancy, working for companies like Calor, Ericsson, Renault, Saviem, Tefal, Thomson, and Airbus, while blending traditional craftsmanship with modernist design.

Midnight in the Garden of Good and Evil was a box office failure, grossing $25.1 million to an estimated $30 million budget. It received mixed reviews. It scores 51% on review aggregator Rotten Tomatoes, based on 37 reviews with an average rating of 6/10. The site's consensus states: "Clint Eastwood's spare directorial style proves an ill fit for this Southern potboiler, which dutifully trudges through its mystery while remaining disinterested in the cultural flourishes that gave its source material its sense of intrigue." On Metacritic, the film has a weighted average score of 57 out of 100, based on 23 critics, indicating "mixed or average reviews". Audiences polled by CinemaScore gave the film an average grade of "C+" on an A+ to F scale.

The shell is very elongate-ovate, deeply umbilicated, light yellow. Its length measures 5.5 mm. The whorls of the protoconch are very deeply immersed. The five whorls of the teleoconch are very slightly rounded in the middle between the sutures, more strongly so near the anterior end and toward the summit. The summit is strongly narrowly tabulate. The periphery of the body whorl is inflated. The base of the shell is very strongly suddenly rounded, widely and deeply umbilicated. The entire surface is marked by numerous fine, closely spaced, spiral striations. The aperture is broadly ovate. The posterior angle is obtuse. The outer lip is thin. The columella is very slender, strongly curved, revolute, and provided with a deep fold a little below its insertion. The parietal wall is glazed with a thin callus.

Purcell helped to defend the town during the Siege of Limerick. At the surrender on 27 October 1651 he was one of the 22 exempted by name from pardon. He hid in the pest house and was arrested there together with the Terence O'Brien, bishop of Emly. At the court-marshal Purcell fell on his knees and begged for his life. At his hanging he was held up by two of Ireton's musqueteers. His head was affixed on St John's Gate, the southern gate of Limerick's Irishtown.

Fernandez always stressed the positive in her life and in her interaction with people. Her most often repeated quote is "¡Arriba, corazones!" ("Hearts, go up!"). Fernández's signature song is the Lito Peña composition "Gracias, Mundo" ("Thank you, World"), which, in a way similar to Louis Armstrong's "What a Wonderful World", depicts the planet in a very optimistic way. In many occasions, particularly at charity telethons, Fernández would be asked to sing the song as a closer, which she would do willingly. Raptor is a family of rocket engines developed by SpaceX exclusively for use in Starship and Super Heavy. Gina Rippon, a prominent neuroscientist and critic of pseudoscientific claims, coined the term **"neurotrash"** to describe the misrepresentation and misuse of brain research, particularly by self-help gurus, relationship counselors, and advocates of single-sex education. She challenges the argument that biological differences between male and female brains explain behavioral gaps, dismissing such claims as **"neurofoolishness"** and emphasizing the role of cultural influences in shaping brain development. Rippon aims to counter these misconceptions by promoting rigorous, evidence-based neuroscience. She accuses "neurotrashers" of cherry-picking data to prop up outdated stereotypes, arguing their flawed science perpetuates the harmful idea that **"biology is destiny."** This, she warns, has dangerous political implications, as it suggests resisting biological differences could be damaging. Rippon cites examples of neurotrash in popular media and education systems, where neuroscience is misused to justify gender disparities. Her work seeks to dismantle these myths, advocating for a more nuanced understanding of brain plasticity and societal influences.

**Nontrinitarianism** encompasses beliefs that accept Christ's pre-existence but reject his full divinity as co-equal with the Father in the Trinity. A notable example is **Arius**, founder of **Arianism**, who argued the Son was a created being, subordinate to the Father. Thomas Aquinas noted Arius viewed the Son as "not eternal or co-eternal" with the Father, maintaining "there was when he was not," denying the Son's eternal existence.

In 1544, Pál Várdai, Archbishop of Esztergom, appointed György Szondy as captain of Drégely Castle, a key Hungarian border fortress. Szondy commanded a small garrison of 146 men, supported by only 4 small cannons and 7 castle guns. Strategically vital for both Hungary and the Ottomans, Drégely guarded northern approaches. Upon learning of an approaching Turkish force in 1552, Szondy fortified the decaying walls and requested reinforcements from Erasmus Teuffel, who sent only 25 soldiers. Despite overwhelming odds, Szondy prepared for a heroic last stand against Ali Pasha’s 10,000-strong Ottoman army.

**Ancient forest uncovered**  
As the Mendenhall Glacier near Juneau, Alaska, retreats, ancient tree stumps and logs—some still rooted and bearing bark—have been exposed since at least 2011. Preserved by glacial ice for over two millennia, these trees, now lying in a gravelly mix of churned-up stone, provide insights into past climates, including precipitation and atmospheric conditions. Scientists determine their ages by analyzing preserved remains, with Cathy Conner reporting the most recent stumps to be 1,200–1,400 years old and the oldest around 2,350 years old. The findings help reconstruct historical climate patterns as the glacier continues to shrink.

The downy birch aphid has a pale green body, lightly dusted with bluish wax particles, membranous wings and long legs. During the spring and summer all adults are females and give birth to live young by parthenogenesis. This aphid is very similar in appearance to its close relative the silver birch aphid (Euceraphis betulae) which lives and feeds exclusively on silver birch trees Betula pendula. At one time they were thought to be the same species but chromosomal differences between the two have been found and they are now considered to be separate species.

The **Aboriginal Arts Board (AAB)** was established in 1973 under Gough Whitlam's government, comprising Aboriginal artists, writers, and performers. On **October 23, 2023**, an **F/A-18D** from **Fliegerstaffel 11** of the Swiss Air Force crashed into the **Lopper rock face near Alpnach, Obwalden**, killing its pilot, **Stiwi Jäger**, and passenger, **German flight surgeon Volker Lang (54)**. On **October 2, 2002**, a bomb exploded in **Zamboanga City**, Philippines, killing **seven people**—all Filipino civilians—and injuring **more than 150**. The blast occurred on a bus along the **EDSA highway** in Quezon City, despite heightened security following earlier attacks. Heather Dale discovered Medievalism at age 18 while studying at the University of Waterloo through the Society for Creative Anachronism. She made her first recordings in 1992 and released her debut album, *The Trial of Lancelot*, in 2000. Its standout track, "Mordred's Lullaby," became her most popular song, amassing over 20 million YouTube views by August 2020. Her music blends Celtic folk with world and rock influences, often drawing on mythology and history.

The Benton County Courthouse's history began in 1837 with a small log courthouse on Bentonville’s square, housing early court sessions. This was replaced in 1841 by a brick structure, later burned in 1862. A three-story Italianate-style courthouse, built in 1872, served until the 1920s, when architect Albert O. "A.O." Clark—who moved from St. Louis to Rogers in 1904—designed its Roman Temple-style successor. Clark, known for his Classic Revival works like the Applegate Drugstore, also designed Bentonville’s new jail and courthouse.

After the October Revolution, Georgy Stepanov continued serving in the Soviet Navy. From March to September 1918, he participated in the Ice Cruise of the Baltic Fleet, a mission to relocate ships from Revel (Tallinn) and Helsinki to Kronstadt to prevent their capture by German forces. From September 1918 to March 1919, he commanded the minelayer *Sheksna*. He then served as chief of staff of the Onega Military Flotilla from March 1919 to March 1920, briefly acting as its commander in early 1920. During this time, he fought against White Finns on Lake Onega and was awarded the Order of the Red Banner for his actions. In May 1920, he became chief of staff of the Black Sea Fleet's active detachment. Temporarily, from June to August 1921, he led the West Black Sea coast defence sector. From August 1921 to December 1924, he headed the Operational Directorate of the Republic's Naval Staff. Later, from December 1924 to October 1925, he served as chief of staff of the Black Sea Fleet.

**Correspondence**  
Constantijn Huygens was a key figure in the *Muiderkring*, an informal circle of intellectuals centered around poet Pieter Corneliszoon Hooft, with whom he engaged in poetic exchanges. In the early 1630s, he corresponded with notable figures like René Descartes, Rembrandt, and Jan Lievens. He befriended the English poet John Donne, translating his works into Dutch. Huygens was deeply affected by the death of his wife, "Sterre," in 1637, which left him unable to write for months. Inspired by Petrarch, he later composed the sonnet *Op de dood van Sterre* ("On the Death of Sterre"), adding it to his unfinished *Dagh-werck*. This poem, along with others like *Trijntje Cornelis*, was published in his 1657 collection *Koren-bloemen*, showcasing his literary brilliance and emotional depth.

The ꞌAreꞌare are an ethnically Melanesian people that had up to 18,000 native speakers as of 1999 which was a steep increase from the 9,000 native speakers that were recorded in 1993 by John Houainamo Naitoro. According to census conducted by the Solomon Islands National Statistics Office, the total population of the islands rose from 285,176 in 1986 to 515,870 people in 2009. However, the ꞌAreꞌare language does not encompass the entirety of the Islands. Rather, most of the speaker population is limited to the southern side of the Malaita island as well as the capital Honiara on the island of Guadalcanal. The speakers live as hunters and agriculturalists. They are traditionally a religious people and although they are mostly a Christian culture now, the ꞌAre still place a large importance on the connection between ancestors and the land around them. Burial grounds are viewed to be closely related both spiritually and in terms of "ancestral power". The culture of the ꞌAreꞌare people is traditionally passed down orally through myths and other stories, which is why it has been difficult to keep record of all aspects of their beliefs and language. However, steps have been taken by the Wairokai Translation Committee to establish a written standard that can later be implemented into the ꞌAreꞌare school curriculum.

The curriculum in the early part of the 20th century included bookkeeping, typing, shorthand, English, and mathematics. The school admitted both men and women.

**1991–1997: RCA Records**  
Aaron Tippin performed his first Nashville nightclub show in 1990, earning him a contract with RCA Records Nashville. His debut single, *"You've Got to Stand for Something"*, released in 1991, conveyed a message of standing up for personal beliefs and became a popular anthem for American soldiers during the Gulf War. The song peaked at No. 6 on the *Billboard* Hot Country Singles & Tracks charts and served as the title track for his debut album, *You've Got to Stand for Something*, released in late 1991. The album was certified gold in the U.S., though its follow-up singles, *"I Wonder How Far It Is Over You"* (No. 40) and *"She Made a Memory Out of Me"* (No. 54), underperformed. Brian Mansfield of *Allmusic* praised the album as "exciting hardcore country" from a blue-collar artist, while Alanna Nash of *Entertainment Weekly* gave it an "A," applauding Tippin's "humor" and "pointed language." This period solidified Tippin's neotraditionalist country style, resonating with the working class.

Moseley Braun was first elected to public office in 1978, when she was elected to the Illinois House of Representatives. She became the first African-American woman to serve as assistant majority leader in that body. As a state representative, she became recognized as a champion for liberal social causes. State Board of Elections, she successfully sued her own party and the state of Illinois on behalf of African-American and Hispanic citizens. When she left the state legislature, her colleagues recognized her in a resolution as "the conscience of the House." St Peter's College has a strong and successful sporting tradition. This tradition started early when in 1939 the college affiliated to the secondary schools' rugby union. Softball also started early at St Peter's and it was recorded that College students were playing in the softball competitions in 1945. In 1981, St Peter's College won the inaugural national Secondary School's Softball Championship and other national championships in 1990, 1999, 2000, 2002 and 2003. It also won the Auckland Softball Premiership every year from 1994 until 2008, and several times since then. One sport promoted in the early days of the college was boxing. The school boxing championships were held annually, usually at the Municipal Hall, Newmarket.

From 1870 to 1899, Venezuela was primarily led by Antonio Guzmán Blanco and Joaquín Crespo. Guzmán Blanco's government was allegedly rife with corruption, as he reportedly stole from the treasury and abused his power. Seeking to transform Venezuela from a "backward and savage country" into a prosperous nation, he admired the United States and France. After clashing with a bishop, he expelled dissenting clergy and seized Catholic Church property. To bolster his legitimacy amid disapproval, he ordered the exhumation and reburial of Simón Bolívar's body in the National Pantheon of Venezuela, despite their opposing views. His rule shaped Venezuela into one of Latin America's most secular states.

In the twelve-step program, the human structure is symbolically represented in three dimensions: physical, mental, and spiritual. The problems addressed manifest in each dimension. The artworks collected for the Führermuseum were originally stored in multiple repositories, including the **Fuhrerbau** in Munich—an administrative office building for Hitler that also served as a way station for paintings acquired for the Linz Museum project. Most purchases were kept in the Fuhrerbau's air raid shelters, under the control of the Nazi Party Chancellery, beyond the reach of plunderers like Alfred Rosenberg's **ERR** or Hermann Göring. Hitler often visited these stored artworks upon arriving in Munich, discussing them at length. Meanwhile, confiscated items were also hidden in remote deposits across **Upper Austria**, such as forests, mountains, and the **salt mines of Alt Aussee**. The ERR alone requisitioned **six estates** for storage, including **Neuschwanstein Castle** in the Bavarian Alps, which held looted French treasures, alongside other sites like the Benedictine monastery on Frauenchiemsee and the Grand Duke of Luxembourg's hunting lodge. DecembeRadio was recorded at Southern Tracks Recording in Atlanta, Georgia from January 26 to February 1, 2006. Whereas the band's previous album of original material, Dangerous, contained many mid-tempo songs and ballads with prominent acoustic guitar, most of the songs on DecembeRadio are propelled by overdriven chords played in unison by the two guitarists. Verses often give way to sections of jangly ("Table" and "Can’t Hide") or ringing ("Greed") guitars punctuated by crunchy power chords. First single "Love Found Me (Love’s Got a Hold)" is the only obvious example of the band's Southern rock influences, sounding particularly like The Black Crowes with its underlying piano, horns and female backing vocals. Acoustic guitars feature prominently in the ballad "Drifter", and also appear in the mid-tempo "Least of These" and "Alright My Friend". Producer Scotty Wilbanks adds color not only with subtle sound and vocal effects, but with his own keyboard playing (which takes center stage in the untitled studio jam after "Least of These").

**Legacy**  
Kenneth Buxton left behind a mission hospital in Burundi, which he designed and built in 1938, later expanding it with a maternity unit and nurse training school. As medical superintendent of London's Mildmay Mission Hospital (1954–1974), he oversaw its reopening in 1988 as Europe’s first dedicated AIDS hospice, pioneering care amid controversy. The hospital also became the first under the NHS to specialize in AIDS treatment. Buxton wrote articles on tropical diseases encountered in Burundi. He chaired the Ruanda Mission’s council (1965–1974) and later led Mildmay’s League of Friends, securing funds to sustain the hospital. His legacy endures in healthcare innovation and missionary service.

Eigenmannia and other weakly electric fish use active electrolocation – they can locate objects by generating an electric field and detecting distortions in the field caused by interference from those objects. Electric fish use their electric organ to create electric fields, and they detect small distortions of these fields using special electroreceptive organs in the skin. All fish with the JAR are wave-discharging fish that emit steady quasi-sinusoidal discharges. For the genus Eigenmannia, frequencies range from 240 to 600 Hz. The EOD frequency is very steady, typically with less than 0.3% variation over a 10-minute time span.

The Orlando Branch Campus of Everglades University is located in a 28,000 square-foot building on Trafalgar Court, in Maitland, Florida. The campus relocated from Altamonte Springs to Maitland on July 7, 2015, and held a ribbon cutting ceremony which Maitland Mayor A. Dale McDonald attended. The facility houses offices, classrooms, a computer lab, a library, faculty lounge and a student lounge. The university expects that due to its proximity to the metropolitan Orlando area that the campus will quickly expand to 1,000 students, making it the largest Everglades University campus. The staff, faculty, and students at the Boca Raton Campus have participated in the Walk to Cure Arthritis, Making Strides Against Breast Cancer Walk, collecting toys for Toys for Tots, and hosted several blood drives with OneBlood.

Sora was designed by Tetsuya Nomura as the protagonist of Kingdom Hearts. He was not originally slated to be the protagonist, as Disney wanted Donald Duck to fill the role, while Square wanted Mickey Mouse. Nomura designed Sora with the concepts of the Disney characters in mind, basing his outfit on Mickey Mouse's trademark white gloves, red shorts and giant yellow shoes. Sora originally wielded a weapon resembling a chainsaw; however, the weapon was not well received by Disney, which led Nomura to redesign the weapon into a Keyblade. He was also designed with lion-like features, which were removed as the staff found them to be similar to those of Final Fantasy IX protagonist Zidane Tribal. The design was further reworked after a talk with the Disney staff, and Nomura finished it after a night's work. Because Sora was a new character in the first Kingdom Hearts, Square added Final Fantasy characters who support the protagonist.

Eastern Kentucky native, Dr. Patrick Angel initially spent most of his career within the Office of Surface Mining Reclamation and Enforcement instructing mining companies on how to fix land that was negatively affected by strip-mining and mountaintop removal mining. These efforts consisted of packing soil tightly together and planting grass, a plant which Angel says was the "only type of plant he trusted to hold the ground in place". In 2002, scientists like Dr. Angel started realizing that Appalachian forests destroyed by strip-mining weren't coming back, even with rejuvenation efforts. In Appalachia, a land mass larger than the state of Delaware should have been covered in trees, yet only held grass. After realizing this was an ecological disaster, Dr. Angel led efforts to plant more than 187 million trees which cover more than 275,000 acres of former mining land in Appalachia. Dr. Kathy Newfont, an Appalachian history professor at the University of Kentucky commented on Dr. Angel's efforts saying "...it was one of the most hopeful things I'd heard about the region in decades,".

On 22 October 2002, Boka Star was seized by Croatian authorities at the port of Rijeka, following a tip-off from American intelligence, on suspicion that the ship was being used for arms smuggling. The ship's cargo, loaded at Bar, Montenegro, included 14 transport containers of chemical pellets, declared as activated carbon and water filters, which was in fact 208,337 kilograms (459,304 lb) of explosives, consisting of nitrocellulose and nitroglycerin. The captain and chief officer of Boka Star were acquitted, but Balić was found guilty in absentia of smuggling arms and issuing forged documents, and sentenced to six years in prison. Following his arrest in Greece and appeal to the Croatian Supreme Court in 2014, he was released from prison and his previous sentence was dismissed as the original judgement was ruled to be invalid. The charges were reduced to those of a customs violation and he is awaiting retrial.

**Murder of Carla Walker**  
Carla Walker, a 17-year-old high school student, was abducted at gunpoint from a parking lot in Fort Worth, Texas, on February 16, 1974, after attending a Valentine's dance at Western Hills High School with her boyfriend, Rodney McCoy. During the attack, McCoy was beaten unconscious. Walker’s body was found three days later in a drainage ditch near Lake Benbrook, approximately 30 minutes from Fort Worth. The autopsy revealed she had been tortured, repeatedly raped, and strangled. The case remained unsolved for 46 years before a suspect was arrested in 2020.

The most common methods for silver nanoparticle synthesis fall under the category of **wet chemistry**, where nucleation occurs within a solution. This process begins when a silver ion complex—typically **AgNO₃ or AgClO₄**—is reduced to colloidal silver in the presence of a reducing agent, such as an aldehyde. As the concentration of dissolved metallic silver ions increases, they bind together to form a stable surface. However, when the cluster is small, the surface is **energetically unfavorable** because the energy lost from creating a new surface outweighs the energy gained by reducing the dissolved particle concentration. The cluster becomes stable only when it reaches a certain size, known as the **critical radius**, at which point the Gibbs energy decreases with growth, making further expansion favorable. Once the nucleus reaches this critical radius, it continues to grow as additional silver atoms diffuse through the solution and attach to its surface. Eventually, when the dissolved concentration of atomic silver decreases sufficiently, nucleation ceases, and any remaining silver is absorbed by diffusion into the growing nanoparticles. This balance between nucleation and growth determines the final size and stability of the nanoparticles.

The men's individual road race debuted at the first modern Olympics in Athens 1896, covering 87km and featuring six cyclists, including Germany's August Goedrich and Britain's Edward Battel, a servant at the British Embassy. Excluded from the next three Games, it returned in 1912 and has been held at every Summer Olympics since. The 1896 race marked the only cycling road event until women's races were introduced in 1984, with the modern format of road races and time trials established in 1996.

The music for the first season of *Human Target* (2010 TV series) was composed by Bear McCreary, a three-time Grammy-nominated and Emmy-winning composer known for *Battlestar Galactica*. **Sixth Impressionist Exhibition of 1881**  
Edgar Degas' sculpture *La Petite Danseuse de Quatorze Ans* (*The Little Dancer of Fourteen Years*) was exhibited in Paris at the Sixth Impressionist Exhibition in 1881. Critics were divided: some praised its modernity, while others condemned its perceived vulgarity. Most reacted with shock, comparing the dancer to a monkey and an Aztec, and describing her as a "flower of precocious depravity" with a face marked by "every vice" and a "profoundly heinous character." The work's unconventional wax figure, dressed in real fabric, sparked controversy, leading Degas to withdraw it after the exhibition. It was not displayed again until after his death.

**Religious exemptions** in Australia's LGBTQ rights framework allow religious bodies to legally discriminate in certain circumstances. When introducing federal LGBTI protections in 2013, the Gillard Government assured religious groups they could maintain exemptions under the *Sex Discrimination Act 1984*, particularly under **Sections 37** (general religious bodies) and **38** (religious educational institutions), which permit discrimination based on sexuality, gender identity, or marital status. For example, religious schools can dismiss LGBTQ staff, as seen in 2017 when Perth teacher **Craig Campbell** was fired by South Coast Baptist College after revealing his same-sex relationship. Ahead of the 2016 election, the Greens pledged to remove these exemptions, while Labor announced in January 2018 it would review them. The Turnbull Government, following the same-sex marriage plebiscite, commissioned the Ruddock review into religious freedoms, a concern later emphasized by Prime Minister **Scott Morrison**, who vowed to strengthen protections without becoming a "culture warrior." These exemptions remain contentious, reflecting tensions between LGBTQ rights and religious freedoms. (185 words)

Simon criticized the notion that natural resources are finite: 'Incredible as it may seem at first, the term "finite" is not only inappropriate but is downright misleading when applied to natural resources, both from the practical and philosophical points of view. As with many important arguments, the finiteness issue is "just semantic." Yes the semantics of resources scarcity muddle public discussion and bring about wrongheaded policy decisions. [...] The quantity of the services we obtain from copper that will ever be available to use should not be considered finite because there is no method (even in principle) of making an appropriate count of it, given the problem of the economic definition of "copper," the possibility of using copper more efficiently, the possibility of creating copper or its economic equivalent from other materials, the possibility of recycling copper, or even obtaining copper from sources beyond planet Earth, and thus the lack of boundaries to the sources from which "copper" might be drawn.'

Ambivalent sexism reflects the duality of hostility towards women and the tendency for women to be rated more positively than men in surveys. Hostile sexism impacts those perceived as nontraditional women who threaten male power, for example, female professionals and intellectuals, feminists, and political lesbians. Conversely, benevolent sexism protects women who are perceived as adhering to traditional gender roles, such as housewives and secretaries. Fiske asserts that these two forms of sexism comprise ambivalence. On the one hand, women are viewed as competent but not warm, while on the other hand, they are viewed as warm but incompetent. In the workplace, nontraditional women tend to suffer from hostile sexism since they are viewed as competitors. As benevolent sexism includes perceived obligations of protection and help, it leads to women being viewed as less worthy of hiring, training, and promoting due to the concern of them being less able to effectively manage both personal and professional. Fiske contends that when addressing bias against women, both demeaning benevolence and dangerous hostility must be account for.

The Leyland cypress was commercialized after nurserymen in the mid-20th century sought a fast-growing conifer for barren, windy, and salty areas like coastal regions. The six original trees, developed by Christopher Leyland at Leighton Hall in 1888, gained attention when a freak tornado blew down one in 1954. The Forestry Commission's research division propagated branches from the fallen tree, leading to hybrids. Commercial nurseries, specializing in conifers, recognized its potential, and the Leyland cypress became a top seller, accounting for 10% of garden centre sales in Britain.

**Soil Burial**  
Accelerated soil burial tests evaluate the biodegradability of materials, such as PLA-based composites, by replicating natural degradation conditions in a controlled, biologically active soil environment. These tests simulate long-term exposure during or after a material's service life, typically lasting up to 84 days (or longer, in some cases). Samples are periodically exposed to air to mimic real-world cycles of wetting and drying. Experimental conditions must reflect realistic soil activity, verified using reference materials like cotton fabric, which degrades within weeks. Temperature (e.g., 29°C) and humidity are carefully controlled, and soil properties (e.g., microbial activity, composition) must be recorded to ensure consistency. These tests help assess durability and degradation rates under extreme conditions.

The 2014 Indianapolis 500 opened with a record 149 consecutive caution-free laps, shattering the previous modern-era record of 66 laps set in 2000. 16S ribosomal RNA is the most common housekeeping genetic marker for classifying and identifying bacterial species, as it is present in all bacterial species, has an identical function in most organisms, and is large enough (~1,500 bp) to capture sufficient variation to distinguish bacteria. The sequence of 16S rRNA consists of highly conserved sequences which alternate with nine windows of "hypervariable regions". This allows universal primers to be used to sequence many species at a time, and provides the possibility of distinguishing bacteria given the variable regions alone. Many papers suggest that 16S rRNA gene sequencing provides genus identification in >90% of cases, but species level identification in approximately ~65 to 83% of cases. The Ribosomal Database Project (RDP) and SILVA databases contain sequence information for rRNA in bacteria, eukarya and archaea.

Ten pound notes were introduced by the Bank of England for the first time in 1759 as a consequence of gold shortages caused by the Seven Years' War. The earliest notes were handwritten, and were issued as needed to individuals. These notes were written on one side only and bore the name of the payee, the date, and the signature of the issuing cashier. With the exception of the Restriction period between 1797 and 1821, when the French Revolutionary Wars and the Napoleonic Wars caused a bullion shortage, these notes could be exchanged in full, or in part, for an equivalent amount of gold when presented at the bank. If redeemed in part, the banknote would be signed to indicate the amount that had been redeemed. From 1853 printed notes replaced handwritten notes, with the declaration "I promise to pay the bearer on demand the sum of ten pounds" replacing the name of the payee. This declaration remains on Bank of England banknotes to this day. A printed signature of one of three cashiers appeared on the printed notes, though this was replaced by the signature of the Chief Cashier from 1870 onwards.

Elective spinal injections should be performed with imaging guidance, such as fluoroscopy or the use of a radiocontrast agent, unless that guidance is contraindicated. Imaging guidance ensures the correct placement of the needle and maximizes the physician's ability to make an accurate diagnosis and administer effective therapy. Without imaging, the risk increases for the injection to be incorrectly placed, and this would in turn lower the therapy's efficacy and increase subsequent risk of need for more treatment. While traditional techniques without image guidance, also known as blind injections, can assure a degree of accuracy using anatomical landmarks, it has been shown in studies that image guidance provides much more reliable localization and accuracy in comparison.

The production and eventual demise of the *Whole Earth Software Catalog* and *Review* are discussed by Fred Turner in *From Counterculture to Cyberculture: Stewart Brand, the Whole Earth Network, and the Rise of Digital Utopianism*. Proposed in 1983 by John Brockman, the *Whole Earth Software Catalog* aimed to "do for computing what the original *Whole Earth Catalog* had done for the counterculture: identify and recommend the best tools as they emerged." The quarterly *Whole Earth Software Review*, announced at the 1984 West Coast Computer Faire in San Francisco, was published as a supplement to the *Catalog*. Both were published by Doubleday and notably avoided traditional advertising, relying instead on curated reviews. The *Catalog* and *Review* mirrored the eclectic, opinionated style of their predecessor, blending how-to guides, product reviews, and cultural analysis with a tone of anarchic libertarianism and snark. The *Review* merged with *CoEvolution Quarterly* in 1985 to form the *Whole Earth Review*, marking the end of its brief but influential run.

Esat Digifone was originally a joint venture between Esat Telecom and Telenor. After the acquisition of Esat Telecom by BT the Telenor share in Esat Digifone was also acquired by BT. Subsequently, the fixed operations, Esat Telecom later became BT Ireland and the demerged mobile operations, rebranded without the Esat name as Digifone, became a part of the newly formed separate company, mmO2 plc. Digifone became O2 Ireland in 2001 as a subsidiary of mmO2 plc following the demerger from BT. O2 Ireland became a subsidiary of Telefónica in 2006, after its parent company O2 in the United Kingdom was purchased. In June 2013, Hutchison Whampoa announced it would acquire the Irish arm of O2 for €780m. O2 was merged into Hutchison Whampoa's subsidiary Three Ireland in March 2015.

Unaegbu was born in Amankwo-Inyi village, Oji River LGA of Enugu State, Nigeria, the first child of five surviving children to Mr. John Ejike Unaegbu and Mrs. Cecilia Enonanma Unaegbu. He attended the Government Secondary School, Minna in 1990, studying sciences and graduated in 1996. He then attended Muhanatu Computer Institute, graduating in 2000 with distinctions in Certificate and Diploma in Computer Data Processing. He then attended the University of Nigeria, Nsukka from 2002 to 2006, graduating with second class in Mass Communication. As a student, Unaegbu served as the Editor of the university's Roar Magazine. He also won faculty and poetry prizes.

Erdinç Tekir was a member of the pan-Turkish Islamic association **Solidarity with Chechnya and the Caucasian Peoples**. During the **Georgian–Abkhazian conflict (1992–1993)**, he joined the **Abkhazian Army**, where he held the rank of **lieutenant** and operated under the code name **"Hacerat."** In **1995**, he attempted to reach Chechnya via **Russia** to participate in the Chechen War but was **arrested and deported to Turkey**. In **1996**, he participated in hijacking the **MV Avrasya** ferry to demand the release of Chechen prisoners. Sentenced to **nearly nine years** in a Turkish security court, he served **3.5 years** before joining the **IHH** as a volunteer.

Yifei Liu portrays Mulan in a live-action adaptation of the 1998 animated film. The character's journey through the live-action movie is mostly the same as the animated film. A phoenix also serves as a guardian to her instead of Mushu.

A second remix by Arthur Baker features on New Order's *Singles* (2005), which includes the original *Brotherhood* (1986) version. The 7-inch version appears on the 2024 remastered reissue. His sister Louisa Morris Rutherfurd married Henry White, a U.S. diplomat, while his niece Margaret Stuyvesant Rutherfurd wed William Kissam Vanderbilt. Lewis Morris Rutherfurd Jr. married Alice Morton, and Winthrop Rutherfurd wed Lucy Mercer, who had a famed affair with Franklin D. Roosevelt but later cared for Winthrop during his illness.

The National Military Reserve was to act as a pool of manpower for the Territorial Force in the event of war and also provide a force of less able men for menial work. **The Holocaust in occupied Poland**  

Following the German invasion of Poland in September 1939, Wyszków’s Jewish population, including Berek Lajcher and his family, was expelled. The Nazis locked around 300 older Jews and Poles in a barn and burned them alive, while another 350 Jews were later shot in the marketplace. The Lajchers fled to Węgrów, where Berek joined the Jewish Council and Aid Committee in summer 1940, organizing a folk kitchen, hospital, and bathhouse to combat typhus. The Węgrów ghetto was closed off in February 1941, when Lajcher wrote letters to Ernst Gramss, the Kreishauptmann, advocating for the Jewish community. In September 1942, the ghetto was liquidated, and Lajcher was deported to Treblinka, where he later perished in the 1943 uprising.

The seat of the archeparchy is the Cathedral of the Immaculate Conception, built in the style of the Hagia Sophia, and located across the street from the Archeparchy's offices. In 1979, it hosted a papal visit by Pope John Paul II, the first time a Roman Pontiff had visited an Eastern Catholic church in the United States. In addition, Ukrainian President Viktor Yushchenko and his wife paid a state visit to the Archeparchy and the Cathedral in 2005.

The **71st (South African) Siege Battery, Royal Garrison Artillery** returned to the line in the **Hulluch sector** in late August 1918 as part of the **Hundred Days Offensive**. A section of the battery established a **silent position** near **Sailly**, while the rest went into action at **Vermelles** on **27 August**, with billets at **Noyelles-les-Vermelles**. Throughout August, the battery maintained pressure to disrupt German reinforcements. At Vermelles, the guns averaged **150 rounds per day**, targeting **German artillery, trenches, and observation posts** when weather permitted. In early September, the silent guns at **Sailly** were moved forward to support advancing infantry.

William W. Arnold, an ornithologist and physician, became an Associate Member of the American Ornithologists' Union in 1888 and joined the Cooper Ornithological Club (later involving members like Harry Harris). In his later years, he advocated for wildlife conservation, establishing a hospital for disabled birds. He frequently gave talks to children, inspiring a young Colorado Springs resident to bring him an injured bird for care.

The plant was built on the location of Framingham Airport, and ground was broken in 1945. The plant cost $12 million ($209,589,649 in 2024 dollars ) and was one of three new plants that year. The Rock and Roll Hall of Fame included the tune on its list of the "500 Songs That Shaped Rock and Roll". Stylistically, the song also contributed to the development of soul music. In addition, the song was a success, influencing rock and roll by demonstrating the commercial success of using content that appeals to white listeners and by the effectiveness of its gospel feel. In late 2004, InterPride selected Jerusalem as the host city for WorldPride 2006, with the Israeli group **Open House** chosen to organize the event. In response, **QUIT! (Queers Undermining Israeli Terrorism)** launched a campaign calling for a boycott of the parade, criticizing its staging in a city divided by a 30-foot concrete wall under occupation. The finale of **Walton's Cello Concerto** in **B minor** (the third of three movements) follows a **theme and four improvisations** structure—two for orchestra and two for solo cello. After the improvisations, the movement reverts to the **melancholic mood of the first movement**, featuring the **cello** playing an elaborate theme in its high register. The opening bars present **lush Romantic harmonies** and a lyrical melody, culminating in a subdued, **adagio** close, though Walton later composed an alternative ending.

The Bernard Ginsburg House is a two-and-one-half-story structure made of red brick and stone. A carved wood arcade atop stone walls runs the width of the front porch. Unique and beautiful statues support the porch roof. The openings for windows and doors have frames and quoins constructed of limestone.

When La Petite Danseuse de Quatorze Ans was shown in Paris at the Sixth Impressionist Exhibition of 1881, it received mixed reviews. Though critics lauded the work as “the only truly modern attempt" at sculpture, the majority were shocked by the piece. They compared the dancer to a monkey and an Aztec, and referred to her as a "flower of precocious depravity" with a face "marked by the hateful promise of every vice" and "bearing the signs of a profoundly heinous character." She looked like a medical specimen, they reported, in part because Degas exhibited the sculpture inside a glass case.

The lead single, *"Encore un soir"*, was released on July 21, 2016, with the album's preorder, and topped charts in France, where it remained No. 1 on Pop-Adulte Radio for three weeks. Clelin Ferrell was born to Cleavester and Faye Ferrell, both military veterans—his father served in Vietnam and his mother in Operation Desert Storm. The youngest of eight siblings, Ferrell attended Benedictine College Preparatory, a private military academy in Richmond, Virginia, where his parents enrolled him for structure and discipline. His father, Cleavester, died of cancer in March 2012 when Ferrell was a freshman; the entire Benedictine junior varsity team attended the funeral. A standout player, Ferrell was a 4-star recruit, ranked No. 5 in Virginia and No. 101 nationally by 247Sports. He committed to Clemson but missed his senior season due to a torn ACL, redshirting his freshman year there.

The genus *Jacksonia* was first formally described by James Edward Smith in 1811, based on an unpublished manuscript by Robert Brown. It was published in *The Cyclopaedia* (Vol. XVIII) by A. Rees. No species were described alongside the genus in this publication. The genus name does not honor George Jackson, a Black Panther activist murdered at age 29 in August 1971, who had no botanical association. The name instead reflects early taxonomic work by Smith and Brown.

Father Dubois landed at Norfolk, Virginia in August, 1791, bearing commendatory letters from the Marquis de Lafayette (whose wife was devout) to James Monroe, Patrick Henry, and members of other distinguished families including the Lees, Randolphs and Beverleys. All received him cordially, even ardent supporters of revolutionary principles like Mr. Monroe, who served as his host until Father Dubois rented a house in Richmond near a major bridge and opened a school to teach French, classics and arithmetic. Virginia had disestablished the Episcopal Church by statute in 1786, and that law also guaranteed freedom of religion, releasing the Commonwealth's small Catholic population from civil restrictions. Patrick Henry helped the priest learn English, and the two priests who alternated holding religious services in the capitol, the Episcopalian John Buchanan and the Presbyterian John Blair, became his friends. At the General Assembly's invitation, Rev. Dubois even once celebrated Mass in the courtroom of the new State House, but for two years mostly celebrated mass in rented rooms or at the homes of the town's few Catholic families.

Lee K. Abbott devoted himself almost exclusively to short stories, occasionally attempting novels. In an interview, when asked why he never completed a novel (year unspecified), he quipped, "I’m allergic to anything longer than 30 pages." He admitted feeling "lost" when writing beyond his usual length. Abbott claimed to know "two things" about story writing: the genius lies in "the voice," and a story succeeds by making readers "believe the unbelievable." His work emphasized sweep and miracle.

Eisenhower delegated the selection of his cabinet to two close associates, Lucius D. Clay and Herbert Brownell Jr. Brownell, a legal aide to Dewey, became attorney general. The office of Secretary of State went to John Foster Dulles, a long-time Republican spokesman on foreign policy who had helped design the United Nations Charter and the Treaty of San Francisco. Dulles would travel nearly 560,000 miles (901,233 km) during his six years in office. Outside of the cabinet, Eisenhower selected Sherman Adams as White House Chief of Staff, and Milton S. Eisenhower, the president's brother and a prominent college administrator, emerged as an important adviser. Eisenhower also elevated the role of the National Security Council, and designated Robert Cutler to serve as the first National Security Advisor.

A Little Bit TV is the fifth incarnation of the A Little Bit... series of television programmes. In 2005 the first series, A Little Bit Country, profiled a well-known performer in the country and Irish music genre. Three years later in 2008 A Little Bit Showband profiled some of the most popular showbands from the 1960s. The following year A Little Bit Funny profiled some of the most popular comedians from yesteryear. In 2011 A Little Bit Eurovision looked at Ireland's close links with the Eurovision Song Contest. The Following year RTÉ Television celebrated its golden jubilee and the fifth incarnation, A Little Bit TV, began profiling some of the most well-known television presenters from fifty years of television in Ireland.

On **October 10, 2002**, the **Boka Star**, a Yugoslav auxiliary ship, was seized by Croatian authorities in the port of **Rijeka** following a tip-off from **American intelligence**. The vessel was suspected of smuggling **rocket fuel components** for Iraqi Scud missiles. The cargo, loaded in **Bar, Montenegro**, included **208 metric tons of gunpowder** disguised as **activated charcoal and water filters**. The captain, **Josip Nikolić**, and chief officer, **Damir Balabušić**, were acquitted, as the court ruled they were unaware of the cargo's contents. **Marko Balić**, the shipowner, was convicted **in absentia** of smuggling arms and forging documents, receiving a **six-year prison sentence** and a **10-year expulsion from Croatia**. Arrested in **Greece** in 2009, Balić's appeal in **2014** led to a retrial, where charges were reduced to **evading customs control** and **illegal possession of explosives**. He remains in custody in **Rijeka**, awaiting final sentencing.

The Lottery also offers Print 'n Play games. As with traditional Lottery games, tickets are printed by the terminal; however as in scratchers, winning status are determined when the ticket is printed (there is no drawing). A $20 tier has recently been added along with a new game: Print 'n Play × (Times) the Money, based on a popular series of scratchers.

He was born in Dundee where he attended school and was educated at the nearby University of St Andrews. Arnold Kirkland rode in the Kentucky Derby at least twice, with his best finish being 3rd in 1948 aboard Escadru. That year, he also won the Peter Pan Stakes on the same horse. In the 1948 Belmont Stakes, Kirkland and Escadru challenged Citation, who held on to win. The 1954 Kentucky Derby was won by Determine. Kirkland's other Derby appearance was in 1954, finishing 11th on Super Devil. His career highlights include victories in major stakes races, showcasing his skill as a jockey.

Chuck Coleman performed in hundreds of airshows across the U.S., showcasing his aerobatic skills in events such as the Las Cruces International Airport airshow. He flew for organizations like McDonnell Aircraft Corporation and Scaled Composites, as well as training actors for *Top Gun: Maverick*. Coleman conducted 140 G-tolerance training flights in an Extra EA-300 to prepare actors Glen Powell, Miles Teller, Monica Barbaro, Jay Ellis, Lewis Pullman, and Danny Ramirez for filming in F/A-18 Super Hornets. The flight training regime was designed by Tom Cruise, aiming to acclimate the actors to extreme maneuvers and forces up to 8 Gs.

Since the age of 18, Al-Arrayedh began writing poetry, with his first set of poems being published in Baghdad in 1931. Since he was a multi-linguist, he translated the works of poets between Persian, Urdu, English, and Arabic. His poems were popular in Iraq, Syria and Egypt; such that the American University of Beirut asked him to deliver lectures on Arab literature, which he had agreed to. The La Salle Green Hills High School (LSGH-HS) is a Catholic secondary school that is divided into the Junior High School (Grades 7–10) and the Senior High School (Grades 11 and 12). Its Junior High School is located within the St. Brother Mutien Marie FSC building aside from the Arts and Technology classrooms which are located at the St. Benilde Gym's mezzanine, and the top floors of the St. Joseph the Worker building and Central House. Its Senior High School is located within the St. Brother Arnold Reche FSC and also houses related facilities. Previously an exclusive all-boys' school, its Senior High School opened to female students in 2020, then eventually to all levels in 2021.

The alliance among Octavian, Mark Antony and Marcus Lepidus, commonly known as the Second Triumvirate, was renewed for a five-year term at Tarentum in 37 BC. However, the triumvirate broke down when Octavian saw Caesarion, the professed son of Julius Caesar and Queen Cleopatra VII of Egypt, as a major threat to his power. This occurred when Mark Antony, the other most influential member of the triumvirate, abandoned his wife, Octavian's sister Octavia Minor. Afterward he moved to Egypt to start a long-term romance with Cleopatra, becoming Caesarion's de facto stepfather. Octavian and the majority of the Roman Senate saw Antony as leading a separatist movement that threatened to break the Roman Republic's unity.

Tracy Reiner was born Tracy Henry on July 7, 1964, in Albuquerque, New Mexico, to actress and director Penny Marshall and former college football player Michael Henry. She is the niece of filmmaker Garry Marshall. After her parents separated, her mother married director Rob Reiner, who adopted Tracy and raised her as his own. Penny Marshall was of Italian, English, German, and Scottish descent. Tracy's adoptive paternal grandparents were actor Carl Reiner and actress Estelle Reiner. Growing up, fifteen of her relatives worked at labs run by the United States Atomic Energy Commission.
