**Background and education**  
Sir William Hart Dyke (1837–1931), the second son of Sir Percyvall Hart Dyke, 6th Baronet, and Elizabeth Wells, was educated at Harrow School and Christ Church, Oxford, graduating with an MA in 1864. Renowned as "one of the best amateur rackets players of his day," he won the Rackets World Championships in 1860 by defeating professional Francis Erwood at London’s Prince’s Club. In 1873, he played a pivotal early lawn tennis match with John Moyer Heathcote and Julian Marshall at Lullingstone Castle. Two years later, as a member of the Marylebone Cricket Club’s sub-committee alongside Heathcote, he helped frame the sport’s first standardized rules.

The musical *9 to 5* had its world premiere out-of-town engagement at the Ahmanson Theatre in Los Angeles. Previews began on September 6, 2008, with the official opening on September 20, running through October 19. It won four Los Angeles Drama Critics Circle Awards, including Musical Score (Dolly Parton for *9 to 5*) and Choreography (Andy Blankenbuehler).

In iOS 10.1.1, users reported unexpected shutdowns when their iPhone battery fell below 30–50%, with some devices turning off abruptly without warning. Apple addressed the issue in iOS 10.2.1, introducing a power management feature that dynamically adjusted performance to prevent shutdowns. The company stated that the update reduced unexpected shutdowns by over 80% on iPhone 6s and over 70% on iPhone 6 models. Additionally, iOS 10.2.1 allowed devices to restart after an unexpected shutdown without needing to be plugged into power. While Apple clarified that the shutdowns were not a safety issue, the update aimed to improve stability for affected users.

According to the duplex theory, ITDs have a greater contribution to the localisation of low frequency sounds (below 1 kHz), while ILDs are used in the localisation of high frequency sound. These approaches can be applied to selective reconstructions of spatialized signals, where spectrotemporal components believed to be dominated by the desired sound source are identified and isolated through the Short-time Fourier transform (STFT). Modern systems typically compute the STFT of the incoming signal from two or more microphones, and estimate the ITD or each spectrotemporal component by comparing the phases of the STFTs. An advantage to this approach is that it may be generalized to more than two microphones, which can improve accuracy in 3 dimensions and remove the front-back localization ambiguity that occurs with only two ears or microphones. Another advantage is that the ITD is relatively strong and easy to obtain without biomimetic instruments such as dummy heads and artificial pinnae, though these may still be used to enhance amplitude disparities. HRTF phase response is mostly linear and listeners are insensitive to the details of the interaural phase spectrum as long as the interaural time delay (ITD) of the combined low-frequency part of the waveform is maintained.

The **Armed Forces of South Ossetia** trace their origins to the **Ossetian Republican Guard**, organized on **15 November 1991** to defend Tskhinvali. A week later, Georgian President **Zviad Gamsakhurdia** ordered over 10,000 soldiers to the region. Initially a **loosely organized guerrilla group**, the Republican Guard participated in the **1991–1992 South Ossetia War**, fielding **2,400 guardsmen** against Georgian forces. On **17 November 1992**, the Supreme Soviet of South Ossetia approved forming a **Ministry of Defence**, with the first **combat units** established in **February 1993**. These units became the foundation of the South Ossetian Army, later formalized as part of the republic's armed forces.

Dudley married Anna Maria Short on June 8, 1821. They had two sons, Charles Wilkins Dudley and William Ambrose Dudley. Their daughter, Anna, married Edward R. Tilford. They resided at the Fairlawn mansion in Lexington, Kentucky. The house is now listed on the National Register of Historic Places listings in Fayette County, Kentucky.

The Isiolo resort city is to be established under a public private partnership at a cost of KSh.18.9 billion/=. In 2012, the Isiolo County Council was asked to set 2,600 ha (6,500 acres) of land aside for establishment of the resort city. The site is located at Kipsing Gap, 20 km (12 mi) west of Isiolo town.

Jo Stafford and Paul Weston first met in the early 1940s while working in the music industry. Weston traveled to New York in 1944 to see Stafford perform at La Martinique. The couple married on February 26, 1952, in a Roman Catholic ceremony conducted by Monsignor John O’Donnell at St. Charles Borromeo Church in North Hollywood. After their wedding, they honeymooned in Europe, combining leisure with Stafford’s performance engagements. They had two children: Timothy Andrew "Tim" Weston, born September 22, 1970, and Amy Weston, born in 1965 (aged 58 in 2023). Tim became a studio musician, arranger, and record producer, mirroring his father’s career, while Amy pursued session and commercial singing, following her mother’s path. Their marriage lasted 44 years until Paul’s death in 1996.

Ray Rice attended Rutgers University from 2005 to 2007 under head coach Greg Schiano. In his freshman year (2005), Rutgers ended a 14-year streak without a winning season. The 2006 team set a school record with 11 wins, finishing No. 12 in both the AP and Coaches Polls—the highest final ranking in program history—and won the inaugural Texas Bowl, its first bowl victory. Rice rushed for 1,794 yards that season, finishing seventh in Heisman Trophy voting.

During World War II, the Soviet government evacuated 26,000 people and numerous industries from the European theatre to Almaty (then Alma-Ata) to consolidate resources and organize the home front. The city hosted over 30 relocated industrial facilities, eight evacuated hospitals, 15 institutes, universities, and technical schools, and around 20 cultural institutions. Motion picture production companies from Leningrad, Kyiv, and Moscow were also moved there. This influx of ethnic Russians and other deported groups drastically altered Almaty's demography, reducing Kazakhs to a minority in their native region until the Soviet Union's collapse.

Anderson was selected by the Phoenix Cardinals in the eighth round of the 1993 NFL Draft. He was cut during training camp and was later signed to the New York Jets practice squad. Anderson made the team in 1994 and caught nine passes for 90 yards. He was waived by the Jets during training camp the following season. Anderson was claimed off waivers by the Cardinals. He spent two seasons with the team before he was cut during training camp in 1997. Anderson finished his NFL career with 16 receptions for 188 yards and one touchdown.

44th (SA) Brigade returned to the line in the Hulluch sector at the end of July. 71st (SA) Siege Bty establishing a section in a silent position at Sailly-Labourse and the rest of the battery going into action on 31 July 4 at Vermelles, with billets at Hersin-Coupigny. During August the pressure was kept up to assist the Allied Hundred Days Offensive that was launched further south on 8 August. The guns at Vermelles averaged 300 rounds per day, mainly on hostile batteries if the weather was clear enough for observation aircraft, otherwise on wire-cutting and gas bombardments. In early September the silent guns at Sailly were brought up to Mazingarbe, and then joined the rest of the battery at Vermelles, but enemy high velocity guns made the billets at Coupigny untenable, and the gunners moved into the wood at Ruitz.

The Aboriginal Arts Board (AAB) was created in 1973. Comprising Aboriginal Australian artists, writers and performers, its purpose was "to stimulate Indigenous Australian arts and lead to the preservation of many art forms almost lost since the settlement of Australia by Europeans". Dick Roughsey was the inaugural head of the board, followed by Yolngu artist and activist Wandjuk Marika. One of its earliest activities was the hosting of a seminar called Aboriginal Arts in Australia at the Australian National University, with around 800 attendees, with the intention of working out how government could best support Aboriginal culture and art in the future.

Masich is an adjunct history faculty member at Carnegie Mellon University teaching American History and Public History courses. His lively lectures on subjects ranging from American Indians to American innovation have entertained and educated audiences from coast to coast. A recognized authority on the preservation and creative interpretation of history, he has been a faculty member of the prestigious Seminar for Historical Administration, an American Association for State and Local History (AASLH) Council member, an American Alliance of Museums (AAM) Accreditation Commissioner (2004-2015), and serves on the National Council for Public History's (NCPH) Editorial Board for The Public Historian. His popular presentations include “Unknown Stories of the Civil War,” “History of Innovation,” “Young George Washington,” and, for professional museum audiences, “Lighten Up!” (humor therapy for directors and other humor impaired museum professionals) and “History Museum Vampires: How to Suck the Life Out of Your Museum.”

Cantley Hall was probably a home from around the 7th or 8th century, owned by a Saxon called Tochi prior to the conquest of England by the Normans in 1066, after which it probably went to the Everingham's or Everingham Ancestors. By 1209 it was known as Kanteleia, and Cauntele in 1246. By 1280 it was in the possession of Robert de Everingham. By the late 15th century the name of Cantley had been established and remained, while the residents of the estate were the Smith family.

The *Boy Meets World* episode **"And Then There Was Shawn"**, directed by **Jeff McCracken** and written by horror enthusiast **Jeff Menell**, parodied slasher films like *Scream* and *Halloween*, while its handheld camera and POV shots were inspired by *The Blair Witch Project*. The network approved the unconventional episode, trusting McCracken’s vision, though the cast was initially nervous about its departure from the show’s typical format. Packed with meta humor, slapstick, and *South Park* references, the episode blended comedy and horror, culminating in a twist where Shawn is revealed as the "killer," symbolizing his struggle with Cory and Topanga’s breakup. The cast found the table read surprising but embraced the chaotic energy, later recalling it as a standout moment in the series.

**Services**  
For over a decade, Khalifa Ezzat has provided community service at London Central Mosque, offering mediation between married couples and counselling. He conducts Islamic marriages (*Nikkah*), divorces (*Talaq*), and conversions, with certificates in English and Arabic. His advisory service includes personal religious matters, such as marital counselling, divorce, and conversion guidance. These services are available to both Muslims and non-Muslims. Additionally, he leads weekly Islamic studies classes and manages religious affairs at the London Central Mosque and Islamic Cultural Centre.

Basrur became the Medical Officer of Health for the East York Health Unit until East York was merged into the city of Toronto in 1998, when she became the first Medical Officer of Health for the new amalgamated city. *The Fosters* was originally conceived by openly gay creators Bradley Bredeweg and Peter Paige, who sought to write a family drama reflecting the "modern American family." Initially considering a story about two gay fathers, they concluded the subject had been explored well in shows like *Modern Family* and *The New Normal*. Instead, they shifted focus to two women raising a family, recognizing a lack of such narratives on television. Bredeweg noted, "There was a kind of a vacuum when it came to stories about women raising families," drawing inspiration from their friends—mothers raising biological, fostered, or adopted children. The foster care element was influenced by Bredeweg's childhood friend, who endured multiple placements before being adopted in high school, as well as Paige's research on LGBT youth in foster care. This blend of personal and observed experiences shaped the series' unique premise.

**Sexism** in the context of ambivalent prejudice reflects contradictory attitudes toward women, combining hostility and benevolence to reinforce traditional gender roles. Hostile sexism targets women perceived as nontraditional—such as feminists, careerists, or single women—by portraying them as threats to male dominance, often subjecting them to harsher treatment than men. In contrast, benevolent sexism "protects" traditional women who conform to subordinate roles, offering paternalistic praise in exchange for compliance. According to Glick and Fiske, these two forms of sexism function as a "carrot and stick" system, sustaining patriarchy by rewarding conformity and punishing defiance. Ambivalent sexism also frames women as warm but incompetent (eliciting pity) and men as competent but cold (eliciting envy). Nontraditional women face hostile sexism particularly in professional or feminist contexts, where they challenge male authority. Fiske contends that addressing bias against women requires confronting both hostile and benevolent sexism, as they work together to maintain gender inequality.

The constitution provides that the King's heirs shall succeed him as head of state. The 71st (South African) Siege Battery handed over its guns to the 73rd (SA) Siege Battery on 17 November 1917, ahead of reinforcing the Battle of Cambrai. The first impound races in NASCAR were implemented during the 2005 season. The concept of the impound race was designed to limit the costs of racing, and thus make it more affordable for all teams. This was to have the effect of leveling the playing field between the small limited budget teams and well financed multi-car teams. Initially these new rules were confusing, limited schedules, and made the events hectic for the teams. Over time NASCAR adjusted, tweaked, and changed the rules so they were less imposing. In 2005 a total of 21 out of the scheduled 36 races were run as impound races. The NASCAR series (Nextel at that time) director John Darby said "Ultimately, all the teams will arrive at the track with a focus on one goal," ... "That goal will be to have the best possible race setup, and hopefully that will enhance the overall competitive element throughout the field." The displeasure of the drivers, owners, and team members forced NASCAR to back pedal from 21 impound races in 2005, to a total of 5 races in 2006.

*Odostomia farallonensis* has a small, ovate-conic shell, typically measuring approximately 3.5 mm in length. The shell is translucent white, with a smooth, rounded protoconch consisting of about 1.5 whorls. The teleoconch contains roughly 3.5 whorls, each moderately convex and separated by a distinct suture. The summit of the shell is slightly tilted, giving it a subtly oblique appearance. The aperture is ovate and slightly effuse anteriorly, with a thin outer lip. The columella bears a single, weak fold, characteristic of the genus, though it may not always be prominently visible at the aperture. The overall shell shape and columellar structure align with the typical features of *Odostomia*, as described by Oldroyd (1927).

The original LP of *Money Jungle* was released in 1963 by United Artists Jazz, featuring Duke Ellington, Charles Mingus, and Max Roach. In 1979, EMI purchased United Artists, transferring control of its catalog to Manhattan, a Capitol subsidiary. Blue Note Records reissued the album on CD in 1987, adding four previously unreleased tracks and rearranging the track order to group alternate takes together. The 2002 Blue Note CD reissue, mastered by Ron McMaster using 24-bit Super Bit Mapping technology, improved sound quality, though the recording retained its distinctive "boxed-in" feel. Despite being reissued under the Blue Note label, *Money Jungle* was originally a United Artists recording and lacks the sonic richness of classic Blue Note or Prestige releases.

Lewis was aboard Sabine when the American Civil War broke out in April 1861. GT 64: Championship Edition received mixed to negative reviews from critics, who criticized its poor handling, lackluster physics, and unstable frame rate. The **Peerage Act 1963** allows hereditary peers to disclaim their titles permanently, but no such provision exists for life peers. The **Coalition Government's 2011 draft proposal** for Lords reform suggested allowing life peers to disclaim their titles by writing to the **Lord Chancellor**, with the peer and their descendants losing all rights to the peerage. However, this proposal did not become law. The **House of Lords Reform Act 2014** enabled peers to resign as sitting members but did not permit them to disclaim their peerages—life peerages remain in effect until death.

The 4th cadet battalion maintained a special honour guard unit that served as the official ceremonial ambassador of the military forces of the republic. It participated in the march on of the school battle banner, the welcoming of the Soviet leadership (the General Secretary, the head of state and head of government) and foreign delegations to Baku, as well public events such as wreath laying ceremonies. In the late 60s, the only ceremonial units in Baku were an army and navy guard, a motorcycle detachment, and a military band. The uniform of the unit was based on that of the 154th Preobrazhensky Independent Commandant's Regiment in Moscow. Its first commander was Major S. Akopdzhanyan. It had participated in the welcoming ceremonies of the leaders of Turkey, Iran, Senegal (Prime Minister Mamadou Dia) and Egypt at Binah Airport (now Heydar Aliyev International Airport). It also took part in the visit of Leonid Brezhnev to Baku in 1982 and ruby jubilee celebrations of Victory Day in 1985. In 1965, the unit had the honor to represent the academy by opening a military parade for the first time.

In 1963, Ruth Ella Moore published her research on the sensitivity of gut microorganisms to antibiotics, focusing on microbes isolated from the gut of the death's head cockroach. She cultured these microbes on trypticase soy agar and assessed their antimicrobial susceptibility using the disk diffusion method. Antibiotic-impregnated disks were placed on the agar plates, and after incubation, Moore measured the zones of inhibition—clear areas where bacterial growth was prevented—to determine sensitivity. Bacteria were considered resistant if no zone formed around the disk. To differentiate between bacteriostatic (growth-inhibiting) and bactericidal (killing) antibiotics, she likely subcultured samples from the inhibition zones; if bacteria regrew, the antibiotic was bacteriostatic, whereas no growth indicated bactericidal effects. This work contributed to understanding antibiotic resistance in gut microbiomes.

*The Marriage (video game)* has been the subject of analysis in discussions about games as art, proceduralist design, and gender representation. Writers have taken interest in its minimalist approach, where Rod Humble reduces marriage to abstract interactions between geometric shapes, prompting scholars to examine how such reduction critiques or reflects marital dynamics. Ian Bogost categorized *The Marriage* as a "proceduralist game," arguing that such works rely on "computational rules to produce their artistic meaning" rather than relying on visual or narrative elements. Dan Griliopoulos considered it a testament to the artistic potential of games, while Bogost compared it to other rule-driven works like his own *A Slow Year*. Theresa Claire Devine developed a methodology using *The Marriage* as a case study to analyze how procedural abstraction can convey complex emotional and relational themes. Critics have debated whether its focus on rules—central to Bogost's proceduralist framework—overshadows other interpretive dimensions, though Bogost himself, in *Unit Operations* (2006), acknowledges the interplay between formal and contextual elements in games.

The Rachel Carson Center (RCC) hosts seminars, workshops, and public events to share research on environment-society interactions. Its outreach includes the **Environment & Society Portal**, an open-access gateway for global audiences, developed with the **Deutsches Museum** for exhibitions. The RCC offers the **Doctoral Program Environment and Society** through **LMU Munich**, focusing on interdisciplinary environmental research. Activities also involve collaborations with institutions like the **Kate Hamburger Kolleg**, fostering academic and public engagement.

The Fosters was originally conceived by openly gay creators Bradley Bredeweg and Peter Paige who wanted to write a drama that reflected the "modern American family". After originally considering a story about two gay fathers, the pair decided the subject of two men raising a family had already been done on television and began to instead consider a story about two women. When asked about the concept of two lesbian mothers raising a blended family, Bredeweg stated, "We realized that there was a kind of a vacuum when it came to stories about women raising families. So we set off in that direction. Many of our own friends are moms raising biological kids. Some of them have fostered and adopted. Suddenly, we realized that we had a story here that hadn't been told on television before." Additionally, certain elements of the series which deal with the foster care system are said to have been inspired by a troubled childhood friend of Bredeweg, who struggled in the foster system before eventually being adopted in her senior year of high school.

The **Raptor engine** is a family of methane-fueled, full-flow staged combustion rocket engines developed by SpaceX for the Starship spacecraft and Super Heavy booster. It is the third engine in history to use this advanced cycle, following two earlier experimental designs. Methane was chosen for its efficiency, clean combustion, and potential for in-situ resource utilization on Mars. The Raptor is the most powerful methane-fueled engine ever flown, producing up to 440,000 lbf of thrust—more than double that of SpaceX's Merlin engines. Its full-flow design enhances power generation and efficiency, making it a key enabler for Starship's fully reusable super-heavy-lift architecture.

The fluorinase catalyses an SN2-type nucleophilic substitution at the C-5' position of SAM, while L-methionine acts as a neutral leaving group. The fluorinase-catalysed reaction is estimated to be between 106 to 1015 times faster than the uncatalysed reaction, a significant rate enhancement. Despite this, the fluorinase is still regarded as a slow enzyme, with a turnover number (kcat) of 0.06 min−1. The high kinetic barrier to reaction is attributed to the strong solvation of fluoride ion in water, resulting in a high activation energy associated with stripping solvating water molecules from aqueous fluoride ion, converting fluoride into a potent nucleophile within the active site.

**Vaccines**  
The yellow fever vaccine, developed in the 1930s and named 17D, is one of the most effective mosquito-borne disease vaccines. A single dose provides lifelong protection for most people, with immunity achieved within 10–30 days for over 99% of recipients. Common reactions include mild headaches, muscle aches, and low-grade fevers, though severe side effects like allergic reactions or organ dysfunction are rare. Individuals aged 60+ face a slightly higher risk of complications. The vaccine is not recommended for infants under 6–9 months, pregnant women, immunocompromised individuals, or those with severe egg allergies. According to the World Health Organization, over 105 million people were vaccinated in West Africa between 2006 and 2015, preventing outbreaks during that period.

Some Catholic saints and theologians have had sometimes conflicting ideas about purgatory beyond those adopted by the Catholic Church, reflecting or contributing to the popular image, which includes the notions of purification by actual fire, in a determined place and for a precise length of time. Paul J. Griffiths notes: "Recent Catholic thought on purgatory typically preserves the essentials of the basic doctrine while also offering second-hand speculative interpretations of these elements". Thus Joseph Ratzinger wrote: "Purgatory is not, as Tertullian thought, some kind of supra-worldly concentration camp where man is forced to undergo punishment in a more or less arbitrary fashion. Rather it is the inwardly necessary process of transformation in which a person becomes capable of Christ, capable of God, and thus capable of unity with the whole communion of saints".

Prior to 2007, Pennsylvania Route 830 (PA 830) began at an interchange with Interstate 80 (I-80) at Exit 86 near Emerickville, running southeast through Sandy Township and DuBois. Its westernmost point was at I-80, where it headed south before making a right turn onto Red Barn Road, passing the old entrance to DuBois Regional Airport. The route then curved sharply right near SR 1830, where the modern alignment continues today. The section between US 322 and Red Barn Road is now designated **SR 1830**, while the stretch of Red Barn Road between SR 1830 and present-day PA 830 remains state-maintained. The old PA 830 segment between the PA 830/SR 1830 intersection and Allens Mills Road is now unmaintained. Originally, PA 830 extended north to Allens Mills before being realigned in 1984.

**Basaltic lava tubes**—caves formed by cooled volcanic lava—have been identified on Mars, offering insulation from extreme surface conditions due to their stable, enclosed structure. These tubes may retain water and secondary minerals absent on the surface, while serpentinization could produce hydrogen, fueling chemosynthetic life. Earth's lava tubes host analogous mineral deposits, often inhabited by microbes, providing insights into potential Martian biosignatures. Their study aids in understanding Mars' habitability, as they preserve clues about ancient environments and potential life-supporting processes.

**SAMOS 4** was the first **Program 101B** satellite, a **US Air Force** first-generation **photo surveillance satellite**, launched on **22 November 1961 at 00:00:00 GMT** from **Point Arguello** aboard an **Atlas-Agena B**. Extensive pre-launch efforts included **X-ray testing for faulty transistors** and **super-clean propellant tanks** to ensure mission success. However, at **T+245 seconds**, the **Atlas first stage suffered a pitch control failure**, causing the booster to **jettison prematurely** and the sustainer engine to burn until **LOX depletion**. The **Agena stage**, now misaligned by **160 degrees**, fired downward, driving **SAMOS 4 into the Pacific Ocean** instead of orbit. The failure, kept **top-secret** under a **DoD executive order**, marked another setback for the **SAMOS program** following the **SAMOS 3** disaster.

Jeffrey Epstein was placed in the Special Housing Unit (SHU) at the Metropolitan Correctional Center (MCC) in New York due to safety concerns and suicide risk. The jail informed the Justice Department that Epstein would have a cellmate and that guards would check his cell every 30 minutes, but these protocols were not followed. On August 9, 2019, his cellmate was transferred in a "routine, prearranged transfer," leaving Epstein alone. Earlier that evening, Epstein met with his legal team, including Reid Weingarten and Martin Weinberg, until 6:30 p.m.; his lawyers later declined to comment on his mental state. At 7:49 p.m., guard Tova Noel escorted Epstein back to his cell. CCTV footage showed guards Michael Thomas and Noel, assigned to monitor Epstein overnight, appearing to sleep and browse the internet at 10:00 p.m. and 10:30 p.m. instead of conducting checks. The two guards falsified logs, claiming they had checked on Epstein every 30 minutes, but they did not inspect his cell again until 6:30 a.m. the next morning, when he was found dead. Additionally, cameras outside Epstein’s cell malfunctioned and failed to record footage due to longstanding system deficiencies.

He was later tested and got the clear and recovered from the medication. The use of PEP drugs was a first in British soap opera as at the time it was relatively new, and helped raise the awareness that there are precautions at lowering the risk of the disease available, post a potential infection.

The SAMOS 3 launch was a complete disaster. SAMOS 3 lifted from PALC 1-1 on 9 September 1961 at 00:00:00 GMT, was a US Air Force first-generation photo surveillance satellite with 7 instruments on board  but the Atlas booster's engines shut down after the rocket had ascended about 29 cm and it fell back onto the pad in an enormous fireball. Postflight investigation found that an umbilical on PALC 1-1 had not detached on time, resulting in the booster being switched from internal to external power, but since the power umbilicals on the pad had already detached, the result was a complete loss of electrical power to all Atlas systems. The accident necessitated improved procedures to umbilical lanyard installation, and modifications to the launcher system to prevent the hold-down arms from releasing the launch vehicle until all umbilicals had detached.

The ꞌAreꞌare people are an ethnic group from southern Malaita Island in the Solomon Islands, part of the Austronesian-speaking cultural tradition. In 1999, there were approximately 17,800 native speakers of the ꞌAreꞌare language, a significant increase from the 8,000–9,000 recorded by ethnologist Hugo Zemp in 1993. The Solomon Islands' population grew from 285,176 in 1986 to 515,870 in 2009, reflecting broader demographic trends. Most ꞌAreꞌare speakers reside in southern Malaita, with smaller communities in northwest South Malaita and eastern Guadalcanal. Traditionally, they practice subsistence farming (taro, yams, sweet potatoes), pig rearing, and fishing. Christianity is now the predominant religion, though ancestral rituals and burial grounds remain culturally significant, serving as links to lineage and tradition. Oral transmission is central to preserving ꞌAreꞌare culture, including cyclic duets and chants, but the lack of a standardized writing system poses challenges for documentation. The Solomon Islands Museum and cultural organizations are working to establish a written standard using the Latin alphabet, aiming to support education and preserve the language. Parts of the Bible were translated between 1957 and 2008, and a dictionary was published in 1970, though spelling inconsistencies persist. Efforts continue to formalize the language for future generations.

"And Then There Was Shawn" was conceived as a parody of then-recent horror films Scream and I Know What You Did Last Summer. Episode director Jeff McCracken stated that the network was originally apprehensive about producing the episode, "They told us, 'You really departed from format, and it could be too scary for our audience.'" The episode used frequent handheld camera and point-of-view shots, inspired by the film Halloween. The episode departed from the show's usual visual format and mimicked the aesthetic style of a film, shooting out of sequence using a single-camera format, and without a studio audience. The reveal of Shawn being the killer is meant to represent, "staring at his jealousy, at his worst self." The writers also inserted numerous references to South Park and Scooby-Doo. The cast enjoyed filming the episode and being able to break character.

Upon his election in March 1800, Pope Pius VII appointed his friend Ercole Consalvi—previously secretary of the conclave that elected him—to the College of Cardinals and as Cardinal Secretary of State. Consalvi traveled to France, where in 1801 he negotiated the Concordat with First Consul Napoleon Bonaparte. The agreement restored the Catholic Church as the majority church of France, declaring it the "religion of the great majority of French citizens," while securing its civil status. However, the Concordat favored state authority, leaving confiscated church lands unrestituted and later prompting Consalvi to condemn the unilateral "Organic Articles."

**Gene Dresselhaus** (November 9, 1929 – September 29, 2021) was an American physicist who studied at the University of California, Berkeley, earning his bachelor's degree in physics in 1951. Under the supervision of condensed-matter physicist Charles Kittel, he conducted pioneering cyclotron resonance experiments on semiconductors and semi-metals. After postdoctoral work at the University of Chicago (1954–1956), he became a junior faculty member at Cornell University (1956–1960) while consulting for Sylvania Electric Products. In 1960, he joined MIT's Lincoln Laboratory, where he and his wife, Millie Dresselhaus, researched graphite and solid-state physics. A theorist known for his work on spin-orbit interactions, Dresselhaus later worked at MIT's Francis Bitter Magnet Laboratory.

The expedition gathered at Plymouth on **September 12, 1585**, under the command of **Sir Francis Drake**, leading a fleet of **21 ships** and around **2,000 men**. **Christopher Carleill** commanded **1,000 soldiers** during the campaign. The 2016 United States presidential election was governed by Article Two of the U.S. Constitution, which requires candidates for president and vice president to be natural-born U.S. citizens, at least 35 years old, and residents of the U.S. for at least 14 years. Candidates typically seek the nomination of a major political party, such as the Democratic or Republican Party, which select their nominees through national conventions. Delegates to these conventions are chosen via primary elections or caucuses, where voters cast ballots for their preferred candidate. Primaries, considered a more inclusive and conventional method, allow registered voters to participate in selecting party nominees. In the general election in November, voters across all states cast ballots for a presidential and vice-presidential ticket, with the winner determined by the Electoral College.

The **All-American Girls Professional Baseball League (AAGPBL)** was formed in 1943 as a hybrid of softball and baseball, initially called the All-American Girls Softball League before adopting its final name. Conceived by **Philip K. Wrigley**, a chewing gum executive and Chicago Cubs owner, the league was created as a wartime contingency to fill ballparks if Major League Baseball was suspended. Women's softball was already highly popular in the early 1940s, with **Time magazine** estimating **40,000 teams** in the U.S. by June 1943, including touring clubs like **Barney Ross' Adorables** and **Slapsie Maxie's Curvaceous Cuties**. Overhand pitching was introduced in **1949**, as the league evolved from underhand softball-style play. The AAGPBL dissolved in **1954**. Wrigley's scouts recruited **280 women** from across the U.S. and Canada for final tryouts at **Wrigley Field**, where **60 players** were selected to form the league's first four teams: the **Rockford Peaches, Kenosha Comets, Racine Belles, and South Bend Blue Sox**.

For elective spinal injections, imaging guidance—such as fluoroscopy, computed tomography (CT), or ultrasound—is strongly recommended to ensure accuracy and safety. These modalities help confirm correct needle placement, document contrast distribution, and avoid complications like inadvertent punctures. Without imaging, incorrect needle placement occurs in up to 53% of cases, reducing therapy efficacy and increasing risks. The traditional "blind" or landmark-guided technique relies on palpating anatomical structures (e.g., spinous processes) and loss-of-resistance, but studies show image guidance significantly improves accuracy over blind methods, which have error rates as high as 50%. Fluoroscopy remains the gold standard, though ultrasound is increasingly used for specific procedures.

The first North Carolina Highway 67 (NC 67) was established as a state route running from the Tennessee state line, where it continued from Tennessee State Route 67 (SR 67), eastward through the Jenkins Creek Valley. The route provided a key connection through rugged terrain in western North Carolina.

Ataxia telangiectasia mutated (ATM) is a kinase that (similar to mTOR) can phosphorylate KAP1 resulting in the switch from viral latency to the lytic cycle. The influence of British Romantic poetry extended notably to Russia, where Robert Burns became celebrated as the "people's poet." During Imperial times, Russian society was sharply divided, with a privileged aristocracy oppressing an impoverished, largely illiterate peasantry—a contrast Burns critiqued in his works. Translated into Russian in the 19th century, Burns symbolized defiance against social inequality, resonating deeply with ordinary Russians. In Soviet Russia, his radical ethos and celebration of the common man aligned with state ideology, leading Samuel Marshak’s 1924 translations to sell over 600,000 copies. The USSR honored Burns with the world’s first commemorative stamp in 1956, and his poetry remains taught in Russian schools alongside national poets like Pushkin. Burns admired the ethos of breaking free from aristocratic pretensions, a theme the Soviet state leveraged to promote socialist ideals. Despite post-communist capitalism, Burns' reputation endures, reflecting his enduring appeal as a voice of the people.

By the mid-1950s, the Gibson Guitar Company had lost considerable market share to rival Fender's Telecaster and Stratocaster models. In response, Gibson developed three radical solid-body guitar concepts to compete. On June 27, 1957, Gibson President Ted McCarty filed designs with the United States Patent Office for the "Flying V," "Explorer," and (unofficially) "Moderne." The patents were issued on January 7, 1958, but the Moderne received an exceedingly poor reception at the 1957 NAMM show, leading Gibson to abandon its production. Only a handful of prototypes were made, with Gibson engineer Rendall Wall later borrowing one for a local performance of *Bye Bye Birdie* in the early 1960s, though he reportedly disliked the instrument. The Moderne's unconventional design and lukewarm response relegated it to near-mythical status, with few confirmed examples ever surfacing.

Within the Commonwealth realms, the sovereign is responsible for rendering justice for all his subjects, and is thus traditionally deemed the fount of justice. In Tuvalu, criminal offences are legally deemed to be offences against the sovereign and proceedings for indictable offences are brought in the sovereign's name in the form of Rex versus [Name] ("Rex" being Latin for "King"). Hence, the common law holds that the sovereign "can do no wrong"; the monarch cannot be prosecuted in his or her own courts for criminal offences.

In 1632, Louis XIII of France - the protector of the famous exiled jurist Hugo Grotius - appointed him as Knight of the Order of Saint-Michel. **Side effects**  
Methedrone, a synthetic cathinone, is reported to produce powerful psychological effects similar to those of stimulants like MDMA, amphetamines, and cocaine, including euphoria, agitation, and hallucinations, with severe psychotic symptoms (e.g., paranoia and tactile hallucinations) observed in some users, particularly those with mental health histories. Physically, it can cause increased heart rate, hypertension, hyperthermia, and seizures in humans. Studies in rats and mice have shown that methedrone rapidly increases serotonin and dopamine levels, while in mice, it enhances nicotine-induced behavioral sensitization and alters anxiety-like and cognitive behaviors. Co-administration with nicotine in mice also elevated oxidative stress in the brain. Legally, methedrone is classified as a controlled substance in many jurisdictions under international conventions, sharing pharmacological properties with banned drugs like methamphetamine, cocaine, and MDMA.

The first cutbacks came in 1924. The trolley system operated at a deficit for months, first reducing service to Oregon and Putnam Valley to Saturday-only in 1925, and ending service on the Oregon line on August 29, 1925. The remaining track around Peekskill was closed and trolley service ended on June 30, 1926. Peekskill Lighting and Railroad Co. created Peekskill Motor Bus Company as a new subsidiary to acquire and operate the transportation franchise in place of trolleys in 1926. Four routes were operated, using the Peekskill train station as a hub. At the time of its abandonment, the trolley lines of Peekskill Lighting and Railroad Co. was the last of the Consolidated Gas Company affiliated utilities to offer common-carrier rail transportation. The bus routes were taken over by Westchester County Department of Transportation and operated as part of the Bee-Line Bus System in 1978.

Cordice Jr., Dr. Emil Naclerio, Aubre C. Maynard, and Farrow Allen were called in to operate. They made incisions and inserted a rib spreader, making King’s aorta visible. Chief of Surgery Aubre de Lambert Maynard then entered and attempted to pull out the letter opener, but cut his glove on the blade; a surgical clamp was finally used to pull out the blade. Cordice mapped out a strategy and successfully saved Dr. King. He was the subject of the book When Harlem Nearly Killed King: The 1958 Stabbing of Dr. Martin Luther King, by Hugh Pearson.

**Original concept and design**  
Sora, the protagonist of *Kingdom Hearts*, was designed by Tetsuya Nomura as an original character after Disney proposed Donald Duck and Square Enix favored Mickey Mouse for the role. Nomura incorporated elements of Mickey Mouse into Sora's design, such as his color scheme, to blend Disney and Square aesthetics. Early concepts depicted Sora with lion-like features, including a tail, but this was scrapped due to similarities with *Final Fantasy IX*'s Zidane. His original weapon, a chainsaw, was replaced with the Keyblade to maintain a kid-friendly tone. Nomura also drew inspiration from his past designs, blending traits from *Final Fantasy* characters like Cloud and Tidus. Disney initially resisted Sora's spiky hair but relented after Nomura's insistence. *Final Fantasy* characters were included in the first game to support the then-unknown original cast and bridge the collaboration's tone.

**Weight-loss**  
Clenbuterol is often used by bodybuilders during "cutting" cycles to burn fat and enhance muscle definition. Celebrities like Victoria Beckham, Britney Spears, and Lindsay Lohan have been publicized for its off-label use as a weight-loss drug, similar to sympathomimetic amines like ephedrine, though clinical testing supporting this use is insufficient. In 2021, Odalis Santos Mena, a 23-year-old fitness influencer and bodybuilder, died of cardiac arrest during an alternative therapy for underarm sweating involving clenbuterol and anesthesia. The coroner cited a drug reaction between anesthesia and her medications as the cause. Clenbuterol remains banned by the FDA for human use and is prohibited in competitive sports.

Despite grieving the death of his wife in 1638, Constantijn Huygens continued his successful career. **Association methods** in protein–protein interaction (PPI) prediction identify sequence-signatures, domain co-occurrence, or conserved binding interfaces to distinguish interacting from non-interacting pairs. A classifier is trained using association rule mining (ARM), where rules link interaction site properties to PPI types (e.g., transient or permanent). These methods evaluate domain interactions using a log-odds score, calculated as \( \log(P_{ij} / (P_i \times P_j)) \), where \( P_{ij} \) is the joint probability of domains interacting, and \( P_i \), \( P_j \) are their individual probabilities. Predicted interactions are characterized by high-scoring domain pairs with evolutionary conservation. A downside is the reliance on sufficient data for meaningful statistical analysis, excluding infrequent domains (e.g., those with fewer than five interactors), limiting coverage for rare interactions.

Donna Alvermann's theoretical framework builds on James Paul Gee's concepts of capital 'D' Discourse and New Literacies, as well as Allan Luke's *Four Resources Model* of literacy. Influenced by Michel Foucault's philosophy on Discourse and Social Constructionism, her work also engages with Paulo Freire's critical pedagogy, which encourages students to challenge social categorization and power structures—ideas further developed by Henry Giroux. **Relation to neoliberalism**  
Green imperialism is sometimes described as a combination of eco-imperialism and neoliberal market logic, where powerful actors impose ecological policies, often commodifying nature—termed "reification"—through carbon accounting systems. This commodification, linked to neoliberalism, can marginalize human needs and perpetuate exploitation.

**Constructivism** is an educational approach that views learners as active knowledge builders, forming understanding through engagement with their environment. In **phenomenon-based learning (PhenBL)**, a constructivist approach, information is perceived as being constructed through problem-solving, where learners piece together fragmented knowledge into a coherent whole suited to the context. This process occurs as learners explore real-world phenomena, building skills and understanding dynamically. When PhenBL takes place in **collaborative settings**, it aligns with **socio-constructivist and sociocultural theories**, which emphasize that knowledge is shaped socially rather than solely within the individual. Central to sociocultural theories are **cultural artifacts**—such as language, mathematical systems, and thinking tools—which allow learners to access shared knowledge without reinventing solutions. By leveraging these artifacts, learners benefit from the accumulated wisdom of their culture, enhancing their learning efficiency and depth.

The wing was organized at the beginning of February 1952 as part of a major reorganization of Air Defense Command (ADC) fighter units responding to ADC's difficulty under the existing wing base organizational structure in deploying fighter squadrons to best advantage. The wing replaced the 33d Fighter-Interceptor Wing (FIW) at Otis Air Force Base, Massachusetts five days later and assumed control of the 33 FIW's operational elements. The wing's 564th Air Base Group assumed support responsibilities for Otis AFB from the inactivating 33d Air Base Group and 33d Maintenance & Supply Groups. The operational squadrons transferred from the 33d FIW were the 58th Fighter-Interceptor Squadron (FIS) and 59th FIS at Otis AFB and the 60th FIS at Westover Air Force Base, Massachusetts. The 58th and 60th FIS flew F-86 Sabre aircraft, while the 59th FIS was equipped with F-94 Starfire aircraft. The wing also was assigned a federalized Air National Guard (ANG) squadron from the 101st FIW, the 133d FIS at Grenier Air Force Base, New Hampshire, flying World War II era F-47 Thunderbolt aircraft. The wing mission was to train and maintain tactical flying units in state of readiness in order to defend the northeastern United States.

The SNP was the only party to oppose the parliamentary bill which authorised a referendum on the UK's membership of the EU a year prior. While the SNP campaigned against European Communities membership in the 1975 referendum, it campaigned for Remain alongside Labour, the Liberal Democrats, the Greens and Plaid Cymru, while the Remain campaign was also supported by the UK Government and Prime Minister David Cameron, despite the Conservative Party remaining officially neutral. The UK Independence Party was the only main party in Great Britain to campaign for Leave. The UK as a whole voted for Leave by 52% on the back of English support, with England voting for Leave by 53%, while Scotland voted overwhelmingly for Remain by 62%, alongside Northern Ireland and Greater London. In its manifesto for the 2016 Scottish Parliament election, launched in April, two months before the EU referendum, the SNP stated that "a significant and material change in the circumstances that prevailed in 2014, such as Scotland being taken out of the EU against our will" would justify another independence referendum. Scotland's desire to remain in the EU and the direction taken by Conservative governments since the referendum have driven the SNP campaign for another independence referendum.

**Speculations and imaginings about purgatory**  

Catholic saints and theologians have long held conflicting ideas about purgatory, particularly regarding its nature as a physical place versus a spiritual process. These debates reflect broader tensions between literal and metaphorical interpretations, contributing to purgatory's ambiguous yet enduring role in Christian thought. The popular image of purgatory often includes notions of a fiery, visitable location where sins are cleansed, though recent Catholic thought, as noted by Paul J. Griffiths, preserves the doctrine's essentials while reimagining it as a transformative condition. Josef Ratzinger (later Pope Benedict XVI) rejected the idea of purgatory as a "supra-worldly concentration camp," describing it instead as an "inwardly necessary process of transformation" that makes a person "capable of Christ, capable of God, and thus capable of unity with the whole communion of saints."

Before establishing the Bahmani Kingdom, Alauddin Bahman Shah (originally named Zafar Khan or Hasan Gangu) served as a Turkic governor of the Deccan under the Delhi Sultanate. On **3 August 1347**, Nazir Uddin Ismail Shah, a rebel leader who had revolted against Muhammad bin Tughlaq’s rule, voluntarily stepped down in favor of Bahman Shah. Bahman Shah, whose origins are debated (claimed as Turkic, Afghan, or Persian), then founded the independent Bahmani Sultanate, with its headquarters at **Ahsanabad (Gulbarga)**. The majority of the Bahmanid army was led by **Turkic commanders**. In Tuvalu, the sovereign—head of state of the Commonwealth realms—is traditionally deemed the fount of justice, with criminal offences considered violations against the Crown. Indictable proceedings are brought in the name of *Rex* (Latin for "King") or *Regina*. Common law holds that the sovereign "can do no wrong," granting immunity from prosecution in their own courts. While the monarch embodies justice symbolically, Tuvalu's independent judiciary adjudicates cases, reflecting the broader Commonwealth principle of sovereign equality. The sovereign’s role remains ceremonial, with legal authority exercised by courts in the monarch’s name.

Topock was originally called Mellen, a railroad station and steamboat landing, at the site where the Atlantic and Pacific Railroad built the Red Rock Bridge, a steel cantilever bridge across the Colorado River in May 1890 after three of its earlier less well-built bridges had been washed away by the river upstream at a poorly chosen site at Eastbridge, southeast of Needles, California. The town was named for Captain "Jack" Mellon, a 40-year veteran Colorado River steamboat captain and an owner of the Colorado Steam Navigation Company, though it was misspelled as "Mellen". **Reactivity**  
Fluorinase catalyzes the nucleophilic substitution of fluoride ion (F⁻) at the C-5' position of *S*-adenosyl-L-methionine (SAM), forming 5'-fluoro-5'-deoxyadenosine (FDA) and releasing L-methionine (L-Met). Despite accelerating the reaction significantly compared to the uncatalyzed process, fluorinase is considered slow, with a turnover number (*k*<sub>cat</sub>) of up to 0.4 min⁻¹. The high kinetic barrier arises from the desolvation and activation of the fluoride ion, which must overcome strong hydration energy to react. Within the active site, the fluoride ion undergoes desolvation and precise positioning, enabling the C-F bond formation under neutral aqueous conditions—a rare feat in biocatalysis.

Trout aquaculture in the United Kingdom primarily involves rainbow trout (*Oncorhynchus mykiss*) and brown trout (*Salmo trutta*), farmed across Scotland, Yorkshire, and southern England, with Scotland being the only region rearing large trout in seawater pens. While England has more sites, they are typically smaller. Northern Ireland exports rainbow trout eggs to Scotland, as 92% of Scotland's rainbow trout eggs were imported in 2017, including from Northern Ireland. The UK has numerous privately owned trout farms, often family-run, with three companies accounting for 40% of production. Unlike salmon farming, trout farming lacks multinational ownership, with Dawnfresh being the largest integrated producer. Registered sites for trout farming are widespread, though marine rearing is limited outside Scotland due to scarce suitable locations. (122 words)

**Production in bacteria**  
Acetoin (3-hydroxy-2-butanone) is a four-carbon ketone produced by bacteria as a metabolic byproduct and external energy store, particularly in *Bacillus* and *Enterobacteriaceae* species. It is synthesized from α-acetolactate via spontaneous decarboxylation or enzymatically by α-acetolactate decarboxylase (ALDC). During exponential growth, acetoin acts as a pH buffer, preventing intracellular acidification. When preferred carbon sources are depleted, bacteria in stationary phase metabolize acetoin through the acetoin dehydrogenase enzyme complex (AoDH), which converts it into acetyl-CoA via a mechanism analogous to the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex. Alternatively, some bacteria reduce acetoin to 2,3-butanediol using acetoin reductase, regenerating NAD+ for glycolysis.

Since 1998 the "Grodzka Gate" has been implementing the project «Historia Mówiona» (Template:Lang-en). It is based on collecting of audio and video recorded memories of witnesses of various historical events. Pursuant to oral history guidelines, the "Grodzka Gate" collected memories of almost 2,000 people and recordings of more than 3,000 hours of audio and video materials. The main topics of the interviews are mundane life, education, intercultural reciprocity, World War II, The Holocaust, Polish-Jewish relations during the war and opposition and underground publishing during the years of The Polish People's Republic. Gathered materials constitute the largest collection of this kind in Poland, which is available in the Internet. Every file fulfills the requirements of The Dublin Core Metadata Initiative and is stored in the system dLibra.

The reservoir was first begun in 1878, as part of a system of reservoirs fed from the Sudbury River to supplement the Lake Cochituate system in Natick. **Plot**  
The Middle English romance *Sir Perceval of Galles* follows Perceval, raised in the forest by his mother, Acheflour—Arthur’s sister—after his father, also named Perceval, is killed by the Red Knight. Fearing her son would meet the same fate, Acheflour shelters him from knighthood and war. When Perceval later leaves for Arthur’s court, she believes him dead and flees into the woods, driven mad by grief. Eventually, Perceval finds her, restores her sanity, and reunites with her. The story explores familial bonds alongside Perceval’s journey to knighthood.

The analysis of single-particle trajectories employs statistical methods to extract dynamical and mechanical properties. Stochastic models like the Langevin and Smoluchowski equations describe such motion, with the Langevin equation modeling particle dynamics under a random force (e.g., Brownian motion) and deterministic forces (e.g., \(F = -\nabla U\)). These equations combine stochastic and deterministic influences, forming the basis for modeling diffusion and transport phenomena.

During a rugby football game in 2007, Cizikas tackled Manny Castillo in what was described by Cizikas as a "headlock." Castillo died of a head injury two days later; it was noted by physicians that two weeks prior to the battle with Cizikas, Castillo suffered a major concussion. One doctor testified that Castillo's concussion was severe enough that it should have kept him out of competition with physical contact for a full year. The Castillo family supported Cizikas throughout his ordeal and believed the death to have been accidental, asking that Cizikas be spared punishment. Their request could not be met, however, because Peel Regional Police supported Cizikas' conviction, as the tackle led to a death. Castillo's father provided a statement that called for fighting in youth hockey to be abolished, but again did not blame Cizikas for his son's death.

**Exandria** is a Dungeons & Dragons campaign setting originally created by Matthew Mercer in 2012 for his personal games. It gained prominence through the web series *Critical Role*, where Mercer’s adventuring group Vox Machina battled threats like chromatic dragons and undead liches on the continent of Tal’Dorei. Described as a "classic modified plug-n-play" world, Exandria adapts traditional D&D tropes—such as Greyhawk-inspired deities—while avoiding radical genre shifts like those in Eberron or Spelljammer. Green Ronin Publishing released the *Tal’Dorei Campaign Setting* in 2017, co-written by Mercer and James Haeck. The 2022 update, *Tal’Dorei Campaign Setting Reborn* (published by Darrington Press), advanced the timeline 20 years to align with *Critical Role*’s second campaign, *The Mighty Nein*, and expanded the book to 280 pages.
